options.D: Because incremental development removes the need for any baseline requirements

correct_answer_text: Because there is significant technical risk, and incremental delivery allows for the latest technology insertion or potential changes in needs or requirements

question: Section 2.2.2 cites which ISO/IEC/IEEE standard clause for further details on incremental life cycle models, including typical applicable systems and associated risks and opportunities?

options.A: ISO/IEC/IEEE 15288, Clause 6.1
options.B: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24748-2 (2018), Clause 6.4.3.2
options.C: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24748-2 (2018), Clause 6.4.3.1 (which actually covers sequential models)
options.D: ISO/IEC/IEEE 42010, Clause 5.3

correct_answer_text: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24748-2 (2018), Clause 6.4.3.2

question: Per Section 2.2.2, what is the stated rationale for carefully timing iteration cycles within an incremental approach?

options.A: To eliminate any need for stakeholder feedback between cycles
options.B: To minimize rework cost as a project learns experimentally and empirically
options.C: To force all learning to happen after final delivery
options.D: To allow each iteration to skip its decision gate

correct_answer_text: To minimize rework cost as a project learns experimentally and empirically

question: Per Section 2.2.3, what condition primarily makes an evolutionary life cycle approach appropriate compared to sequential or incremental approaches?

options.A: The full set of final system requirements is known and stable at the start of the effort
options.B: Only a single deployment to the end user is planned for the entire program
options.C: The final system requirements are unknown or only partially known, especially for novel systems
options.D: The organization wishes to minimize the number of planned iterations and deployments

correct_answer_text: The final system requirements are unknown or only partially known, especially for novel systems

question: Using Table 2.2 characteristics, how is the evolutionary approach distinguished from the incremental approach?

options.A: Evolutionary uses a partial requirement set at start with typically multiple deployments; incremental uses a full requirement set at start with only potential multiple deployments
options.B: Evolutionary uses a full requirement set at start; incremental uses a partial requirement set at start
options.C: Evolutionary plans a single iteration with no deployment; incremental plans multiple iterations with typical deployments
options.D: Evolutionary and incremental both require a fully defined requirement set at start; they differ only in deployment cadence

correct_answer_text: Evolutionary uses a partial requirement set at start with typically multiple deployments; incremental uses a full requirement set at start with only potential multiple deployments

question: Section 2.2.3 classifies agile software development methods as which of the following?

options.A: A specialized form of the sequential (once-through) life cycle model
options.B: A type of evolutionary development
options.C: An incremental method that requires fully defined requirements at project start
options.D: A non-life-cycle approach that bypasses ISO/IEC/IEEE 15288 processes

correct_answer_text: A type of evolutionary development

question: Per Section 2.2.3, what three key principles typically characterize DevOps?

options.A: Fixed scope, sequential handoffs, and late-stage integration
options.B: Shared ownership, workflow automation, and rapid feedback
options.C: Full requirements baseline, single deployment, and design freeze
options.D: Independent teams, manual approvals, and milestone-driven releases

correct_answer_text: Shared ownership, workflow automation, and rapid feedback

question: According to Section 2.2.3, how does DevSecOps (Figure 2.8) extend DevOps?

options.A: It replaces continuous integration with milestone-based integration
options.B: It centralizes security decisions in a separate compliance organization outside the delivery teams
options.C: It integrates security practices into DevOps, with each delivery team responsible and empowered to pick appropriate security means
options.D: It removes operations responsibility from development teams and assigns it to a dedicated operations team

correct_answer_text: It integrates security practices into DevOps, with each delivery team responsible and empowered to pick appropriate security means

question: Per Section 2.2.3, why is well-functioning configuration management considered essential to the evolutionary approach?

options.A: Because all versions are merged into a single release before any deployment occurs
options.B: Because new capabilities are typically assigned to exactly one version, and multiple versions can be operated and supported simultaneously
options.C: Because evolutionary development does not require any operating manuals, maintenance instructions, or spare parts considerations
options.D: Because the requirements baseline is fully fixed at the start and must be protected from change

correct_answer_text: Because new capabilities are typically assigned to exactly one version, and multiple versions can be operated and supported simultaneously

question: Section 2.2.3 notes that the evolutionary approach offers significant advantages under what specific condition?

options.A: When the development team is intentionally isolated from stakeholders to avoid scope changes
options.B: When it is possible to obtain steady and high-quality feedback from relevant stakeholders
options.C: When the final requirements set is fully known and frozen before the first cycle
options.D: When only a single deployment is planned at the very end of the program

correct_answer_text: When it is possible to obtain steady and high-quality feedback from relevant stakeholders

question: Per Section 2.2.3, what is the role of an MVP (minimal viable product) in early evolutionary cycles?

options.A: To freeze the requirements baseline before any feedback is collected
options.B: To replace configuration management during the early cycles
options.C: To demonstrate basic feasibility and facilitate market entry, while emerging technical innovations can be planned for later versions
options.D: To deliver the final, fully featured system in a single deployment

correct_answer_text: To demonstrate basic feasibility and facilitate market entry, while emerging technical innovations can be planned for later versions

question: Section 2.2.3 recommends what consideration when developing subsequent evolutionary versions so that long-term evolution of the system is enabled?

options.A: Only adaptability needs to be considered; modularity and flexibility are explicitly discouraged
options.B: Adaptability, flexibility, and modularity should be carefully considered, with decisions made in the context of life cycle cost
options.C: Previous versions must always be completely replaced and never upgraded in place
options.D: Life cycle cost is irrelevant once the first MVP has been deployed

correct_answer_text: Adaptability, flexibility, and modularity should be carefully considered, with decisions made in the context of life cycle cost

question: Figure 2.9 (Rockwell Collins military radios) illustrates a mixed approach where teams working on electronic-board hardware, firmware, and software have different timings for hardware increments and firmware/software epics. What does Figure 2.9 demonstrate about this mixed-discipline engineering?

options.A: All disciplines must be locked to a single synchronous cadence to avoid integration issues
options.B: Iterations and increments are asynchronous across the disciplines, with integrated work-in-process testing performed using the latest increments and versions from each discipline
options.C: Hardware development uses a sequential model while software development uses no life cycle model at all
options.D: Only software development is performed evolutionarily; hardware and firmware are deferred until software is fully complete

correct_answer_text: Iterations and increments are asynchronous across the disciplines, with integrated work-in-process testing performed using the latest increments and versions from each discipline

question: According to Section 2.2.3, which standard provides further details on evolutionary life cycle models, including typical applicable systems as well as associated risks and opportunities?

options.A: ISO/IEC/IEEE 15288 (2023), Clause 6.4.3.4
options.B: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24748-2 (2018), Clause 6.4.3.4
options.C: ISO/IEC 33060 (2020), Clause 6
options.D: INCOSE SECF (2018), Clause 6.4

correct_answer_text: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24748-2 (2018), Clause 6.4.3.4

question: Per Section 2.2.3, what is the stated goal of DevOps as an example of an evolutionary approach?

options.A: To freeze the design baseline so that no further changes are made after the first release
options.B: To provide continuous integration of the system and continuous delivery of capabilities
options.C: To enforce sequential, single-deployment delivery aligned to fixed milestones
options.D: To replace systems engineering processes with purely operational procedures

correct_answer_text: To provide continuous integration of the system and continuous delivery of capabilities

question: In the DevOps principle of 'shared ownership' as described in Section 2.2.3, what is the central idea?

options.A: Ownership of the system is transferred from the development team to a separate operations organization upon release
options.B: Development and operations responsibilities are jointly held by the delivery team so that integration and delivery of capabilities are continuous
options.C: Customers, not engineers, own the configuration baseline once a version has been deployed
options.D: Ownership of source artifacts is divided strictly along disciplinary boundaries to prevent overlap

correct_answer_text: Development and operations responsibilities are jointly held by the delivery team so that integration and delivery of capabilities are continuous

question: Why is 'workflow automation' identified in Section 2.2.3 as a key DevOps principle supporting evolutionary delivery?

options.A: It eliminates the need for any configuration management because tools handle versioning implicitly
options.B: It enables continuous integration of the system and continuous delivery of capabilities at the pace required by evolutionary cycles
options.C: It removes the need for stakeholder feedback by replacing it with automated test results
options.D: It locks the requirements baseline so that no manual rework is ever required between cycles

correct_answer_text: It enables continuous integration of the system and continuous delivery of capabilities at the pace required by evolutionary cycles

question: Per Section 2.2.3, the 'rapid feedback' DevOps principle is closely tied to which broader evolutionary precondition?

options.A: The ability to obtain steady, high-quality feedback from relevant stakeholders so that subsequent cycles incorporate prior experience
options.B: The expectation that the requirements set is fully fixed before development begins
options.C: The use of a single, end-of-program deployment to maximize learning from one release
options.D: The avoidance of any deployable versions until all cycles have been completed

correct_answer_text: The ability to obtain steady, high-quality feedback from relevant stakeholders so that subsequent cycles incorporate prior experience

question: In DevSecOps as described in Section 2.2.3, why is security integrated into the DevOps flow rather than handled by a separate gate?

options.A: Because security is treated as a discretionary concern that can be delayed until late in the life cycle
options.B: Because each delivery team is made responsible and empowered to pick appropriate security means as part of continuous integration and delivery
options.C: Because evolutionary methods prohibit any organization-level security policies
options.D: Because security only applies during disposal, not during development or operations

correct_answer_text: Because each delivery team is made responsible and empowered to pick appropriate security means as part of continuous integration and delivery

question: Per Section 2.2.3, which feedback-loop distinction best separates evolutionary from incremental approaches?

options.A: Evolutionary cycles refine partially known requirements using experience and stakeholder feedback from earlier cycles; incremental approaches assume a mostly known requirements set at the start
options.B: Incremental cycles refine partially known requirements using feedback; evolutionary cycles assume requirements are fully known at the start
options.C: Both approaches assume fully known requirements at the start and differ only in deployment frequency
options.D: Evolutionary approaches forbid any deployable versions until requirements are finalized

correct_answer_text: Evolutionary cycles refine partially known requirements using experience and stakeholder feedback from earlier cycles; incremental approaches assume a mostly known requirements set at the start

question: According to Section 2.2.3, how should the goal of each evolutionary cycle typically be treated at the beginning of the cycle?

options.A: Each cycle's goal is fully fixed and identical to the goals of all other cycles
options.B: Each cycle's goal may be more or less unknown at the start, making it particularly important to carry forward experience gained in earlier cycles
options.C: Each cycle's goal is independent and must not be informed by prior cycles, to avoid bias
options.D: Each cycle's goal is determined solely by the configuration management tool, not by the team

correct_answer_text: Each cycle's goal may be more or less unknown at the start, making it particularly important to carry forward experience gained in earlier cycles

question: Section 2.2.3 cites which historical NASA example as having been developed using an evolutionary approach?

options.A: The Apollo Lunar Module guidance computer
options.B: The Space Shuttle high-temperature tiles (Forsberg, 1995)
options.C: The Voyager probe communications subsystem
options.D: The International Space Station habitation module

correct_answer_text: The Space Shuttle high-temperature tiles (Forsberg, 1995)

question: Per Section 2.2.3, which two project types are explicitly identified as frequent users of an evolutionary approach?

options.A: Fixed-price construction projects and single-deployment infrastructure projects
options.B: Research and development (R&D) projects and system-of-systems (SoS) developments
options.C: High-volume manufacturing and certified avionics-only projects
options.D: Disposal projects and legacy-system retirements

correct_answer_text: Research and development (R&D) projects and system-of-systems (SoS) developments

question: Per Section 2.2.3, what happens to a project's schedule and cost targets if the assignment of new capabilities to specific versions is lost or becomes unclear?

options.A: Schedule and cost targets are unaffected because evolutionary methods are inherently self-correcting
options.B: Confusion results and schedule and cost targets are negatively impacted
options.C: The cycle automatically becomes a sequential model, preserving the original targets
options.D: Configuration management becomes unnecessary, simplifying delivery

correct_answer_text: Confusion results and schedule and cost targets are negatively impacted

question: When planning subsequent evolutionary versions, Section 2.2.3 says teams should examine what specific question?

options.A: Whether to abandon stakeholder feedback once a baseline is set
options.B: Whether previous versions should be completely replaced by newer ones, or whether subsequent versions can support partial or complete upgrade of previous versions
options.C: Whether to freeze the requirements baseline permanently after the first version
options.D: Whether to merge all versions into a single final deployment before any field use

correct_answer_text: Whether previous versions should be completely replaced by newer ones, or whether subsequent versions can support partial or complete upgrade of previous versions

question: Per Section 2.2.3, which life cycle support items must be considered alongside configuration control when multiple versions of an evolutionary system are operated and supported simultaneously?

options.A: Only the as-designed drawings of the latest version
options.B: Operating manuals, maintenance instructions, spare parts, and disposal instructions
options.C: Only the marketing materials for the next planned release
options.D: Only the certification artifacts for the original baseline version

correct_answer_text: Operating manuals, maintenance instructions, spare parts, and disposal instructions

question: Per Section 2.2.3, in which section of the handbook should decisions about adaptability, flexibility, and modularity be evaluated for life cycle cost implications?

options.A: Section 2.2.1 (Sequential Methods)
options.B: Section 2.3.4.5 (Configuration Management)
options.C: Section 3.1.2 (life cycle cost)
options.D: Section 4.1 (tailoring of life cycle models)

correct_answer_text: Section 3.1.2 (life cycle cost)

question: According to Section 2.2.3, what enables evolutionary approaches to provide the 'adaptability and flexibility' needed when final system requirements are unknown or only partially known?

options.A: Locking the requirements baseline at project start
options.B: Planning cycles on a regular periodic basis, each producing a deployable version, with requirements increasingly refined cycle by cycle
options.C: Eliminating all stakeholder involvement until the final version
options.D: Deferring all deployments to the end of the program

correct_answer_text: Planning cycles on a regular periodic basis, each producing a deployable version, with requirements increasingly refined cycle by cycle

question: Per Section 2.2.3, similar to the incremental approach, evolutionary versions may be developed how?

options.A: Only sequentially, never in parallel
options.B: Only in parallel, never sequentially
options.C: Sequentially or in parallel
options.D: Only after the final version has been fully specified

correct_answer_text: Sequentially or in parallel

question: Per Section 2.2.3, all life cycle approaches (sequential, incremental, evolutionary, and mixed) are supported by which process framework, and the chosen model should have which property?

options.A: ISO/IEC 33060 (2020); the model must be unchangeable once selected
options.B: ISO/IEC/IEEE 15288 (2023) processes; the chosen life cycle model should be sufficiently adaptable and flexible
options.C: INCOSE SECF (2018); the chosen model must mandate a single deployment
options.D: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24748-2 (2018) alone; the model must forbid tailoring

correct_answer_text: ISO/IEC/IEEE 15288 (2023) processes; the chosen life cycle model should be sufficiently adaptable and flexible

question: Per Section 2.3.1, how is a 'process' defined in the context of system life cycle processes?

options.A: A single decision gate that authorizes entry into a life cycle stage
options.B: A series of activities and tasks performed to achieve one or more outcomes for a stated purpose
options.C: A documented set of inputs and outputs exchanged between organizations
options.D: An ordered sequence of life cycle stages bounded by entry and exit criteria

correct_answer_text: A series of activities and tasks performed to achieve one or more outcomes for a stated purpose

question: How does Section 2.3.1 say system life cycle processes are intended to be applied across the life cycle stages?

options.A: Sequentially, one process at a time, at a single level of the hierarchy
options.B: Concurrently, iteratively, and recursively with other enablers
options.C: Only when a stage exit decision gate fails to be met
options.D: Exclusively at the SoI level, never recursively to system elements

correct_answer_text: Concurrently, iteratively, and recursively with other enablers

question: Which standard does the handbook identify as providing the common process framework that organizes the system life cycle processes into four process groups?

options.A: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24748-1 (2018)
options.C: ISO/IEC/IEEE 15288 (2023)

correct_answer_text: ISO/IEC/IEEE 15288 (2023)

question: ISO/IEC/IEEE 15288 (2023) organizes the system life cycle processes into how many process groups?

options.A: Three
options.B: Four
options.C: Five
options.D: Six

correct_answer_text: Four

question: Which set lists the four process groups defined by ISO/IEC/IEEE 15288 (2023) exactly as the handbook names them?

options.A: Agreement; Organizational Project-Enabling; Technical Management; Technical
options.B: Agreement; Enterprise; Project; Engineering
options.C: Acquisition; Supply; Management; Engineering
options.D: Business; Organizational; Project; Technical

correct_answer_text: Agreement; Organizational Project-Enabling; Technical Management; Technical

question: According to Figure 2.10 and Section 2.3.1, the Agreement Processes group consists of which two processes?

options.A: Acquisition and Supply
options.B: Acquisition and Portfolio Management
options.C: Project Planning and Project Assessment and Control
options.D: Supply and Configuration Management

question: Which process is part of the Organizational Project-Enabling Processes group?

options.A: Risk Management
options.B: Portfolio Management
options.C: System Analysis
options.D: Acquisition

correct_answer_text: Portfolio Management

question: Which of the following processes belongs to the Technical Management Processes group?

options.A: Knowledge Management
options.B: Quality Management
options.C: Quality Assurance
options.D: Validation

question: Which process is listed under the Technical Processes group rather than the Technical Management Processes group?

options.A: Measurement
options.B: Decision Management
options.C: Configuration Management
options.D: Business or Mission Analysis

correct_answer_text: Business or Mission Analysis

question: Per Section 2.3.1, which process is included in the Technical Processes group?

options.A: Human Resource Management
options.B: Information Management
options.C: Disposal
options.D: Life Cycle Model Management

correct_answer_text: Disposal

question: Per Figure 2.10, how many total system life cycle processes does ISO/IEC/IEEE 15288 (2023) define across the four process groups?
Which sub-section of the Process overview is NOT part of the common section structure used to describe each system life cycle process in the handbook?

options.A: Purpose
options.B: Description
options.C: Maturity rating
options.D: Inputs/outputs

correct_answer_text: Maturity rating

question: To ensure consistency with ISO/IEC/IEEE 15288, what does the handbook reproduce verbatim from the standard for each process described?

options.A: The complete list of inputs and outputs
options.B: The purpose statements
options.C: The IPO diagrams
options.D: The list of controls and enablers

correct_answer_text: The purpose statements

question: Per Section 2.3.1.1, how do the process activity descriptions in the handbook treat 'how' versus 'what'?

options.A: They describe both 'what' should be done and 'how' to do it in equal detail
options.B: They describe 'how' to do the activity but not 'what' should be done
options.C: They describe 'what' should be done, not 'how' to do it
options.D: They neither describe 'what' nor 'how', leaving both to Part III

correct_answer_text: They describe 'what' should be done, not 'how' to do it

question: What does the sample IPO diagram (Figure 2.11) show for each system life cycle process?

options.A: Only the inputs and outputs, with activities described elsewhere
options.B: Typical inputs, process activities, and typical outputs
options.C: The mandatory ordering of process activities and their decision gates
options.D: A maturity model showing how the process evolves over the life cycle

correct_answer_text: Typical inputs, process activities, and typical outputs

question: Per Section 2.3.1.1, how should the IPO diagrams be interpreted relative to the way SE processes are performed?

options.A: They represent the only correct way the SE processes must be performed
options.B: They represent 'a' way the SE processes can be performed, but not necessarily 'the' way they must be performed
options.C: They are normative requirements from ISO/IEC/IEEE 15288 that cannot be tailored
options.D: They define the controls and enablers that govern each process individually

correct_answer_text: They represent 'a' way the SE processes can be performed, but not necessarily 'the' way they must be performed

question: Why are the controls and enablers shown in Figure 2.11 (the sample IPO) not repeated on subsequent IPO diagrams in the handbook?

options.A: They are unique to each individual process and listed elsewhere
options.B: They are optional and apply only when projects choose to use them
options.C: They govern all processes described in the handbook and therefore are shown only once
options.D: They have been superseded by ISO/IEC 33060 (2020) and removed for that reason

correct_answer_text: They govern all processes described in the handbook and therefore are shown only once

question: Per Section 2.3.1.1, where can a complete list of all inputs and outputs (and of all controls and enablers) with their respective descriptions be found?

options.A: Appendix D
options.B: Appendix E
options.C: Part III
options.D: Section 4.1 (Tailoring)

correct_answer_text: Appendix E

question: Per Section 2.3.1.1, what is the relationship between the system life cycle process 'outputs' and documents?

options.A: Outputs must always be produced as formal documents
options.B: Outputs are 'results' that may be captured in documents, artifacts, or models, rather than producing documents simply because they are identified as outputs
options.C: Outputs are limited to models, since documents have been deprecated in 15288
options.D: Outputs are produced only when controls and enablers explicitly require a document

correct_answer_text: Outputs are 'results' that may be captured in documents, artifacts, or models, rather than producing documents simply because they are identified as outputs

question: Per Section 2.3.1.1, what reference is used to keep typical inputs and outputs consistent across the handbook when possible?

options.A: ISO/IEC/IEEE 24748-1 (2018)
options.D: INCOSE Systems Engineering Competency Framework (2018)

question: How many processes does ISO/IEC/IEEE 15288 (2023), as listed in Section 2.3.1, include in the Organizational Project-Enabling Processes group?

options.A: Two
options.B: Six
options.C: Eight
options.D: Fourteen

correct_answer_text: Six

question: How many processes are in the Technical Management Processes group per Section 2.3.1?

options.A: Six
options.B: Seven
options.C: Eight
options.D: Fourteen

correct_answer_text: Eight

question: How many processes are in the Technical Processes group per Section 2.3.1?

options.A: Eight
options.B: Ten
options.C: Twelve
options.D: Fourteen

correct_answer_text: Fourteen

question: Per Section 2.3.1, which of the following processes is in the Organizational Project-Enabling Processes group rather than the Technical Management Processes group?

options.A: Risk Management
options.B: Information Management
options.C: Knowledge Management
options.D: Configuration Management

question: Which process appears in the Technical Management Processes group and is commonly confused with the similarly named Organizational Project-Enabling process?

options.A: Quality Assurance (vs Quality Management)
options.B: Risk Management (vs Knowledge Management)
options.C: Project Planning (vs Portfolio Management)
options.D: Decision Management (vs Configuration Management)

correct_answer_text: Quality Assurance (vs Quality Management)

question: Per Section 2.3.1, which of the following is a Technical Process rather than a Technical Management Process?

options.A: Measurement
options.B: Project Assessment and Control
options.C: System Analysis
options.D: Decision Management

correct_answer_text: System Analysis

question: Per Section 2.3.1.1, which ordered list correctly states the common section structure used to describe each system life cycle process?

options.A: Purpose; Description; Inputs/outputs; Process activities; Common approaches and tips; Process elaboration
options.B: Description; Purpose; Process activities; Inputs/outputs; Process elaboration; Common approaches and tips
options.C: Purpose; Inputs/outputs; Description; Process activities; Process elaboration; Common approaches and tips
options.D: Process activities; Purpose; Description; Inputs/outputs; Common approaches and tips; Process elaboration

correct_answer_text: Purpose; Description; Inputs/outputs; Process activities; Common approaches and tips; Process elaboration

question: Per Section 2.3.1.1, what is the purpose of the 'Process elaboration' sub-section in each life cycle process write-up?

options.A: It lists the controls and enablers unique to that one process
options.B: It provides additional details on topics unique to that specific life cycle process
options.C: It maps the process to the corresponding ISO/IEC 33060 (2020) clause numbers
options.D: It contains the verbatim purpose statement from ISO/IEC/IEEE 15288

correct_answer_text: It provides additional details on topics unique to that specific life cycle process

question: Per Section 2.3.1.1, where does the handbook direct readers for topics that cross-cut multiple life cycle processes?

options.A: Appendix D
options.B: Appendix E
options.C: Part III
options.D: Section 4.1 (Tailoring)

correct_answer_text: Part III

question: Per Section 2.3.1.1 and Figure 2.11, in the IPO model the enablers function in what way relative to the inputs and outputs?

options.A: Enablers replace controls when no project direction is available
options.B: Enablers are downstream outputs that feed the next process as inputs
options.C: Enablers work together with the inputs to be transformed by the process into the outputs under the direction of the controls
options.D: Enablers and controls are interchangeable labels for the same set of project artifacts

correct_answer_text: Enablers work together with the inputs to be transformed by the process into the outputs under the direction of the controls

question: Per Section 2.3.1, what does ISO/IEC/IEEE 15288 (2023) state that the four process groups provide?

options.A: A maturity model for assessing SE process capability across organizations
options.B: A common process framework for describing the life cycle of engineered systems, adopting a Systems Engineering approach
options.C: A normative ordering in which processes must be executed during each life cycle stage
options.D: A tailoring guide that prescribes which processes may be omitted for small projects

correct_answer_text: A common process framework for describing the life cycle of engineered systems, adopting a Systems Engineering approach

question: Per Section 2.3.1, where in the handbook are acronyms for the system life cycle process names provided?

options.A: Appendix C
options.B: Appendix D
options.C: Appendix E
options.D: Inside the IPO diagram on Figure 2.10

correct_answer_text: Appendix D

question: Per Section 2.3.1, what supports the application of the system life cycle processes by SE practitioners and is defined in the INCOSE Systems Engineering Competency Framework (SECF) (2018)?

options.A: The relevant competencies
options.B: The controls and enablers
options.C: The four process groups
options.D: The verbatim purpose statements

correct_answer_text: The relevant competencies

question: Per Section 2.3.1.1, how should readers approach the IPO diagrams relative to the surrounding text and figures?

options.A: Rely on the IPO diagrams as the authoritative description of each process
options.B: Use the IPO diagrams in place of the verbatim purpose statements
options.C: Study the complete information provided in the combination of figures and text, not relying solely on the IPO diagrams
options.D: Skip the IPO diagrams unless tailoring per Section 4.1 is being performed

correct_answer_text: Study the complete information provided in the combination of figures and text, not relying solely on the IPO diagrams

question: From Figure 2.10, drag each system life cycle process to its ISO/IEC/IEEE 15288 process group.

items.text: Stakeholder Needs and Requirements Definition Process

question: Per the SE Handbook, what is the primary reason that the system life cycle processes should be applied in a concurrent, iterative, and recursive manner rather than as a sequential, linear application at a single level of the system hierarchy?

options.A: It is mandated by ISO/IEC/IEEE 15288 for all defense acquisition programs
options.B: It minimizes the number of decision gates that must be executed during the life cycle
options.C: It facilitates the exchange of valuable information and insight between processes so that ongoing learning and decisions are accounted for as the technical solution evolves
options.D: It eliminates the need for horizontal and vertical integration between hierarchy levels

correct_answer_text: It facilitates the exchange of valuable information and insight between processes so that ongoing learning and decisions are accounted for as the technical solution evolves

question: How does the SE Handbook formally define CONCURRENCY in the context of the system life cycle processes?

options.A: The repeated application of and interaction between two or more processes at a given level of the system hierarchy
options.B: The parallel application of two or more processes at a given level in the system hierarchy
options.C: The repeated application of the set of life cycle processes, tailored as appropriate, at successive levels in the system hierarchy
options.D: The simultaneous execution of multiple decision gates across different life cycle stages

correct_answer_text: The parallel application of two or more processes at a given level in the system hierarchy

question: How does the SE Handbook formally define ITERATION in the context of the system life cycle processes?

options.A: The parallel application of two or more processes at a given level in the system hierarchy
options.B: The repeated application of the set of life cycle processes at successive levels in the system hierarchy
options.C: The repeated application of and interaction between two or more processes at a given level in the system hierarchy
options.D: The reapplication of a single process whose outputs at one level become inputs for the next-lower level

correct_answer_text: The repeated application of and interaction between two or more processes at a given level in the system hierarchy

question: How does the SE Handbook formally define RECURSION in the context of the system life cycle processes?

options.A: The repeated application of and interaction between two or more processes at a given level of the system hierarchy
options.B: The parallel application of two or more processes at a given level in the system hierarchy
options.C: The application of feedback loops within a single process at the same level of the hierarchy
options.D: The repeated application of the set of life cycle processes, tailored as appropriate, at successive levels in the system hierarchy

correct_answer_text: The repeated application of the set of life cycle processes, tailored as appropriate, at successive levels in the system hierarchy

question: Which statement BEST captures the critical distinction between iteration and recursion as used in INCOSE SE practice?

options.A: Iteration is the repeated application of processes at the same level of the system hierarchy, while recursion is the application of the set of processes at successive (different) levels of the system hierarchy
options.B: Iteration occurs only within a single process, while recursion occurs only between two or more processes
options.C: Iteration is performed during system definition (top-down), while recursion is performed only during system realization (bottom-up)
options.D: Iteration applies to Technical Processes only, while recursion applies to Technical Management Processes only

correct_answer_text: Iteration is the repeated application of processes at the same level of the system hierarchy, while recursion is the application of the set of processes at successive (different) levels of the system hierarchy

question: In Figure 2.12 of the SE Handbook, which symbology is used to indicate RECURSION?

options.A: Parallel lines
options.B: Circular arrows
options.C: Down and up arrows
options.D: Diagonal dashed lines

correct_answer_text: Down and up arrows

question: In Figure 2.12 of the SE Handbook, which symbology is used to indicate ITERATION?

options.A: Parallel lines
options.B: Down and up arrows
options.C: Circular arrows
options.D: A branching tree structure

correct_answer_text: Circular arrows

question: According to the SE Handbook, which pair of processes is given as a typical example of two processes that are usually performed in a continual and concurrent manner, providing information to one another?

options.A: Risk Management and Measurement
options.B: System Requirements Definition and System Architecture Definition
options.C: Acquisition and Supply
options.D: Verification and Validation

correct_answer_text: Risk Management and Measurement

question: Per the SE Handbook, which pair of processes is cited as a typical example where ITERATION occurs at a given level of the system hierarchy?

options.A: Acquisition and Supply
options.B: Risk Management and Measurement
options.C: Configuration Management and Information Management
options.D: System Requirements Definition and System Architecture Definition

correct_answer_text: System Requirements Definition and System Architecture Definition

question: The SE Handbook states that the Technical Management and Technical Processes are expected to be recursively applied at each successive level of the system hierarchy. At what point does this recursive application STOP?

options.A: When the verification and validation processes have been successfully completed at the top level
options.B: When the level is reached at which the decision is made to make, buy, or reuse a system element
options.C: When the project exhausts its allocated budget for system definition activities
options.D: When the System Architecture Definition process produces its first baseline

correct_answer_text: When the level is reached at which the decision is made to make, buy, or reuse a system element

question: Per the SE Handbook, during the recursive application of the life cycle processes, the outputs at one level become inputs for the next successive level. In which direction does this flow occur for system definition versus system realization?

options.A: Below for system definition; above for system realization
options.B: Above for system definition; below for system realization
options.C: Below for both system definition and system realization
options.D: Above for both system definition and system realization

correct_answer_text: Below for system definition; above for system realization

question: The SE Handbook contrasts horizontal and vertical integration in the concurrent, iterative, and recursive environment. Which statement correctly maps each term to its purpose?

options.A: Horizontal integration ensures consistency between levels; vertical integration ensures completeness before diving deeper
options.B: Horizontal integration ensures completeness before diving deeper; vertical integration ensures consistency between levels
options.C: Horizontal integration applies only during system definition; vertical integration applies only during system realization
options.D: Horizontal integration synchronizes decision gates; vertical integration synchronizes IPO diagrams

correct_answer_text: Horizontal integration ensures completeness before diving deeper; vertical integration ensures consistency between levels

question: According to the SE Handbook, the system architecture should enable concurrency through which set of design methods?

options.A: Sequential decomposition, waterfall partitioning, and rigid coupling
options.B: Modularization, encapsulation, and commonality/reuse
options.C: Centralized control, monolithic design, and tight integration
options.D: Single-level hierarchy, point-to-point interfaces, and bespoke components

correct_answer_text: Modularization, encapsulation, and commonality/reuse

question: Per the SE Handbook, iteration between two or more processes at a given level of the hierarchy is needed for several reasons. Which of the following is NOT identified by the handbook as a reason for iteration?

options.A: To accommodate stakeholder decisions and evolving understanding
options.B: To account for architectural decisions or constraints
options.C: To resolve trade-offs for affordability, adaptability, feasibility, and resilience
options.D: To eliminate the need to apply the System Analysis and Decision Management processes

correct_answer_text: To eliminate the need to apply the System Analysis and Decision Management processes

question: In Figure 2.12 of the SE Handbook, which symbology is used to indicate CONCURRENCY?

options.A: Parallel lines
options.B: Circular arrows
options.C: Down and up arrows
options.D: A nested matrix

correct_answer_text: Parallel lines

question: A project team is performing system definition at the SoI level. The System Requirements Definition process and the System Architecture Definition process repeatedly exchange information so that the evolving requirements help shape the architecture and architectural feasibility constraints drive requirement changes. Per the SE Handbook, this is BEST classified as:

options.A: Recursion, because two processes are repeatedly applied
options.B: Concurrency only, because the two processes run in parallel
options.C: Iteration, because two processes interact repeatedly at the same level of the system hierarchy
options.D: Vertical integration, because consistency between processes is being ensured

correct_answer_text: Iteration, because two processes interact repeatedly at the same level of the system hierarchy

question: After the SoI architecture is baselined, the team applies the Technical Management and Technical Processes again at the next-lower (subsystem) level, then at the assembly level, treating the outputs of the higher level as inputs to the lower level. Per the SE Handbook, this is BEST classified as:

options.A: Iteration, because the processes are being repeated
options.B: Recursion, because the set of life cycle processes is repeatedly applied at successive levels of the system hierarchy
options.C: Concurrency, because the upper and lower levels are running in parallel
options.D: Horizontal integration, because completeness is being ensured before diving deeper

correct_answer_text: Recursion, because the set of life cycle processes is repeatedly applied at successive levels of the system hierarchy

question: Per the SE Handbook, in what circumstance is it NOT necessary for system life cycle processes to be performed serially, making them strong candidates for concurrent application?

options.A: When the project has been authorized to skip decision gates
options.B: When one process is not dependent on another for information or results
options.C: When the processes belong to different process groups (Technical vs Agreement)
options.D: When the System Architecture Definition process has not yet been started

correct_answer_text: When one process is not dependent on another for information or results

question: The SE Handbook describes an example in which detailed electrical modeling reveals that a particular system element's load exceeds its allocated power budget, forcing a design change or reallocation of network power assignments. This is presented as an example of:

options.A: Recursion of the Design Definition process across hierarchy levels
options.B: Iteration between System Requirements Definition and System Architecture Definition at a given level
options.C: Concurrency between Risk Management and Measurement
options.D: Horizontal integration between two adjacent SoIs

correct_answer_text: Iteration between System Requirements Definition and System Architecture Definition at a given level

question: According to the SE Handbook, when iteration between requirements and architecture surfaces trade-offs or infeasible requirements, which two additional processes can be invoked to help resolve them?

options.A: Acquisition process and Supply process
options.B: Configuration Management process and Information Management process
options.C: System Analysis process and Decision Management process
options.D: Verification process and Validation process

correct_answer_text: System Analysis process and Decision Management process

question: Per the SE Handbook, which set of process groups is expected to be recursively applied at each successive level of the system hierarchy?

options.A: Agreement and Organizational Project-Enabling Processes only
options.B: Technical Management and Technical Processes
options.C: Technical Processes only
options.D: Decision Management and Risk Management only

correct_answer_text: Technical Management and Technical Processes

question: Per the SE Handbook, during the recursive application of life cycle processes for SYSTEM DEFINITION, the outputs of one level become inputs to which next successive level?

options.A: The level above (toward the SoI)
options.B: The level below (toward the lowest system element)
options.C: The same level, after iteration completes
options.D: The enterprise level, where they are integrated horizontally

correct_answer_text: The level below (toward the lowest system element)

question: Per the SE Handbook, during the recursive application of life cycle processes for SYSTEM REALIZATION, the outputs of one level become inputs to which next successive level?

options.A: The level below (toward the lowest system element)
options.B: The same level, after concurrency completes
options.C: The level above (toward the SoI)
options.D: Any level, as determined by the Configuration Management process

correct_answer_text: The level above (toward the SoI)

question: The SE Handbook ties the stopping condition for recursive application of life cycle processes to a specific decision identified in Section 1.3.5. Which decision is this?

options.A: The decision to baseline the System Architecture
options.B: The decision to enter the Production stage
options.C: The decision to make, buy, or reuse a system element
options.D: The decision to award an acquisition contract

correct_answer_text: The decision to make, buy, or reuse a system element

question: Per the SE Handbook, the system architecture is expected to ENABLE concurrency. Which of the following design methods is NOT listed by the handbook as an architectural enabler of concurrency?

options.A: Modularization
options.B: Encapsulation
options.C: Commonality/reuse
options.D: Sequential serialization of interfaces

correct_answer_text: Sequential serialization of interfaces

question: A team has completed an initial pass at functions, performance, and requirements at the SoI level, but before decomposing to the next-lower level they verify that all functions, inputs/outputs, performance, and conditions of operation are fully and consistently defined at the SoI level. Per the SE Handbook, what is this activity called?

options.A: Vertical integration
options.B: Horizontal integration
options.C: Recursive integration
options.D: Concurrent integration

correct_answer_text: Horizontal integration

question: A team postulates a next-lower-level architecture and goes down multiple levels to validate that lower-level functions and elements will be suitable solutions for the SoI and its stakeholders, ensuring consistency between the levels. Per the SE Handbook, what is this called?

options.A: Horizontal integration
options.B: Concurrency
options.C: Iteration
options.D: Vertical integration

correct_answer_text: Vertical integration

question: Per the SE Handbook, application of concurrency, iteration, and recursion to the life cycle processes facilitates which specific outcome?

options.A: Incorporation of learning from further analysis and process application as the technical solution evolves
options.B: Elimination of the need for stakeholder requirements baselines
options.C: Reduction of the number of life cycle stages required by ISO/IEC/IEEE 15288
options.D: Replacement of the IPO diagram with a single integrated process model

correct_answer_text: Incorporation of learning from further analysis and process application as the technical solution evolves

question: From Figure 2.12, drag each definition, symbol, or scenario to the SE concept it represents.

items.text: Indicated in Figure 2.12 by parallel lines symbol
Parallel application of two or more processes at a given level in the system hierarchy
Risk Management process and Measurement process performed continually and simultaneously while exchanging information (Figure 2.34)
Enabled by system architecture features such as modularization, encapsulation, and commonality/reuse
Indicated in Figure 2.12 by circular arrows symbol
Repeated application of and interaction between two or more processes at a given level in the system hierarchy
System Requirements Definition and System Architecture Definition cycle back and forth as evolving requirements shape the architecture and trade-offs reshape requirements
Needed to accommodate stakeholder decisions, evolving understanding, and trade-offs for affordability, adaptability, feasibility, and resilience
Indicated in Figure 2.12 by down and up arrows symbol
Repeated application of the set of life cycle processes, tailored as appropriate, at successive levels in the system hierarchy
Technical Management and Technical Processes applied at the SoI level, then again at the subsystem level, then again at the component level until a make/buy/reuse decision is reached
Outputs at one hierarchy level become inputs to the next successive level (below for system definition, above for system realization)

question: According to ISO/IEC/IEEE 15288 as cited in the handbook, the Agreement Processes define the activities necessary to accomplish what?

options.A: Establish requirements baselines between engineering disciplines
options.B: Establish an agreement between two organizations
options.C: Negotiate budget allocations between project portfolios
options.D: Coordinate verification and validation between system elements

correct_answer_text: Establish an agreement between two organizations

question: Which two processes does ISO/IEC/IEEE 15288 identify as the Agreement Processes covered in the handbook?

options.A: Acquisition process and Supply process
options.B: Acquisition process and Project Planning process
options.C: Supply process and Quality Assurance process
options.D: Acquisition process and Business or Mission Analysis process

correct_answer_text: Acquisition process and Supply process

question: The handbook lists several conditions under which Agreement Processes are utilized. Which of the following is NOT one of those conditions?

options.A: An organization cannot satisfy a defined need itself
options.B: A supplier can satisfy a defined need in a more economical or timely manner
options.C: A higher authority has directed the use of a specific supplier
options.D: An organization wants to reduce its internal headcount through outsourcing

correct_answer_text: An organization wants to reduce its internal headcount through outsourcing

question: What does the handbook identify as an overall objective of the Agreement Processes?

options.A: Maximize profit margin for the supplier organization
options.B: Identify the interfaces between the acquirer and supplier(s) and establish the terms and conditions of these relationships, including required inputs and outputs
options.C: Define the system architecture and allocate requirements to system elements
options.D: Establish the organizational strategic plan and project portfolio

correct_answer_text: Identify the interfaces between the acquirer and supplier(s) and establish the terms and conditions of these relationships, including required inputs and outputs

question: What is the role of the SE practitioner during Agreement Process negotiations, according to the handbook?

options.A: Lead role; the SE practitioner negotiates and signs the contract on behalf of the organization
options.B: Supporting role to the project management practitioner, providing impact assessments for changes, trade studies, risk assessments, and other technical input
options.C: Observer role only; SE practitioners are not relevant contributors to Agreement Processes
options.D: Independent auditor role, reporting only to the acquirer's quality organization

correct_answer_text: Supporting role to the project management practitioner, providing impact assessments for changes, trade studies, risk assessments, and other technical input

question: Why does the handbook describe acceptance criteria as critical elements of an agreement?

options.A: They drive the supplier's profit margin and are required by accounting standards
options.B: They protect the acquirer from being coerced into accepting a product with poor quality and the supplier from the unpredictable actions of an indecisive acquirer
options.C: They define the system architecture that the supplier must implement
options.D: They specify the verification methods used during system integration

correct_answer_text: They protect the acquirer from being coerced into accepting a product with poor quality and the supplier from the unpredictable actions of an indecisive acquirer

question: When are acceptance criteria established for an agreement?

options.A: After the system has been delivered, during the acceptance review
options.B: During the Agreement Process negotiations
options.C: During the Verification process at the end of system development
options.D: Only after the first change request is submitted

correct_answer_text: During the Agreement Process negotiations

question: The handbook notes that for a given system, an organization can be:

options.A: Either an acquirer or a supplier, but never both
options.B: Both a supplier (to its end customer) and an acquirer (to organizations providing it system elements, materials, or services)
options.C: An acquirer only when it is the prime contractor
options.D: A supplier only when it shares a common parent organization with the acquirer

correct_answer_text: Both a supplier (to its end customer) and an acquirer (to organizations providing it system elements, materials, or services)

question: Per ISO/IEC/IEEE 15288 as quoted in the handbook, what is the purpose of the Acquisition process?

options.A: To deliver a product or service that satisfies an agreed-upon agreement
options.B: To obtain a product or service in accordance with the acquirer's requirements
options.C: To negotiate the cost and schedule of a supplier contract
options.D: To establish organizational strategic objectives for procurement

correct_answer_text: To obtain a product or service in accordance with the acquirer's requirements

question: The handbook explicitly states the Acquisition process section is written from whose perspective?

options.A: The supplier organization
options.B: The acquirer organization
options.C: An independent third-party assessor
options.D: The end user of the system

correct_answer_text: The acquirer organization

question: According to Figure 2.13 (IPO diagram for the Acquisition process), which of the following is a typical INPUT to the Acquisition process?

options.A: Acquisition agreement
options.B: Acquisition strategy/approach
options.C: Supply response
options.D: Acquisition payment

correct_answer_text: Supply response

question: According to Figure 2.13, which of the following is listed as a typical OUTPUT of the Acquisition process?

options.A: Organization strategic plan
options.B: Acquisition need
options.C: Supply response
options.D: Acquisition agreement

correct_answer_text: Acquisition agreement

question: Which of the following is NOT listed in Figure 2.13 as one of the five top-level activities of the Acquisition process?

options.A: Prepare for the acquisition
options.B: Advertise the acquisition and select the supplier
options.C: Deliver the product or service
options.D: Monitor the agreement

correct_answer_text: Deliver the product or service

question: Under 'Prepare for the acquisition,' the handbook describes collecting needs in a 'request for supply.' Which examples does it give for this mechanism?

options.A: Statement of Work (SOW) or Contract Data Requirements List (CDRL)
options.B: Request for Proposal (RFP) or Request for Quotation (RFQ)
options.C: Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) or Service Level Agreement (SLA)
options.D: Concept of Operations (ConOps) or Mission Needs Statement

correct_answer_text: Request for Proposal (RFP) or Request for Quotation (RFQ)

question: When evaluating supplier responses to the request for supply, the handbook states which two processes provide assessments necessary to determine the suitability of each response?

options.A: Risk Management and Configuration Management
options.B: Project Portfolio Management and Quality Management
options.C: Measurement and Information Management
options.D: Business or Mission Analysis and Stakeholder Needs and Requirements Definition

correct_answer_text: Project Portfolio Management and Quality Management

question: Per the handbook, under 'Establish and maintain an agreement,' the agreement establishes ALL of the following EXCEPT:

options.A: Requirements, development and delivery milestones, and verification/validation/acceptance conditions
options.B: Process requirements such as configuration management, risk management, and measurements
options.C: Exception-handling procedures, payment schedules, and handling of data rights and intellectual property
options.D: Detailed system architecture and physical interface definitions for all subsystems

correct_answer_text: Detailed system architecture and physical interface definitions for all subsystems

question: Under 'Monitor the agreement,' which other process does the handbook reference as providing necessary evaluation information regarding cost, schedule, and performance?

options.A: Quality Assurance process
options.B: Project Assessment and Control process
options.C: Measurement process
options.D: Risk Management process

correct_answer_text: Project Assessment and Control process

question: Under 'Accept the product or service,' the handbook lists actions that occur when an Acquisition process cycle concludes. Which of the following is one of those actions?

options.A: Close the project and dissolve the project team
options.B: Conduct a final review of performance to extract lessons learned for continued process performance, then retire the agreement
options.C: Transfer ownership of the agreement to the Portfolio Management process
options.D: Initiate a new supplier selection cycle for the next acquisition

correct_answer_text: Conduct a final review of performance to extract lessons learned for continued process performance, then retire the agreement

question: The handbook clarifies that the project itself is NOT closed through the Acquisition process. Through which process is the project closed?

options.A: Project Planning process
options.B: Project Assessment and Control process
options.C: Portfolio Management process
options.D: Life Cycle Model Management process

correct_answer_text: Portfolio Management process

question: Among the 'Common approaches and tips,' the handbook recommends establishing a technical point of responsibility within the organization. What is that person's role?

options.A: Serving as the legal signatory for the acquisition agreement
options.B: Monitoring and controlling individual agreements, maintaining communication with the supplier, and participating in the decision-making team to assess technical development and progress
options.C: Acting as the sole interface between the acquirer's executive leadership and the supplier
options.D: Replacing the project manager for all matters relating to the agreement

correct_answer_text: Monitoring and controlling individual agreements, maintaining communication with the supplier, and participating in the decision-making team to assess technical development and progress

question: Among the common approaches and tips, what does the handbook say about measures used to track agreement progress?

options.A: Lagging indicators are preferable to leading indicators to ensure accuracy
options.B: Define and track measures focused on true information needs; leading indicators are preferable
options.C: Only financial measures should be tracked, since technical measures are the supplier's responsibility
options.D: Measures should be defined unilaterally by the acquirer and not shared with the supplier

correct_answer_text: Define and track measures focused on true information needs; leading indicators are preferable

question: The handbook calls out past performance of the supplier as highly important, but cautions about which specific risk that should be identified and evaluated?

options.A: Increases in the supplier's overhead rate
options.B: Changes to key supplier personnel and their potential impact on the current request for supply
options.C: Shifts in the supplier's geographic location
options.D: Updates to the supplier's quality management system certifications

correct_answer_text: Changes to key supplier personnel and their potential impact on the current request for supply

question: The handbook notes that Agreement Processes can also be used for coordinating within a single organization between different business units or functions. In that case, the agreement is typically:

options.A: Forbidden, because agreements require independent legal entities
options.B: More informal, and not requiring a formal or specific contract
options.C: Identical in formality to an external contract, including a signed contract document
options.D: Limited to the Supply process only, not the Acquisition process

correct_answer_text: More informal, and not requiring a formal or specific contract

question: Per the handbook's 2.3.2 introduction, when changes occur during execution of an agreement (e.g., acquirer change requests or supply chain deviations and waivers), what document may be initiated and updated to describe the agreed changes?

options.A: A statement of compliance
options.B: A configuration management plan
options.C: A Concept of Operations (ConOps)
options.D: A Memorandum of Understanding (MOU)

correct_answer_text: A statement of compliance

question: An organization is contracted to supply a satellite system to an end customer and chooses to procure several subsystems and ground services from outside vendors. Per the handbook's dual-role discussion, what is the correct characterization of this organization?

options.A: It is solely a supplier, because it holds the prime contract with the end customer
options.B: It is solely an acquirer, because it does not build everything in-house
options.C: It is a supplier to the end customer and an acquirer to the organizations providing it system elements, materials, or services
options.D: It cannot fulfill both roles simultaneously and must designate a separate legal entity for each

correct_answer_text: It is a supplier to the end customer and an acquirer to the organizations providing it system elements, materials, or services

question: According to Figure 2.13, which of the following is NOT a typical INPUT to the Acquisition process?

options.A: Organization strategic plan
options.B: Acquisition need
options.C: Acquisition agreement
options.D: Supply response

correct_answer_text: Acquisition agreement

question: Per Figure 2.13, which of the following is listed as a typical OUTPUT of the Acquisition process?

options.A: Supply response
options.B: Acquired system or system element
options.C: Acquisition payment
options.D: Organization strategic plan

correct_answer_text: Acquisition payment

question: Under 'Prepare for the acquisition,' the handbook states that when identifying a list of potential suppliers, the suppliers:

options.A: Must be external to the acquirer organization to ensure independence
options.B: May be internal or external to the acquirer organization
options.C: Must hold a current ISO 9001 certification
options.D: Must be located within the same regulatory jurisdiction as the acquirer

correct_answer_text: May be internal or external to the acquirer organization

question: Under 'Advertise the acquisition and select the supplier,' the handbook describes attributes of viable suppliers. Which set of attributes does it explicitly call out?

options.A: Willing to conduct ethical negotiations, able to meet obligations, and willing to maintain open communications throughout the Acquisition process
options.B: Lowest unit price, shortest lead time, and largest installed base
options.C: Holds current security clearances, owns key patents, and accepts fixed-price-only terms
options.D: Located within the acquirer's national borders and using only domestic content

correct_answer_text: Willing to conduct ethical negotiations, able to meet obligations, and willing to maintain open communications throughout the Acquisition process

question: Per the handbook's 'Description' of the Acquisition process, the role of the acquirer demands familiarity with which set of processes, since it is through them that the supplier will execute the agreement?

options.A: Only the Agreement Processes (Acquisition and Supply)
options.B: The Technical, Technical Management, and Organizational Project-Enabling Processes
options.C: Only the Quality Management and Risk Management processes
options.D: Only the Business or Mission Analysis and Stakeholder Needs and Requirements Definition processes

correct_answer_text: The Technical, Technical Management, and Organizational Project-Enabling Processes

question: In the 'Elaboration' subsection, the handbook identifies the primary mechanism through which technical supplier management ensures the supplier meets the allocated project requirements. What is it?

options.A: The Statement of Work (SOW) along with a set of requirements
options.B: The acquirer's organizational strategic plan
options.C: The supplier's internal quality manual
options.D: An informal email log between technical leads

correct_answer_text: The Statement of Work (SOW) along with a set of requirements

question: Among the common approaches and tips, the handbook recommends maintaining traceability between the supplier's responses and the acquirer's solicitation primarily because doing so:

options.A: Eliminates the need for verification and validation by the supplier
options.B: Can reduce the risk of contract modifications, cancellations, or follow-on contracts to fix the product or service
options.C: Transfers all schedule risk to the supplier
options.D: Allows the acquirer to bypass the Project Assessment and Control process

correct_answer_text: Can reduce the risk of contract modifications, cancellations, or follow-on contracts to fix the product or service

question: The handbook discusses 'smart contracts' as a means of establishing and maintaining an agreement. Which best describes a smart contract as defined there?

options.A: A contract drafted exclusively by AI-based legal tools with no human oversight
options.B: A transaction protocol intended to execute automatically and control or document legally relevant events and actions according to the terms of a contract or an agreement
options.C: A contract whose terms are intentionally vague to allow flexibility in interpretation
options.D: A contract reserved for high-value, mission-critical defense acquisitions only

correct_answer_text: A transaction protocol intended to execute automatically and control or document legally relevant events and actions according to the terms of a contract or an agreement

question: An acquirer is planning an acquisition for a system that relies on technology that is not yet mature. Per the handbook's Elaboration, the planning should explicitly account for this by:

options.A: Compressing the delivery schedule to incentivize faster technology maturation
options.B: Excluding the immature technology from the SOW until it matures
options.C: Accounting for uncertainty and additional risk management actions, allowing additional margin and ample lead time in the development/production timeframe
options.D: Transferring all technology maturation risk to the end user via a waiver

correct_answer_text: Accounting for uncertainty and additional risk management actions, allowing additional margin and ample lead time in the development/production timeframe

question: Among the common approaches and tips, the handbook advises the acquirer to communicate clearly with the supplier about priorities and to avoid which behavior, because it introduces risk into the process?

options.A: Conducting periodic in-person technical reviews with the supplier
options.B: Conflicting statements or making frequent changes in the statement of need
options.C: Providing the supplier with measurement results from the Project Assessment and Control process
options.D: Tracking leading indicators alongside lagging indicators

correct_answer_text: Conflicting statements or making frequent changes in the statement of need

question: Per the Elaboration, what is the Project Manager's role with respect to the Acquisition process?

options.A: To act solely as the contract signatory and otherwise delegate the acquisition entirely to the supplier
options.B: To define, execute, and manage the acquisition, focused on project needs to deliver the system/elements/products/services that meet end user requirements and achieve acquisition milestones, in collaboration with SE practitioners and the selected contractor
options.C: To perform supplier technical verification activities personally, independent of the SE practitioners
options.D: To negotiate exclusively on cost, leaving technical scope to the supplier's discretion

correct_answer_text: To define, execute, and manage the acquisition, focused on project needs to deliver the system/elements/products/services that meet end user requirements and achieve acquisition milestones, in collaboration with SE practitioners and the selected contractor

question: Categorize each item in the Acquisition Process IPO diagram (Figure 2.13).

items.text: Advertise the acquisition and select the supplier

question: Per ISO/IEC/IEEE 15288 as cited in the SE Handbook, what is the purpose of the Supply process?

options.A: To obtain a product or service from an external supplier in accordance with the acquirer's requirements
options.B: To provide an acquirer with a product or service that meets agreed requirements
options.C: To establish the resources required by the organization to enable projects to meet stakeholder needs
options.D: To define and maintain the supplier organization's strategic plans, policies, and procedures

correct_answer_text: To provide an acquirer with a product or service that meets agreed requirements

question: From whose perspective is Section 2.3.2.2 (Supply Process) of the SE Handbook written?

options.A: From the perspective of the acquirer organization establishing a request for supply
options.B: From the perspective of an independent third-party broker between buyer and seller
options.C: From the perspective of the supplier organization
options.D: From the perspective of the end-user receiving the delivered system

correct_answer_text: From the perspective of the supplier organization

question: According to the description of the Supply process, what is its relationship to the Technical, Technical Management, and Organizational Project-Enabling Processes?

options.A: The Supply process is a subordinate sub-process invoked by each of the Technical and Technical Management processes
options.B: The Supply process replaces the Technical and Technical Management processes once an agreement is signed
options.C: The Supply process is independent of these processes and runs in parallel without interaction
options.D: The Supply process is the larger context in which the other processes are applied to execute the agreement

correct_answer_text: The Supply process is the larger context in which the other processes are applied to execute the agreement

question: Which of the following is listed as a Typical Input to the Supply process in the Figure 2.14 IPO diagram?

options.A: Acquisition need
options.B: Supply response
options.C: Request for supply
options.D: Acquired system or system element

correct_answer_text: Request for supply

question: Which of the following is listed as a Typical Output of the Supply process in Figure 2.14?

options.A: Supply response
options.B: Acquisition agreement
options.C: Acquisition payment
options.D: Acquired system or system element

correct_answer_text: Supply response

question: The Supply and Acquisition processes are described as "two sides of the same coin." Which statement best characterizes how their inputs and outputs relate?

options.A: Both processes share an identical IPO diagram with the same inputs and outputs
options.B: One party's output is typically the other party's input (e.g., the acquirer's request for supply is an input to the supplier)
options.C: Outputs of the Supply process become outputs of the Acquisition process after agreement
options.D: Both processes have completely independent inputs and outputs with no flow between them

correct_answer_text: One party's output is typically the other party's input (e.g., the acquirer's request for supply is an input to the supplier)

question: Which set of activities correctly enumerates the activities of the Supply process as listed in the SE Handbook?

options.A: Prepare for the acquisition; Advertise the acquisition and select the supplier; Establish and maintain an agreement; Monitor the agreement; Accept the product or service
options.B: Prepare for the supply; Respond to a request for supply of products or services; Establish and maintain an agreement; Execute the agreement; Deliver and support the product or service
options.C: Identify opportunities; Negotiate the contract; Build the system; Deliver the system; Close the agreement
options.D: Plan the project; Develop the system; Verify and validate; Transition to the acquirer; Operate and maintain

correct_answer_text: Prepare for the supply; Respond to a request for supply of products or services; Establish and maintain an agreement; Execute the agreement; Deliver and support the product or service

question: Under the "Respond to a request for supply" activity, which criteria are used to select appropriate acquirers?

options.A: Lowest price, fastest payment terms, and largest order volume
options.B: Geographic proximity, prior project history, and technical similarity
options.C: Willing to conduct ethical negotiations, able to meet financial obligations, and willing to maintain open communications throughout the Supply process
options.D: Holds appropriate certifications, has audited quality systems, and uses smart contracts

correct_answer_text: Willing to conduct ethical negotiations, able to meet financial obligations, and willing to maintain open communications throughout the Supply process

question: When evaluating an acquirer's request and proposing a product or service, assessments from which processes are necessary to determine the suitability of the response and the organization's ability to meet the commitments?

options.A: Risk Management, Configuration Management, Measurement, and Information Management
options.B: Project Planning, Project Assessment and Control, Decision Management, and Quality Assurance
options.C: Infrastructure Management, Knowledge Management, Life Cycle Model Management, and Human Resource Management
options.D: Portfolio Management, Human Resource Management, Quality Management, and Business or Mission Analysis

correct_answer_text: Portfolio Management, Human Resource Management, Quality Management, and Business or Mission Analysis

question: Under the "Execute the agreement" activity of the Supply process, which task initiates the work?

options.A: Advertise the opportunity and identify candidate suppliers
options.B: Render payment to the acquirer in accordance with agreed schedules
options.C: Start the project and invoke the other processes defined in the handbook
options.D: Accept delivery of products and services from the supplier

correct_answer_text: Start the project and invoke the other processes defined in the handbook

question: According to the "Deliver and support the product or service" activity, what happens after acceptance and transfer of the final products and/or services?

options.A: The supplier issues an acquisition payment to the acquirer in accordance with the agreement
options.B: The acquirer will provide payment or other consideration in accordance with all agreements, schedules, and relevant laws and regulations
options.C: The supplier closes the agreement immediately, with no further obligations or support
options.D: Ownership of the system reverts to the supplier until final lessons-learned are documented

correct_answer_text: The acquirer will provide payment or other consideration in accordance with all agreements, schedules, and relevant laws and regulations

question: How is the agreement formally closed at the end of the Supply process cycle?

options.A: Through the Project Assessment and Control process when final cost and schedule variances are reconciled
options.B: Through the Portfolio Management process, which manages the full set of systems and projects of the organization
options.C: Through the Quality Management process upon issuance of a final certificate of conformance
options.D: Through the Configuration Management process when the final baseline is archived

correct_answer_text: Through the Portfolio Management process, which manages the full set of systems and projects of the organization

question: Which item appears in the Typical Outputs list for the Supply process but NOT in the Typical Outputs list for the Acquisition process?

options.A: Change request
options.B: Supply agreement
options.C: Acquisition payment
options.D: Accepted system or system element

correct_answer_text: Supply agreement

question: Which of the following is listed among the Typical Inputs to the Supply process per Figure 2.14?

options.A: Acquisition need
options.B: Supply response
options.C: Acquired system or system element
options.D: Supply payment

correct_answer_text: Supply payment

question: Under "Prepare for the supply," which activities does the supplier organization perform?

options.A: Develop and maintain strategic plans, policies, and procedures to meet the needs of potential acquirer organizations; identify opportunities
options.B: Advertise the opportunity and select an appropriate supplier from candidate organizations
