correct_answer_text: How the system will be delivered, integrated into its operational environment, and introduced into operations (including integration with systems already in operation or replacement of systems in operation)

question: Per the Life Cycle Concepts elaboration, the Support concept addresses which set of items?

options.A: Acquisition strategy, contract deliverables, and supplier oversight
options.B: Deployment timelines, integration sequences, and operational rollout
options.C: Operating support, engineering support, maintenance support, supply support, training support, and post-deployment verification and validation
options.D: Disposal of hazardous materials, IP rights protection, and environmental impacts

correct_answer_text: Operating support, engineering support, maintenance support, supply support, training support, and post-deployment verification and validation

question: Per the Life Cycle Concepts elaboration, the Retirement concept addresses which of the following?

options.A: How the system will be used during operations by its intended users in the operational environment
options.B: How the system will be delivered and introduced into operations
options.C: How logistics and staffing will support the deployed system
options.D: How the system will be removed from operation and retired, including disposal of hazardous materials, IP rights protection, external financial/ownership interests, sustainability, environmental impacts, and security concerns

correct_answer_text: How the system will be removed from operation and retired, including disposal of hazardous materials, IP rights protection, external financial/ownership interests, sustainability, environmental impacts, and security concerns

question: In the Business or Mission Analysis "Define the problem or opportunity space" activity, which analytical technique is used to assess external factors such as Political, Economic, Social, Technological, Environmental, and Legal influences on the problem?

options.A: SWOT analysis
options.B: PESTEL analysis
options.C: FMEA analysis
options.D: Pareto analysis

correct_answer_text: PESTEL analysis

question: Per the Identify Business Needs and Requirements elaboration, business needs can be communicated in which of the following forms?

options.A: Only as engineering specifications and interface control documents
options.B: Only as a single high-level mission statement
options.C: Mission statement, goals, objectives, critical success measures, use cases, user stories, and individual need statements
options.D: Only as quantitative measures of effectiveness with specific thresholds

correct_answer_text: Mission statement, goals, objectives, critical success measures, use cases, user stories, and individual need statements

question: Per the handbook, business needs, business requirements, and critical business success measures together communicate what at the business level?

options.A: The verification and validation strategy
options.B: What is "necessary for acceptance" at the business level
options.C: The detailed design baseline of the SoI
options.D: The contract terms and conditions for supplier delivery

correct_answer_text: What is "necessary for acceptance" at the business level

question: Per the ConOps elaboration citing ISO/IEC/IEEE 29148 (2018), the ConOps serves all of the following purposes EXCEPT:

options.A: A basis for the organization to direct overall characteristics of the future business and systems
options.B: A means for the project to understand its background
options.C: A means for users to implement stakeholder requirements elicitation
options.D: A specification of detailed system-level functional and performance requirements for the SoI

correct_answer_text: A specification of detailed system-level functional and performance requirements for the SoI

question: Per Figure 2.39, which of the following is listed as one of the five Process Activities for Business or Mission Analysis (not an Input or Output)?

options.A: Concept of Operations (ConOps)
options.B: Alternative solution classes
options.C: Evaluate alternative solution classes
options.D: Critical performance measurement data

correct_answer_text: Evaluate alternative solution classes

question: Categorize each item in the Business or Mission Analysis Process IPO diagram (Figure 2.39).

items.text: Prepare for business or mission analysis
Define the problem or opportunity space
Manage the business or mission analysis

section_title: Stakeholder Needs and Requirements Definition Process
question: Per ISO/IEC/IEEE 15288 as cited in the SE Handbook, what is the purpose of the Stakeholder Needs and Requirements Definition process?

options.A: To transform the stakeholder, user-oriented view of desired capabilities into a technical view of a solution that meets the operational needs of the user
options.B: To define the stakeholder needs and requirements for a system that can provide the capabilities needed by users and other stakeholders in a defined environment
options.C: To define the business or mission problem or opportunity and identify preferred solution classes
options.D: To allocate system requirements to architectural elements and verify them against design constraints

correct_answer_text: To define the stakeholder needs and requirements for a system that can provide the capabilities needed by users and other stakeholders in a defined environment

section_title: Stakeholder Needs and Requirements Definition Process
question: According to the Handbook, what defines a 'stakeholder' in the context of this process?

options.A: Only the paying customer and the end user of the system
options.B: Any entity (individual or organization) with a legitimate interest in the system
options.C: Any party formally listed in the acquisition agreement
options.D: Only those parties who can directly approve a decision gate

correct_answer_text: Any entity (individual or organization) with a legitimate interest in the system

section_title: Stakeholder Needs and Requirements Definition Process
question: The Stakeholder Needs and Requirements Definition process activities are constrained and driven primarily by inputs developed in which other process?

options.A: The System Architecture Definition process
options.B: The Project Planning process
options.C: The Business or Mission Analysis process
options.D: The Verification process

correct_answer_text: The Business or Mission Analysis process

section_title: Stakeholder Needs and Requirements Definition Process
question: Which sequence correctly describes the transformation chain at the heart of the Stakeholder Needs and Requirements Definition process?

options.A: System requirements -> stakeholder requirements -> stakeholder needs
options.B: Preliminary life cycle concepts -> stakeholder needs -> stakeholder requirements
options.C: Business requirements -> system requirements -> stakeholder needs
options.D: Operational scenarios -> system requirements -> validation criteria

correct_answer_text: Preliminary life cycle concepts -> stakeholder needs -> stakeholder requirements

section_title: Stakeholder Needs and Requirements Definition Process
question: How do stakeholder requirements differ from stakeholder needs as characterized by this process?

options.A: Stakeholder needs are stated in stakeholder terminology and reflect their expectations; stakeholder requirements are the transformed, more rigorously stated formulation of those needs
options.B: Stakeholder needs are the technical solution view; stakeholder requirements are the business case
options.C: Stakeholder needs are validated at acceptance; stakeholder requirements are only verified at design review
options.D: Stakeholder needs are written by the supplier; stakeholder requirements are written by regulators

correct_answer_text: Stakeholder needs are stated in stakeholder terminology and reflect their expectations; stakeholder requirements are the transformed, more rigorously stated formulation of those needs

section_title: Stakeholder Needs and Requirements Definition Process
question: Per the Handbook, the stakeholder requirements drive and constrain the solution space by:

options.A: Specifying internal subsystem decompositions and software-hardware partitioning
options.B: Defining detailed verification methods and acceptance test procedures for components
options.C: Addressing stakeholder expectations for the SoI, characterizing the operational environment, and identifying external interface boundaries
options.D: Allocating budget and schedule reserves to risk-rated work packages

correct_answer_text: Addressing stakeholder expectations for the SoI, characterizing the operational environment, and identifying external interface boundaries

section_title: Stakeholder Needs and Requirements Definition Process
question: When an organization is acquiring the system, what does the Stakeholder Needs and Requirements Definition process provide as the technical description of the deliverables in an agreement?

options.A: A bill of materials and qualified vendor list
options.B: Typically, a set of system requirements for an SoI and defined interfaces at the SoI boundaries
options.C: A statement of work containing only schedule and cost commitments
options.D: A validated test report covering acceptance criteria

correct_answer_text: Typically, a set of system requirements for an SoI and defined interfaces at the SoI boundaries

section_title: Stakeholder Needs and Requirements Definition Process
question: Per Figure 2.40 (IPO diagram), which of the following is listed as a typical INPUT to the Stakeholder Needs and Requirements Definition process?

options.A: Stakeholder needs and requirements definition strategy/approach
options.B: Concept of operations (ConOps)
options.C: Validation criteria
options.D: Traceability mapping

correct_answer_text: Concept of operations (ConOps)

section_title: Stakeholder Needs and Requirements Definition Process
question: Per Figure 2.40 (IPO diagram), which of the following is listed as a typical OUTPUT of the Stakeholder Needs and Requirements Definition process?

options.A: Alternative solution classes
options.B: Source documents
options.C: Validation criteria
options.D: Problem or opportunity statement

correct_answer_text: Validation criteria

section_title: Stakeholder Needs and Requirements Definition Process
question: Per Figure 2.40, which of the following appears as a typical INPUT (not an output) of the process?

options.A: Stakeholder identification
options.B: Life cycle concepts
options.C: Validated stakeholder needs and requirements
options.D: Requirements imposed on enabling systems

correct_answer_text: Validated stakeholder needs and requirements

section_title: Stakeholder Needs and Requirements Definition Process
question: Per Figure 2.40, which of the following is one of the six top-level Activities of the Stakeholder Needs and Requirements Definition process?

options.A: Allocate stakeholder requirements to architectural elements
options.B: Verify the system against stakeholder requirements
options.C: Transform stakeholder needs into stakeholder requirements
options.D: Derive system requirements from stakeholder requirements

correct_answer_text: Transform stakeholder needs into stakeholder requirements

section_title: Stakeholder Needs and Requirements Definition Process
question: Under 'Prepare for stakeholder needs and requirements definition,' the Handbook directs the project team to identify stakeholders and then to:

options.A: Immediately publish a fixed stakeholder list with no further changes
options.B: Resolve differing interests and establish the strategy/approach for stakeholder needs and requirements definition
options.C: Translate each stakeholder's input directly into system requirements without an intervening needs step
options.D: Delegate stakeholder management entirely to the acquirer

correct_answer_text: Resolve differing interests and establish the strategy/approach for stakeholder needs and requirements definition

section_title: Stakeholder Needs and Requirements Definition Process
question: Within the 'Develop the operational concept and other life cycle concepts' activity, what is the role of operational scenarios (or use cases)?

options.A: They define detailed component-level performance budgets
options.B: They serve as final verification artifacts for the SoI
options.C: They identify required capabilities corresponding to anticipated operational and other life cycle concepts across the SoI life cycle
options.D: They establish the financial business case for portfolio approval

correct_answer_text: They identify required capabilities corresponding to anticipated operational and other life cycle concepts across the SoI life cycle

section_title: Stakeholder Needs and Requirements Definition Process
question: When defining stakeholder needs, the Handbook notes that although needs are not required to be stated as rigorously as requirements, it is useful to follow the same rules as for quality requirements because:

options.A: Stakeholder needs are contractually binding deliverables
options.B: Better-formed needs and sets of needs will result in less ambiguity when transforming the needs into requirements
options.C: ISO/IEC/IEEE 15288 mandates needs and requirements share identical formats
options.D: It eliminates the need to perform validation later in the life cycle

correct_answer_text: Better-formed needs and sets of needs will result in less ambiguity when transforming the needs into requirements

section_title: Stakeholder Needs and Requirements Definition Process
question: Per the Handbook, for each stakeholder need and each stakeholder requirement, which attributes are recommended?

options.A: Vendor, contract clause, line-item number, and procurement code
options.B: Source, rationale, priority, and criticality
options.C: Verification method, test fixture, pass criterion, and witness
options.D: Subsystem allocation, mass, power, and cost

correct_answer_text: Source, rationale, priority, and criticality

section_title: Stakeholder Needs and Requirements Definition Process
question: Which process is invoked in conjunction with this process to prioritize and select the essential stakeholder needs?

options.A: Risk Management process
options.B: Configuration Management process
options.C: Decision Management process
options.D: Quality Assurance process

correct_answer_text: Decision Management process

section_title: Stakeholder Needs and Requirements Definition Process
question: According to the Handbook, ensuring 'high quality stakeholder requirements' means each requirement should possess characteristics such as:

options.A: Necessary, singular, correct, unambiguous, feasible, appropriate to level, complete, conforming, and able to be validated
options.B: Numbered, prioritized, allocated, dollar-costed, and weighted
options.C: Decomposed, partitioned, balanced, optimized, and integrated
options.D: Drafted, reviewed, approved, archived, and audited

correct_answer_text: Necessary, singular, correct, unambiguous, feasible, appropriate to level, complete, conforming, and able to be validated

section_title: Stakeholder Needs and Requirements Definition Process
question: Under the 'Analyze stakeholder needs and requirements' activity, the project team is to define system validation criteria for each stakeholder need and requirement, plus the:

options.A: Verification strategy, verification method, and responsible test laboratory
options.B: Validation strategy, validation method, and responsible organization for providing evidence the needs and requirements have been met
options.C: Acquisition strategy, supplier method, and responsible procurement officer
options.D: Risk strategy, risk method, and responsible board

correct_answer_text: Validation strategy, validation method, and responsible organization for providing evidence the needs and requirements have been met

section_title: Stakeholder Needs and Requirements Definition Process
question: Under 'Manage the stakeholder needs and requirements definition,' which set of actions is the Handbook explicit about?

options.A: Obtain explicit agreement on the needs and requirements; establish and sustain traceability; provide CM the items needed for baselines; manage changes
options.B: Discard outdated needs without notice; rewrite requirements unilaterally; bypass CM to maintain agility
options.C: Convert needs directly into design artifacts; skip baseline approval; outsource change management
options.D: Defer traceability until verification; lock all requirements at kickoff; prohibit modifications

correct_answer_text: Obtain explicit agreement on the needs and requirements; establish and sustain traceability; provide CM the items needed for baselines; manage changes

section_title: Stakeholder Needs and Requirements Definition Process
question: In identifying stakeholders, how does the Handbook say 'detractors' of the system should be treated?

options.A: They are excluded entirely from any project consideration
options.B: They are first considered in establishing consensus needs; beyond that, they are addressed through Risk Management, threat analysis, or system requirements for security, adaptability, agility, or resilience
options.C: They are given veto authority equivalent to the customer
options.D: They are routed exclusively to the marketing department

correct_answer_text: They are first considered in establishing consensus needs; beyond that, they are addressed through Risk Management, threat analysis, or system requirements for security, adaptability, agility, or resilience

section_title: Stakeholder Needs and Requirements Definition Process
question: When direct contact with certain stakeholders (e.g., future generations or broad consumer classes) is not possible, the Handbook advises that:

options.A: Those stakeholders should be ignored to avoid speculation
options.B: Agents (such as marketing or user groups) are identified to represent the concerns of those classes of stakeholders
options.C: The acquirer must individually approve representations of each absent stakeholder
options.D: Those needs are added only after the system is delivered

correct_answer_text: Agents (such as marketing or user groups) are identified to represent the concerns of those classes of stakeholders

section_title: Stakeholder Needs and Requirements Definition Process
question: The Handbook calls reconciliation among disparate stakeholder views an aspect of which integration concept?

options.A: Vertical integration
options.B: Horizontal integration
options.C: Forward integration
options.D: Recursive integration

correct_answer_text: Horizontal integration

section_title: Stakeholder Needs and Requirements Definition Process
question: The Handbook cautions that stakeholder views are framed in the context of cognitive biases arising from role, education, work experience, and culture. The recommended response by the project team is to:

options.A: Disregard any input that appears biased
options.B: Understand these biases during elicitation in order to better understand each stakeholder's perspective
options.C: Replace stakeholder input with engineering judgment
options.D: Defer elicitation until after the architecture is fixed

correct_answer_text: Understand these biases during elicitation in order to better understand each stakeholder's perspective

section_title: Stakeholder Needs and Requirements Definition Process
question: Per the 'Elaboration' subsection, the output of the Stakeholder Needs and Requirements Definition process should support which two downstream organizational functions?

options.A: Verification testing and integration
options.B: Portfolio Management (for authorization/funding) and Acquisition (for a request for supply or internal develop-and-market authorization)
options.C: Quality Management and Knowledge Management
options.D: Decision Management and Risk Management

correct_answer_text: Portfolio Management (for authorization/funding) and Acquisition (for a request for supply or internal develop-and-market authorization)

section_title: Stakeholder Needs and Requirements Definition Process
question: Per the 'Refine Life Cycle Concepts' guidance, what is the PRIMARY objective of developing life cycle concepts within this process?

options.A: To finalize the system architecture and design
options.B: To produce a delivery schedule and cost estimate
options.C: To ensure stakeholder needs and requirements are clearly understood and the rationale for each is incorporated into the decision mechanism for later transformation into system requirements
options.D: To select the supplier and award the contract

correct_answer_text: To ensure stakeholder needs and requirements are clearly understood and the rationale for each is incorporated into the decision mechanism for later transformation into system requirements

section_title: Stakeholder Needs and Requirements Definition Process
question: The Handbook lists 'Refine Life Cycle Concepts' as part of this process; which set of life cycle concepts is given as an example?

options.A: Acquisition, deployment, operations, support, and retirement concepts
options.B: Test, integration, verification, validation, and qualification concepts
options.C: Hardware, software, firmware, data, and human concepts
options.D: Cost, schedule, scope, quality, and risk concepts

correct_answer_text: Acquisition, deployment, operations, support, and retirement concepts

section_title: Stakeholder Needs and Requirements Definition Process
question: The Handbook lists typical stakeholders to consider when identifying the set 'from whom life cycle concepts, needs, and requirements are elicited.' Which of the following is NOT among those typical stakeholders listed?

options.A: Customers, users, operators, and maintainers
options.B: Verifiers, validators, and support organizations
options.C: Regulatory bodies and approving authorities
options.D: Component-level part fabricators only after delivery

correct_answer_text: Component-level part fabricators only after delivery

section_title: Stakeholder Needs and Requirements Definition Process
question: A program manager asks you to distinguish between stakeholder needs, stakeholder requirements, and system requirements. Per Section 2.3.5.2, which characterization is correct?

options.A: Stakeholder needs reflect stakeholder expectations in their own terminology; stakeholder requirements are the transformed/rigorously stated form of those needs from the stakeholders' perspective; system requirements are generated from a technical perspective using the stakeholder needs and requirements
options.B: Stakeholder needs and stakeholder requirements are synonyms; system requirements are simply their numbered version
options.C: System requirements come first from the architect, then stakeholder requirements, then stakeholder needs are derived for validation
options.D: Stakeholder needs are technical; stakeholder requirements are commercial; system requirements are contractual

correct_answer_text: Stakeholder needs reflect stakeholder expectations in their own terminology; stakeholder requirements are the transformed/rigorously stated form of those needs from the stakeholders' perspective; system requirements are generated from a technical perspective using the stakeholder needs and requirements

section_title: Stakeholder Needs and Requirements Definition Process
question: Per the 'Analyze stakeholder needs and requirements' activity, the COMPLETE SETS of stakeholder needs and stakeholder requirements should be analyzed to ensure they are:

options.A: Numbered, allocated, decomposed, and budgeted
options.B: Correct, complete, consistent, comprehensible, appropriate to level, and feasible
options.C: Verified, validated, integrated, and accepted
options.D: Procured, contracted, scheduled, and resourced

correct_answer_text: Correct, complete, consistent, comprehensible, appropriate to level, and feasible

section_title: Stakeholder Needs and Requirements Definition Process
question: During the 'Define stakeholder needs' activity, the Handbook directs that needs be assessed and priorities be assigned in terms of which set of dimensions?

options.A: Cost, schedule, scope, and quality
options.B: Criticality, value, completeness, correctness, consistency, security, and feasibility
options.C: Reliability, availability, maintainability, and testability
options.D: Acquisition, deployment, operations, support, and retirement

correct_answer_text: Criticality, value, completeness, correctness, consistency, security, and feasibility

section_title: Stakeholder Needs and Requirements Definition Process
question: A stakeholder workshop yields conflicting, ambiguous, and partially infeasible expectations. Per Section 2.3.5.2 'Elicit or Derive Stakeholder Needs and Stakeholder Requirements,' what does the Handbook say must be present in the process?

options.A: A 'reconcile' path that recognizes prioritization of competing concerns, or even outright rejection of some stakeholder concerns due to inconsistencies or lack of feasibility
options.B: A mandatory unanimous-consent gate before requirements may be drafted
options.C: An automatic deferral of all conflicts to the acquirer to adjudicate post-delivery
options.D: A rule that the loudest stakeholder's view wins by default

correct_answer_text: A 'reconcile' path that recognizes prioritization of competing concerns, or even outright rejection of some stakeholder concerns due to inconsistencies or lack of feasibility

section_title: Stakeholder Needs and Requirements Definition Process
question: Within 'Define stakeholder needs,' which process is explicitly invoked to analyze the life cycle concepts, resolve conflicts, and assess feasibility?

options.A: The Verification process
options.B: The System Analysis process
options.C: The Configuration Management process
options.D: The Acquisition process

correct_answer_text: The System Analysis process

section_title: Stakeholder Needs and Requirements Definition Process
question: Within 'Develop the operational concept and other life cycle concepts,' which kinds of risks are the project team directed to identify?

options.A: Only schedule risks tied to integration milestones
options.B: Risks associated with management, development, operations, and disposal, including misuse and loss scenarios
options.C: Only cybersecurity risks of the deployed system
options.D: Only risks accepted by the customer in writing

correct_answer_text: Risks associated with management, development, operations, and disposal, including misuse and loss scenarios

section_title: Stakeholder Needs and Requirements Definition Process
question: Per 'Transform stakeholder needs into stakeholder requirements,' which categories of additional stakeholder requirements must be defined beyond the directly transformed needs?

options.A: Requirements relating to safety, security, sustainability, human systems integration, high-priority/critical functionality, performance, operational environment, interactions with users/external/enabling systems, and compliance with standards/regulations
options.B: Requirements relating only to cost, schedule, and contractual deliverables
options.C: Requirements relating only to internal subsystem allocations
options.D: Requirements relating only to verification methods and test fixtures

correct_answer_text: Requirements relating to safety, security, sustainability, human systems integration, high-priority/critical functionality, performance, operational environment, interactions with users/external/enabling systems, and compliance with standards/regulations

section_title: Stakeholder Needs and Requirements Definition Process
question: Per 'Analyze stakeholder needs and requirements,' after defining critical performance measures and quality characteristics, what does the Handbook direct the team to do with the analyzed stakeholder requirements before finalizing them?

options.A: Send them directly to procurement without further review
options.B: Review them with the applicable stakeholders to validate that their needs and expectations have been adequately captured and expressed
options.C: Lock them under configuration control with no stakeholder review
options.D: Defer the review until after the system is delivered

correct_answer_text: Review them with the applicable stakeholders to validate that their needs and expectations have been adequately captured and expressed

section_title: Stakeholder Needs and Requirements Definition Process
question: Per the Elaboration's 'Identify Stakeholders' guidance, when scoping the stakeholder set for SUSTAINABLE DEVELOPMENT, the Handbook specifically directs the team to:

options.A: Identify representation for future generations
options.B: Restrict the stakeholder list to the currently paying customer
options.C: Defer any sustainability considerations to the retirement phase only
options.D: Replace direct stakeholder elicitation with engineering judgment

correct_answer_text: Identify representation for future generations

section_title: Stakeholder Needs and Requirements Definition Process
question: Per the Elaboration, what is the role of the project database in this process?

options.A: It stores only contractual deliverables and excludes engineering artifacts
options.B: It captures the data and information representing the artifacts generated during this process and includes traces between stakeholder needs, stakeholder requirements, and system requirements as a foundation for later refinement/revision
options.C: It is optional and only used after the system is fielded
options.D: It is restricted to risk register entries and excludes traceability

correct_answer_text: It captures the data and information representing the artifacts generated during this process and includes traces between stakeholder needs, stakeholder requirements, and system requirements as a foundation for later refinement/revision

section_title: Stakeholder Needs and Requirements Definition Process
question: A stakeholder offers a vague preference: 'It should feel intuitive to use.' Which activity within Section 2.3.5.2 is the FIRST place this input should be addressed before any requirement statement is written?

options.A: Manage the stakeholder needs and requirements definition (baseline it as-is)
options.B: Transform stakeholder needs into stakeholder requirements (rewrite directly as a requirement)
options.C: Define stakeholder needs (capture as a need with source, rationale, priority, and criticality, applying the quality-requirement rules to reduce ambiguity)
options.D: Analyze stakeholder needs and requirements (defer until set-level analysis)

correct_answer_text: Define stakeholder needs (capture as a need with source, rationale, priority, and criticality, applying the quality-requirement rules to reduce ambiguity)

section_title: Stakeholder Needs and Requirements Definition Process
question: Per the Elaboration, SE practitioners support project management by eliciting/deriving which set of items beyond stakeholder needs and stakeholder requirements?

options.A: Only subsystem-level interface control documents
options.B: System/project constraints (e.g., cost/schedule constraints, technology limitations, applicable specifications and requirements), 'drivers' (e.g., capabilities of the competition, external threats, critical environments), and risks
options.C: Only the supplier list and bill of materials
options.D: Only the marketing positioning statement

correct_answer_text: System/project constraints (e.g., cost/schedule constraints, technology limitations, applicable specifications and requirements), 'drivers' (e.g., capabilities of the competition, external threats, critical environments), and risks

section_title: Stakeholder Needs and Requirements Definition Process
question: Per 'Prepare for stakeholder needs and requirements definition,' what does the Handbook direct regarding enabling systems or services used during this process (e.g., elicitation, recording, traceability tools)?

options.A: Plan for them through the life cycle and ensure access is available to support stakeholder needs and requirement definition
options.B: Procure them only after the requirements baseline is set
options.C: Treat them as out-of-scope deliverables of the SoI
options.D: Defer all tool decisions to the System Architecture Definition process

correct_answer_text: Plan for them through the life cycle and ensure access is available to support stakeholder needs and requirement definition

section_title: Stakeholder Needs and Requirements Definition Process
question: Per Figure 2.40, which of the following appears as a typical OUTPUT (not an input) of the Stakeholder Needs and Requirements Definition process?

options.A: Concept of operations (ConOps)
options.B: Problem or opportunity statement
options.C: Requirements imposed on enabling systems
options.D: Alternative solution classes

correct_answer_text: Requirements imposed on enabling systems

section_title: Stakeholder Needs and Requirements Definition Process
question: Categorize each item in the Stakeholder Needs and Requirements Definition Process IPO diagram (Figure 2.40).

items.text: Prepare for stakeholder needs and requirements definition
Develop the operational concept and other life cycle concepts
Transform stakeholder needs into stakeholder requirements
Manage the stakeholder needs and requirements definition
Stakeholder needs and requirements definition strategy/approach
Stakeholder needs and requirements definition report

question: According to ISO/IEC/IEEE 15288 as cited in the handbook, what is the purpose of the System Requirements Definition process?

options.A: To elicit and document the operational needs of end users in their own language for inclusion in the contract.
options.B: To transform the stakeholder, user-oriented view of desired capabilities into a technical view of a solution that meets the operational needs of the user.
options.C: To decompose the system architecture into design-to specifications for each system element.
options.D: To verify that the delivered system meets each agreed-upon stakeholder requirement.

correct_answer_text: To transform the stakeholder, user-oriented view of desired capabilities into a technical view of a solution that meets the operational needs of the user.

question: Which statement best describes the role of system requirements within the system life cycle?

options.A: They serve as the legal contract between acquirer and supplier and replace the stakeholder requirements once signed.
options.B: They are produced only after the System Architecture Definition process is complete so the architecture can constrain them.
options.C: They are the foundation of system definition and form the basis for the System Architecture Definition, Design Definition, Integration, and Verification processes.
options.D: They are an optional artifact useful only when an acquirer mandates compliance with ISO/IEC/IEEE 15288.

correct_answer_text: They are the foundation of system definition and form the basis for the System Architecture Definition, Design Definition, Integration, and Verification processes.

question: Why does the handbook stress that system requirements should be the minimum set necessary and sufficient to realize the intent of the stakeholder needs and requirements?

options.A: Because each requirement carries a cost, and later changes to system requirements have greater cost and schedule impact.
options.B: Because the acquirer pays per requirement line item in fixed-price contracts.
options.C: Because requirement databases have hard limits on the number of entries that can be traced.
options.D: Because excess requirements automatically violate the 'singular' characteristic in Table 2.7.

correct_answer_text: Because each requirement carries a cost, and later changes to system requirements have greater cost and schedule impact.

question: How is the System Requirements Definition process applied across the system hierarchy?

options.A: Sequentially and only once, after the SoI-level architecture is baselined.
options.B: Concurrently, iteratively, and recursively—particularly with the Stakeholder Needs and Requirements Definition and System Architecture Definition processes.
options.C: Only top-down via allocation from the SoI level, never bottom-up.
options.D: Independently from architecture, with allocation deferred until the verification process.

correct_answer_text: Concurrently, iteratively, and recursively—particularly with the Stakeholder Needs and Requirements Definition and System Architecture Definition processes.

question: Per the handbook, what does the System Requirements Definition process add to each system requirement as it is derived?

options.A: An allocated cost and schedule estimate.
options.B: A verification criteria.
options.C: A configuration management baseline tag.
options.D: A stakeholder approval signature.

correct_answer_text: A verification criteria.

question: According to Figure 2.41 (IPO diagram), which of the following is a typical INPUT—not output—of the System Requirements Definition process?

options.A: Verification criteria.
options.B: Stakeholder needs and requirements.
options.C: System requirements definition strategy/approach.
options.D: Traceability mapping.

correct_answer_text: Stakeholder needs and requirements.

question: Per Figure 2.41, which item is a typical OUTPUT of the System Requirements Definition process?

options.A: Life cycle concepts.
options.B: Constraints on solution.
options.C: Stakeholder identification.
options.D: Verification criteria.

correct_answer_text: Verification criteria.

question: Which of the following is listed in Figure 2.41 as one of the four high-level Activities of the System Requirements Definition process?

options.A: Validate stakeholder needs.
options.B: Allocate budgets to system elements.
options.C: Manage system requirements.
options.D: Perform configuration audit.

correct_answer_text: Manage system requirements.

question: Under 'Prepare for system requirements definition,' which task is explicitly called out by the handbook?

options.A: Establish the strategy/approach for system requirements definition and plan for the necessary enabling systems or services.
options.B: Allocate the SoI-level requirements to subsystem elements.
options.C: Confirm agreement that the system requirements meet the stakeholder needs and requirements.
options.D: Define verification success criteria for each requirement.

correct_answer_text: Establish the strategy/approach for system requirements definition and plan for the necessary enabling systems or services.

question: Under 'Define system requirements,' what does the handbook say must be defined regarding the functional boundary of the system?

options.A: The contractual boundary in terms of acquirer-supplier responsibility.
options.B: The functional boundary in terms of the behavior and properties to be provided.
options.C: The physical boundary in terms of mass, volume, and power envelope.
options.D: The organizational boundary in terms of teams and roles.

correct_answer_text: The functional boundary in terms of the behavior and properties to be provided.

question: Per the activity guidance, defining each expected system function must include which of the following?

options.A: Only the primary mission functions; enabling functions are deferred to the design process.
options.B: Both primary functions and enabling functions, including the associated performance.
options.C: Only enabling functions, because primary functions are inherited from the stakeholder requirements.
options.D: Only functions whose performance can be verified by inspection.

correct_answer_text: Both primary functions and enabling functions, including the associated performance.

question: What does the handbook include in its definition of 'necessary constraints' during the Define system requirements activity?

options.A: Only cost and schedule constraints derived from the project plan.
options.B: Only constraints imposed by the supplier's manufacturing capability.
options.C: Higher-level requirements allocated to the SoI, operational conditions, and interactions with external systems, users, operators, maintainers, and disposers.
options.D: Only safety and security constraints from regulatory bodies.

correct_answer_text: Higher-level requirements allocated to the SoI, operational conditions, and interactions with external systems, users, operators, maintainers, and disposers.

question: Under 'Analyze system requirements,' what set of properties must the requirements set be analyzed against per the handbook text?

options.A: Necessary, singular, unambiguous, verifiable, and conforming.
options.B: Correct, complete, consistent, comprehensible, appropriate to level, and feasible.
options.C: Cost, schedule, technical, programmatic, and operational.
options.D: Allocated, derived, flowed-down, traceable, and baselined.

correct_answer_text: Correct, complete, consistent, comprehensible, appropriate to level, and feasible.

question: Under 'Manage system requirements,' which of the following is an explicit task listed in the handbook?

options.A: Issue contractual change orders to the acquirer.
options.B: Confirm agreement that the system requirements meet the stakeholder needs and requirements, and establish and sustain traceability.
options.C: Perform functional decomposition of architectural elements.
options.D: Conduct the System Verification Review (SVR) prior to delivery.

correct_answer_text: Confirm agreement that the system requirements meet the stakeholder needs and requirements, and establish and sustain traceability.

question: Per the handbook, what is the key distinction between stakeholder requirements and system requirements?

options.A: Stakeholder requirements are written by acquirers; system requirements are written by regulators.
options.B: Stakeholder requirements are written from stakeholders' perspectives of what they require of the SoI; system requirements represent the technical perspective of what the SoI must meet during architecture and design.
options.C: Stakeholder requirements are verifiable; system requirements are only validatable.
options.D: Stakeholder requirements always include verification methods; system requirements never do.

correct_answer_text: Stakeholder requirements are written from stakeholders' perspectives of what they require of the SoI; system requirements represent the technical perspective of what the SoI must meet during architecture and design.

question: When a supplier receives a set of stakeholder requirements from an acquirer, what obligation does the handbook place on the supplier?

options.A: Treat the acquirer's stakeholder requirements as the only valid source and forbid additions to avoid scope creep.
options.B: Copy the stakeholder requirements verbatim into the system requirements baseline and proceed to design.
options.C: Perform an assessment for derived system requirements and for requirements from other non-acquirer stakeholders (e.g., production, test, safety, security, users, regulators).
options.D: Renegotiate the contract before defining any system requirements.

correct_answer_text: Perform an assessment for derived system requirements and for requirements from other non-acquirer stakeholders (e.g., production, test, safety, security, users, regulators).

question: What is the risk identified by the handbook if a supplier blindly follows only the acquirer-supplied requirements in the contract?

options.A: The supplier will breach intellectual property clauses.
options.B: The supplier is likely to generate an SoI that may not work in the integrated system or operational environment, resulting in a system that fails system validation.
options.C: The supplier will fail system verification, since verification criteria are missing.
options.D: The supplier will be unable to baseline requirements through Configuration Management.

correct_answer_text: The supplier is likely to generate an SoI that may not work in the integrated system or operational environment, resulting in a system that fails system validation.

question: Per Table 2.7, which characteristic is defined as: 'The requirement statement defines a capability, characteristic, constraint, or quality factor needed to satisfy a life cycle concept, need, source, or parent requirement'?

options.A: Appropriate.
options.B: Correct.
options.C: Necessary.
options.D: Feasible.

correct_answer_text: Necessary.

question: Per Table 2.7, which requirement-statement characteristic addresses the level of abstraction—i.e., that the specific intent and amount of detail is suited to the level of the entity to which it refers?

options.A: Appropriate.
options.B: Complete.
options.C: Singular.
options.D: Conforming.

correct_answer_text: Appropriate.

question: A requirement reads: 'The system shall respond quickly to user commands and shall be easy to maintain.' Which Table 2.7 characteristic(s) is/are most directly violated?

options.A: Conforming and verifiable only.
options.B: Unambiguous and singular.
options.C: Necessary and correct.
options.D: Feasible and appropriate.

correct_answer_text: Unambiguous and singular.

question: Per Table 2.7, which characteristic states that 'the requirement statement sufficiently describes the necessary capability, characteristic, constraint, conditions, or quality factor to meet the need, source, or higher-level requirement from which it was transformed'?

options.A: Singular.
options.B: Correct.
options.C: Complete.
options.D: Verifiable.

correct_answer_text: Complete.

question: Per Table 2.7, the 'Singular' characteristic of a requirement statement means:

options.A: The requirement applies to only one system element in the architecture.
options.B: The requirement statement states a single capability, characteristic, constraint, or quality factor.
options.C: The requirement is owned by a single stakeholder.
options.D: The requirement has a single verification method.

correct_answer_text: The requirement statement states a single capability, characteristic, constraint, or quality factor.

question: Per Table 2.7, the 'Feasible' characteristic is defined as:

options.A: The requirement is technically implementable using current best practices, regardless of program constraints.
options.B: The requirement can be realized within entity constraints (e.g., cost, schedule, technical, legal, ethical, safety) with acceptable risk.
options.C: The requirement has been verified at least once before in a similar system.
options.D: The requirement is achievable by at least one approved supplier.

correct_answer_text: The requirement can be realized within entity constraints (e.g., cost, schedule, technical, legal, ethical, safety) with acceptable risk.

question: Per Table 2.7, which characteristic is defined as 'the requirement statement is structured and worded such that its realization can be verified to the approving authority's satisfaction'?

options.A: Correct.
options.B: Conforming.
options.C: Verifiable.
options.D: Necessary.

correct_answer_text: Verifiable.

question: Per Table 2.7, the 'Correct' characteristic states that the requirement statement is:

options.A: An accurate representation of the need, source, or higher-level requirement from which it was transformed.
options.B: Written in accordance with the approved style guide.
options.C: Free of any grammatical or spelling errors.
options.D: Approved by the change control board.

correct_answer_text: An accurate representation of the need, source, or higher-level requirement from which it was transformed.

question: Per Table 2.7, what does the 'Conforming' characteristic require of a requirement statement?

options.A: That it conforms to the architecture model.
options.B: That it conforms to the verification plan.
options.C: That it conforms to an approved standard pattern and style guide or standard for writing and managing requirements.
options.D: That it conforms to the approved cost baseline.

correct_answer_text: That it conforms to an approved standard pattern and style guide or standard for writing and managing requirements.

question: A requirement reads: 'The aircraft shall be capable of sustained flight at Mach 12 using only commercial off-the-shelf turbofan engines within a $50,000 unit cost.' Which Table 2.7 characteristic is most directly violated?

options.A: Conforming.
options.B: Unambiguous.
options.C: Feasible.
options.D: Singular.

correct_answer_text: Feasible.

question: Per Table 2.8, which characteristic of a requirement SET is defined as: the set 'should stand alone such that it sufficiently describes the necessary capabilities, characteristics, functionality, performance, drivers, constraints, conditions, interactions, standards, regulations, and/or quality characteristics without requiring other sets of requirements at the appropriate level of abstraction'?

options.A: Consistent.
options.B: Complete.
options.C: Comprehensible.
options.D: Able to be validated.

correct_answer_text: Complete.

question: Per Table 2.8, the 'Consistent' set characteristic requires that the requirement set:

options.A: Contains individual requirements that are unique, do not conflict with or overlap with others, with homogeneous units and language consistent with the architecture model and project data dictionary.
options.B: Uses identical sentence structure for every requirement.
options.C: Is sorted alphabetically within the requirements management tool.
options.D: Contains the same number of requirements at every level of the hierarchy.

correct_answer_text: Contains individual requirements that are unique, do not conflict with or overlap with others, with homogeneous units and language consistent with the architecture model and project data dictionary.

question: Per Table 2.8, the 'Able to be validated' set characteristic means the requirement set:

options.A: Has been approved by the validation authority.
options.B: Will lead to the achievement of the set of needs and higher-level requirements within constraints (cost, schedule, technical, regulatory compliance) with acceptable risk.
options.C: Has a defined verification method for each requirement.
options.D: Has been reviewed by an independent V&V agent.

correct_answer_text: Will lead to the achievement of the set of needs and higher-level requirements within constraints (cost, schedule, technical, regulatory compliance) with acceptable risk.

question: Per Table 2.8, the 'Comprehensible' characteristic of a requirement set means:

options.A: The set is written such that it is clear as to what is expected of the entity and its relation to the macro system of which it is a part.
options.B: Every requirement in the set is written below an 8th-grade reading level.
options.C: Every requirement is documented with an explanatory diagram.
options.D: The set is reviewed by all stakeholders prior to baseline.

correct_answer_text: The set is written such that it is clear as to what is expected of the entity and its relation to the macro system of which it is a part.

question: Reviewers find that the requirement set for an avionics SoI uses 'shall provide' in some requirements and 'must deliver' in others to mean the same thing, mixes SI and English units, and overlaps in places with a separate safety requirements document. Which Table 2.8 set characteristic is most clearly violated?

options.A: Complete.
options.B: Feasible.
options.C: Consistent.
options.D: Comprehensible.

correct_answer_text: Consistent.

question: Per Table 2.9, which of the following is identified as one of the attributes that helps define needs and requirements and their intent?

options.A: Verification Results.
options.B: Rationale.
options.C: Approval Date.
options.D: Status of Implementation.

correct_answer_text: Rationale.

question: Per Table 2.9, into which attribute category does 'Owner' (A19) fall?

options.A: Attributes to Help Define Needs and Requirement and Their Intent.
options.B: Attributes Associated with System Verification and System Validation.
options.C: Attributes to Help Manage the Requirements.
options.D: Attributes to Aid in Product Line Management.

correct_answer_text: Attributes to Help Manage the Requirements.

question: In Table 2.9, the attributes annotated with an asterisk ('*') represent:

options.A: Optional attributes that should be avoided unless mandated by contract.
options.B: A proposed minimum set of attributes.
options.C: Attributes that are required only for safety-critical projects.
options.D: Attributes unique to product-line management.

correct_answer_text: A proposed minimum set of attributes.

question: Per Table 2.9, which of the following is an example of an attribute associated with system verification or system validation?

options.A: Trace to Parent.
options.B: Unique Identifier.
options.C: Success Criteria (verification or validation).
options.D: Product Line.

correct_answer_text: Success Criteria (verification or validation).

question: Per the handbook, system requirements definition is described as a concurrent, iterative, and recursive balancing process that works in which two directions?

options.A: Top-down (allocation, derivation, and flow-down) and bottom-up (compliance analysis and flow-up).
options.B: Left-to-right (sequential) and right-to-left (rework).
options.C: Acquirer-down and supplier-up only.
options.D: Operational-down and logistical-up only.

correct_answer_text: Top-down (allocation, derivation, and flow-down) and bottom-up (compliance analysis and flow-up).

question: In some domains, system requirements—because they feed the architecture and design—are referred to as:

options.A: 'As-built' or 'allocated' requirements.
options.B: 'Design-to' or 'design input' requirements.
options.C: 'Verification' or 'validation' requirements.
options.D: 'Mission' or 'capability' requirements.

correct_answer_text: 'Design-to' or 'design input' requirements.

question: When does the System Requirements Definition process stop being applied recursively across the system hierarchy?

options.A: After two levels of decomposition below the SoI.
options.B: Once verification criteria are defined for the top-level SoI.
options.C: When the system elements reach the level of detail needed to be realized via a make (e.g., build, code), buy, or reuse decision.
options.D: When the configuration management baseline is approved.

correct_answer_text: When the system elements reach the level of detail needed to be realized via a make (e.g., build, code), buy, or reuse decision.

question: Per the handbook, the resulting sets of system requirements for the system elements (produced through recursive application of the process) represent which baseline of the SoI?

options.A: The functional baseline.
options.B: The allocated baseline.
options.C: The product baseline.
options.D: The operational baseline.

correct_answer_text: The allocated baseline.

question: Per the handbook's discussion of Requirements Management (citing ISO/IEC/IEEE 29148), requirements management encompasses tasks that:

options.A: Replace requirements engineering activities after the design is baselined.
options.B: Record and maintain the evolving requirements and associated context and historical information from the requirements engineering activities.
options.C: Only flow requirements down to suppliers under contract.
options.D: Audit supplier compliance during system integration only.

correct_answer_text: Record and maintain the evolving requirements and associated context and historical information from the requirements engineering activities.

question: Per the handbook, requirements management establishes procedures for defining, controlling, and publishing baseline requirements, and provides the resulting sets of requirements to which process for baselining?

options.A: The Quality Assurance process.
options.B: The Configuration Management process.
options.C: The Risk Management process.
options.D: The Decision Management process.

correct_answer_text: The Configuration Management process.

question: A requirement on a satellite power subsystem reads: 'The system shall be reliable.' Which Table 2.7 characteristic is most clearly violated?

options.A: Necessary.
options.B: Conforming.
options.C: Verifiable.
options.D: Appropriate.

correct_answer_text: Verifiable.

question: An SoI-level requirement states: 'The pump's impeller blade root fillet shall be 3.2 mm with a surface roughness Ra of 0.8 micrometers.' Which Table 2.7 characteristic is most clearly violated?

options.A: Appropriate.
options.B: Feasible.
options.C: Verifiable.
options.D: Necessary.

correct_answer_text: Appropriate.

question: A reviewer identifies a system requirement that simply restates the marketing brochure tagline and provides a capability that does not trace to any stakeholder need, life cycle concept, or higher-level requirement. Which Table 2.7 characteristic is most clearly violated?

options.A: Singular.
options.B: Necessary.
options.C: Conforming.
options.D: Correct.

correct_answer_text: Necessary.

question: A parent stakeholder requirement states the system must operate from -40 C to +60 C. The derived system requirement reads: 'The system shall operate from 0 C to +40 C.' Which Table 2.7 characteristic is most directly violated?

options.A: Singular.
options.B: Feasible.
options.C: Correct.
options.D: Unambiguous.

correct_answer_text: Correct.

question: Reviewers note that requirements in a set are written in many different forms—some as 'shall' statements, some as bulleted lists, some as user stories, and some as paragraphs of prose—rather than following the project's approved requirement pattern. Which Table 2.7 characteristic is most clearly violated at the individual statement level?

options.A: Conforming.
options.B: Complete.
options.C: Necessary.
options.D: Correct.

correct_answer_text: Conforming.

question: A requirement reads: 'When commanded, the system shall transmit telemetry.' No transmission rate, frequency, latency, duration, or quality of service is specified, although the stakeholder need calls for a 1 Mbps S-band downlink. Which Table 2.7 characteristic is most clearly violated?

options.A: Singular.
options.B: Complete.
options.C: Verifiable.
options.D: Appropriate.

correct_answer_text: Complete.

question: Per Table 2.8, which set characteristic is defined as: 'The requirement set can be realized within entity constraints (e.g., cost, schedule, technical) with acceptable risk'?

options.A: Complete.
options.B: Feasible.
options.C: Able to be validated.
options.D: Consistent.

correct_answer_text: Feasible.

question: After a major architectural change, several allocated requirement sets at the subsystem level no longer fully reflect the parent SoI capabilities they were transformed from. Per Table 2.8, which set characteristic is most clearly violated?

options.A: Comprehensible.
options.B: Consistent.
options.C: Correct.
options.D: Feasible.

correct_answer_text: Correct.

question: Per Table 2.9, into which attribute category does 'Trace to Parent' (A2) fall?

options.A: Attributes Associated with System Verification and System Validation.
options.B: Attributes to Help Manage the Requirements.
options.C: Attributes to Help Define Needs and Requirement and Their Intent.
options.D: Attributes to Show Applicability and Enable Reuse.

correct_answer_text: Attributes to Help Define Needs and Requirement and Their Intent.

question: Per Table 2.9, which attribute category contains 'Applicability,' 'Region,' 'Country,' 'State/Province,' 'Market Segment,' and 'Business Unit'?

options.A: Attributes to Aid in Product Line Management.
options.B: Attributes to Show Applicability and Enable Reuse.
options.C: Attributes to Help Manage the Requirements.
options.D: Attributes Associated with System Verification and System Validation.

correct_answer_text: Attributes to Show Applicability and Enable Reuse.

question: Per the handbook's text on top-down work, what is the distinction between 'allocation' and 'derivation' as steps in defining lower-level system requirements?

options.A: Allocation is performed by the acquirer; derivation is performed by the supplier.
options.B: Allocation assigns parent system requirements to system elements at the next hierarchy level; derivation then assigns (creates) the system requirements at that next level so that the intent of the allocated parent requirement is met.
options.C: Allocation creates new requirements at the same level; derivation moves requirements to a different level.
options.D: Allocation is bottom-up flow-up; derivation is top-down flow-down.

correct_answer_text: Allocation assigns parent system requirements to system elements at the next hierarchy level; derivation then assigns (creates) the system requirements at that next level so that the intent of the allocated parent requirement is met.

question: Under what specific condition does the handbook say a set of stakeholder requirements may be used directly as the set of system requirements?

options.A: Whenever the project is on a fixed-price contract.
options.B: Whenever the acquirer mandates that no derived requirements be added.
options.C: In some cases—particularly smaller, internal projects—where sufficient analysis went into the stakeholder requirements such that their implementation will result in the stakeholder needs being met; additional requirements may also be added as needed.
options.D: Whenever the SoI is a software-only system.

correct_answer_text: In some cases—particularly smaller, internal projects—where sufficient analysis went into the stakeholder requirements such that their implementation will result in the stakeholder needs being met; additional requirements may also be added as needed.

question: Per the handbook, what is said about implementation bias when defining system requirements?

options.A: Implementation bias is required so that downstream design teams know what to build.
options.B: Outputs must be traceable and consistent with the life cycle concepts, stakeholder needs, and stakeholder requirements, without introducing unnecessary implementation biases; though avoiding implementation is good practice, it is not always possible.
options.C: Implementation bias is acceptable only for software-intensive systems.
options.D: Implementation bias is prohibited and any requirement containing it must be rejected at review.

correct_answer_text: Outputs must be traceable and consistent with the life cycle concepts, stakeholder needs, and stakeholder requirements, without introducing unnecessary implementation biases; though avoiding implementation is good practice, it is not always possible.

question: Per the handbook, which activity under 'Analyze system requirements' supports technical achievement monitoring?

options.A: Defining critical performance measures.
options.B: Establishing the configuration management baseline.
options.C: Allocating system requirements to lower-level elements.
options.D: Negotiating contract change orders with the acquirer.

correct_answer_text: Defining critical performance measures.

question: Categorize each item in the System Requirements Definition Process IPO diagram (Figure 2.41).
From Table 2.7, drag each short definition or violation example to the requirement statement characteristic it describes.

items.text: Defines a capability, characteristic, constraint, or quality factor needed to satisfy a life cycle concept, need, source, or parent requirement.
The specific intent and amount of detail of the statement is appropriate to the level (e.g., level of abstraction, organization, or system architecture) of the entity to which it refers.
Stated such that the intent is clear and the requirement can be interpreted in only one way by all the intended stakeholders.
Sufficiently describes the necessary capability, characteristic, constraint, conditions, or quality factor to meet the need, source, or higher-level requirement from which it was transformed.
States a single capability, characteristic, constraint, or quality factor.
Can be realized within entity constraints (e.g., cost, schedule, technical, legal, ethical, safety) with acceptable risk.
Structured and worded such that its realization can be verified to the approving authority's satisfaction.
An accurate representation of the need, source, or higher-level requirement from which it was transformed.
Conforms to an approved standard pattern and style guide or standard for writing and managing requirements.
Violation: 'The system shall be user-friendly and fast' — vague wording allows multiple interpretations by stakeholders.
Violation: 'The system shall display status AND log errors AND send alerts' — bundles multiple capabilities in one statement.
Violation: 'The system shall travel faster than the speed of light' — cannot be realized within technical/physical constraints at acceptable risk.
Violation: 'The system shall be reliable' — has no measurable criterion, so realization cannot be confirmed by test, inspection, analysis, or demonstration.
Violation: A subsystem-level requirement specifies pin-level wiring detail more suited to a component spec — wrong level of abstraction for the entity.
Violation: The statement uses 'should' and free-form prose instead of the project's mandated 'shall' template and style guide.

question: From Table 2.8, drag each short definition or violation example to the requirement set characteristic it describes.

items.text: The requirement set for a given SOI should stand alone such that it sufficiently describes the necessary capabilities, characteristics, functionality, performance, drivers, constraints, conditions, interactions, standards, regulations, and/or quality characteristics without requiring other sets of requirements at the appropriate level of abstraction.

The requirement set contains individual requirements that are unique, do not conflict with or overlap with others in the set, and the units and measurement systems they use are homogeneous; the language used within the sets is consistent (i.e., the same words are used throughout the set to mean the same thing), and all terms are consistent with the architectural model, project glossary, and project data dictionary.
The requirement set can be realized within entity constraints (e.g., cost, schedule, technical) with acceptable risk.
The requirement set is written such that it is clear as to what is expected of the entity and its relation to the macro system of which it is a part.

The requirement set will lead to the achievement of the set of needs and higher-level requirements within the constraints (such as cost, schedule, technical, and regulatory compliance) with acceptable risk.
The requirement set is an accurate representation of the needs, sources, or higher-level requirements from which it was transformed.
Two requirements in the set contradict each other: one specifies mass shall not exceed 50 kg while another specifies mass shall be at least 60 kg.
The set omits any requirement covering thermal environmental conditions even though the SoI must operate in extreme cold, forcing reviewers to consult an external document to know what is expected.
The set demands a 0.0001-second end-to-end latency that no available technology can achieve within the project's cost and schedule constraints.
The set, as a whole, will not actually deliver the stakeholder mission outcomes because key needs were dropped during transformation, so executing it would not satisfy the higher-level needs.

The set uses the terms 'operator,' 'user,' and 'crew member' interchangeably and switches between SI and US-customary units across different requirements, leaving readers unsure how the set fits into the larger system.

Several requirements in the set misstate values from the parent stakeholder requirements (e.g., listing 100 hr MTBF when the source requires 1000 hr), so the set does not accurately reflect its sources.

question: Per ISO/IEC/IEEE 15288, what is the purpose of the System Architecture Definition process?

options.A: To provide sufficient detailed data and information about the system and its elements to realize the solution in accordance with the system requirements.
options.B: To generate system architecture alternatives, select one or more alternative(s) that address stakeholder concerns and system requirements, and express this in consistent views and models.
options.C: To transform stakeholder needs into a verifiable set of system requirements expressed in the technical language of the developer.
options.D: To provide a rigorous basis of data and information for technical understanding to aid decision-making and technical assessments across the life cycle.

correct_answer_text: To generate system architecture alternatives, select one or more alternative(s) that address stakeholder concerns and system requirements, and express this in consistent views and models.

question: The System Architecture Definition process transforms inputs such as related architectures, organizational policies, life cycle concepts, stakeholder concerns, and system requirements into what primary output?

options.A: A detailed bill of materials and manufacturing instructions for each system element.
options.B: A verification and validation plan with acceptance criteria for each requirement.
options.C: The fundamental concepts and properties of the system and the governing principles for evolution of the system and its related life cycle processes.
options.D: A configuration management baseline of approved engineering change proposals.

correct_answer_text: The fundamental concepts and properties of the system and the governing principles for evolution of the system and its related life cycle processes.

question: According to the handbook, how should the system architecture description be treated over the system life cycle?

options.A: As a frozen baseline that must not change once approved at preliminary design review.
options.B: As a living artifact reflecting both the changing expectations and the evolution of our understanding of what the system solution should be.
options.C: As a contractually binding document that supersedes all stakeholder requirements once issued.
options.D: As an internal working aid that is discarded once the design specification is released.

correct_answer_text: As a living artifact reflecting both the changing expectations and the evolution of our understanding of what the system solution should be.

question: Which standard specifies terms, concepts, and principles considered necessary to address stakeholder concerns and carry out enterprise creation programs and incremental change projects throughout the enterprise's whole life?

options.A: ISO/IEC/IEEE 42010
options.B: ISO/IEC/IEEE 42020
options.C: ISO/IEC/IEEE 15288

question: Which of the following is listed in Figure 2.42 as a typical INPUT to the System Architecture Definition process (not an output)?

options.A: System architecture description
options.B: System architecture definition strategy/approach
options.C: Life cycle concepts
options.D: System architecture rationale

correct_answer_text: Life cycle concepts

question: Which of the following is listed in Figure 2.42 as a typical OUTPUT of the System Architecture Definition process?

options.A: Alternative solution classes
options.B: System architecture description
options.C: Constraints on solution
options.D: Validated system architecture and design

correct_answer_text: System architecture description

question: Which set correctly lists the top-level activities of the System Architecture Definition process per Figure 2.42?

options.A: Prepare for system architecture definition; Conceptualize the system architecture; Evaluate the system architecture; Elaborate the system architecture; Manage results of system architecture definition.
options.B: Define problem; Develop solution; Verify solution; Validate solution; Deliver solution.
options.C: Prepare for architecture; Create the system design; Evaluate the system design; Manage results of design definition.
options.D: Identify stakeholders; Elicit requirements; Allocate requirements; Trace requirements; Baseline requirements.

correct_answer_text: Prepare for system architecture definition; Conceptualize the system architecture; Evaluate the system architecture; Elaborate the system architecture; Manage results of system architecture definition.

question: During the 'Prepare for system architecture definition' activity, the architect establishes the approach for architecting. Which combination of items belongs to that approach?

options.A: Detailed parts list, manufacturing tolerances, and inspection procedures.
options.B: Architecture roadmap and strategy, methods, frameworks, patterns, modeling techniques, tools, and need for enabling systems.
options.C: Cost baseline, earned-value reporting cadence, and risk register thresholds.
options.D: Final integration sequence, test cases, and acceptance criteria.

correct_answer_text: Architecture roadmap and strategy, methods, frameworks, patterns, modeling techniques, tools, and need for enabling systems.

question: Per the handbook, the 'Conceptualize the system architecture' activity is explicitly based on which underlying ISO/IEC/IEEE 42020 process?

options.A: The Architecture Evaluation process.
options.B: The Architecture Elaboration process.
options.C: The Architecture Conceptualization process.
options.D: The Architecture Governance process.

correct_answer_text: The Architecture Conceptualization process.

question: Per Figure 2.43, which three processes are identified as the 'core architecture processes' per ISO/IEC/IEEE 42020?

options.A: Architecture Governance, Architecture Allocation, and Architecture Verification.
options.B: Architecture Conceptualization, Architecture Evaluation, and Architecture Elaboration.
options.C: Architecture Planning, Architecture Implementation, and Architecture Disposal.
options.D: Architecture Requirements, Architecture Design, and Architecture Maintenance.

correct_answer_text: Architecture Conceptualization, Architecture Evaluation, and Architecture Elaboration.

question: Which statement most accurately characterizes the Architecture Conceptualization process per ISO/IEC/IEEE 42020?

options.A: It documents an architecture in a sufficiently complete and correct manner for the intended uses of the architecture.
options.B: It determines the extent to which one or more architectures meet their objectives, address stakeholder concerns, and meet relevant requirements.
options.C: It characterizes the problem space and determines suitable solutions that address stakeholder concerns, achieve architecture objectives, and meet relevant requirements.
options.D: It maintains bi-directional traceability between architectural entities, requirements, and analysis results.

correct_answer_text: It characterizes the problem space and determines suitable solutions that address stakeholder concerns, achieve architecture objectives, and meet relevant requirements.

question: Per Figure 2.43, what is the primary output flowing from the Architecture Elaboration process?

options.A: Problem space definition and architecture objectives.
options.B: Critical success criteria and solution space definition.
options.C: Evaluation results, evaluation findings, and recommendations.
options.D: Architecture models and views, and architecture descriptions.

correct_answer_text: Architecture models and views, and architecture descriptions.

question: Per ISO/IEC/IEEE 42010, an architecture description of a system is composed of what?

options.A: Architecture views.
options.B: Stakeholder requirements.
options.C: Configuration items.
options.D: Verification procedures.

correct_answer_text: Architecture views.

question: Per ISO/IEC/IEEE 42010, what is a viewpoint?

options.A: An information part comprising a portion of an architecture description.
options.B: The set of conventions for the creation, interpretation and use of views to frame one or more concerns of stakeholders.
options.C: A reusable, configurable architectural entity comprising a minimal set of elements applicable for different situations.
options.D: The syntax and semantics intended for describing the architecture and creating view components.

correct_answer_text: The set of conventions for the creation, interpretation and use of views to frame one or more concerns of stakeholders.

question: Per ISO/IEC/IEEE 42010, a view is best defined as:

options.A: An information part comprising a portion of an architecture description, composed of view components derived from models and non-model sources of information.
options.B: A standardized set of conventions for the creation of architecture descriptions.
options.C: A reusable architectural pattern with rules for instantiation.
options.D: A formal language used to express architectural relationships.

correct_answer_text: An information part comprising a portion of an architecture description, composed of view components derived from models and non-model sources of information.

question: Per ISO/IEC/IEEE 42010, what does an architecture framework contain?

options.A: A complete set of validated stakeholder requirements ready for allocation.
options.B: Standardized viewpoints, view templates, metamodels, model templates, etc. that aid in development of architecture views.
options.C: Source code libraries and compilers used to realize the architecture.
options.D: The verification methods, test cases, and acceptance criteria for the architecture.

correct_answer_text: Standardized viewpoints, view templates, metamodels, model templates, etc. that aid in development of architecture views.

question: Per ISO/IEC/IEEE 42010, which of the following are identified as kinds of 'architecture considerations'?

options.A: Stakeholder concerns, architecture aspects, and stakeholder perspectives.
options.B: Functional, performance, and physical requirements.
options.C: Verification, validation, and certification activities.
options.D: Cost, schedule, and risk constraints.

correct_answer_text: Stakeholder concerns, architecture aspects, and stakeholder perspectives.

question: Which statement BEST captures the handbook's distinction between System Architecture and System Design (an ASEP high-value distinction)?

options.A: Architecture and design are synonyms; the handbook uses the terms interchangeably.
options.B: System Architecture focuses on essential concepts, properties, structure, behaviors, and features of the system solution, while Design Definition focuses on developing an overall design sufficiently detailed to allow its realization.
options.C: System Architecture provides manufacturing drawings, while Design Definition produces stakeholder requirements.
options.D: System Architecture occurs only after Design Definition is complete and baselined.

correct_answer_text: System Architecture focuses on essential concepts, properties, structure, behaviors, and features of the system solution, while Design Definition focuses on developing an overall design sufficiently detailed to allow its realization.

question: Per the handbook, what property is desirable in an effective architecture with respect to design?

options.A: It should specify the exact design solution to remove all designer discretion.
