options.A: Logistics delay, administrative delay, and active repair only
options.B: Failure detection/failure isolation (FD/FI), disassembly, active repair, reassembly, and system testing
options.C: Preventive maintenance, corrective maintenance, and predictive maintenance
options.D: Operator training, parts procurement, and shipping

correct_answer_text: Failure detection/failure isolation (FD/FI), disassembly, active repair, reassembly, and system testing

question: Section 3.1.8 cautions that MTTR refers to the mean of an underlying probability distribution. What distribution does it state maintenance times tend to follow, especially for electronic systems without built-in test capability?

options.A: Exponential distribution
options.B: Normal (Gaussian) distribution
options.C: Lognormal distribution
options.D: Weibull distribution with shape parameter of 1

correct_answer_text: Lognormal distribution

question: Per Section 3.1.8, which type of maintenance is scheduled based on the in-service condition of a system to estimate when maintenance should be performed?

options.A: Corrective maintenance
options.B: Preventive maintenance
options.C: Predictive maintenance
options.D: System upgrades

correct_answer_text: Predictive maintenance

question: Per Section 3.1.8, corrective maintenance is best described as:

options.A: Scheduled maintenance to retain a system at a specified level of performance through systematic inspection and servicing
options.B: Unscheduled maintenance accomplished, as a result of failure, to restore a system to a specified level of performance
options.C: Scheduled maintenance based on the in-service condition of a system
options.D: Periodic maintenance to support system life extension and performance upgrades

correct_answer_text: Unscheduled maintenance accomplished, as a result of failure, to restore a system to a specified level of performance

question: Per Section 3.1.8, the maintainability engineering plan should consider aspects such as interchangeability, accessibility, and testability. Testability includes which of the following?

options.A: Built-in test (BIT) capability, diagnostic test equipment, and support software
options.B: Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA) and Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)
options.C: Service-level agreement (SLA) downtime metrics
options.D: Inherent, achieved, and operational availability measurement methods

correct_answer_text: Built-in test (BIT) capability, diagnostic test equipment, and support software

question: Per Section 3.1.8, why does the handbook caution against using verification by test as the primary means of demonstrating quantitative reliability requirements?

options.A: Because reliability tests have been replaced by formal proof techniques
options.B: Because verification by test of reliability is often not practical, especially for high reliability requirements
options.C: Because reliability metrics cannot be expressed quantitatively in any form
options.D: Because ANSI/GEIA-STD-0009 forbids reliability verification by test

correct_answer_text: Because verification by test of reliability is often not practical, especially for high reliability requirements

question: Per Section 3.1.8, which case study from Section 6.4 is explicitly cited as illustrating the importance of maintainability?

options.A: Case 1 (Therac-25)
options.B: Case 4 (Design for Maintainability - Incubators / NeoNurture)
options.C: Case 2 (Oresund Bridge)
options.D: Case 5 (Artificial Intelligence in Systems Engineering - Autonomous Vehicles)

correct_answer_text: Case 4 (Design for Maintainability - Incubators / NeoNurture)

question: Section 3.1.8 advises that RAM activities should be planned and integrated with other SE processes. What practical approach does the handbook recommend?

options.A: Defer all RAM planning to the utilization stage, when actual failure data becomes available
options.B: Develop detailed reliability and maintainability plans early in the system development process and integrate these plans with the SE management plan (SEMP)
options.C: Maintain RAM plans as standalone documents that intentionally remain separate from the SEMP to avoid coupling
options.D: Use the reliability program plan only after the first production unit has been delivered

correct_answer_text: Develop detailed reliability and maintainability plans early in the system development process and integrate these plans with the SE management plan (SEMP)

question: Per Section 3.1.8, why is availability often seen as the most important of the three RAM quality characteristics from the viewpoint of a user or acquirer?

options.A: Because availability is the only RAM characteristic with a formal mathematical definition
options.B: Because any availability loss can usually easily be translated to mission or production loss and increased costs
options.C: Because availability is independent of reliability and maintainability and can be specified separately
options.D: Because availability is the only characteristic that drives the system architecture

correct_answer_text: Because any availability loss can usually easily be translated to mission or production loss and increased costs

question: Per Section 3.1.8, achievement of a required availability usually requires what kind of engineering decision-making?

options.A: Specifying availability solely as a function of inherent reliability, with no consideration of maintainability
options.B: Trade-offs between reliability and maintainability, and other requirements and constraints (e.g., performance, cost)
options.C: Maximizing MTBF without regard to MTTR
options.D: Selecting either preventive maintenance or corrective maintenance, but not both

correct_answer_text: Trade-offs between reliability and maintainability, and other requirements and constraints (e.g., performance, cost)

question: Section 3.1.8 notes that 'failure mode avoidance' approaches aim to improve reliability primarily at what point in the life cycle, and on what basis?

options.A: Late in the utilization stage, by replacing parts that have exhibited high failure rates
options.B: Early during development stages, by evaluating system functions, technology maturity, system architecture, redundancy, and design options in terms of potential failure modes
options.C: Only during verification, by accelerated life testing of all candidate designs
options.D: During retirement, by capturing lessons learned for the next generation

correct_answer_text: Early during development stages, by evaluating system functions, technology maturity, system architecture, redundancy, and design options in terms of potential failure modes

question: Per the SE Handbook 5e, Resilience Engineering is best defined as an approach that:

options.A: Minimizes the probability of failure of system components over a specified period
options.B: Provides required capability when facing adversity
options.C: Restores the original system architecture after any disruption
options.D: Eliminates all sources of adversity from the operating environment

correct_answer_text: Provides required capability when facing adversity

question: Which statement BEST captures the fundamental distinction between reliability and resilience as presented in Section 3.1.9?

options.A: Reliability addresses recovery from disruption; resilience addresses prevention of component failure
options.B: Reliability focuses on avoiding failure under nominal conditions; resilience focuses on delivering required capability when faced with adverse conditions
options.C: Reliability and resilience are synonyms used in different application domains
options.D: Reliability applies only to hardware; resilience applies only to software

correct_answer_text: Reliability focuses on avoiding failure under nominal conditions; resilience focuses on delivering required capability when faced with adverse conditions

question: The handbook emphasizes that resilience focuses on which of the following?

options.A: Maintaining the original architecture and composition of the system at all costs
options.B: Maximizing mean time between failures of system components
options.C: Providing the required capability — not necessarily on maintaining the architecture or composition of the system
options.D: Eliminating all single points of failure through redundancy

correct_answer_text: Providing the required capability — not necessarily on maintaining the architecture or composition of the system

question: According to Section 3.1.9, what are the three fundamental objectives (Layer 1 taxonomy) of resilience for engineered systems?

options.A: Predict, Protect, Preserve
options.B: Avoid, Withstand, Recover
options.C: Detect, Isolate, Repair
options.D: Absorb, Adapt, Anticipate

correct_answer_text: Avoid, Withstand, Recover

question: In the resilience taxonomy, the fundamental objective "Avoid" is defined as:

options.A: Resist capability degradation when stressed
options.B: Replenish lost capability after degradation
options.C: Eliminate or reduce exposure to stress
options.D: Disperse functions across multiple system elements

correct_answer_text: Eliminate or reduce exposure to stress

question: The resilience fundamental objective "Withstand" is defined in Section 3.1.9 as:

options.A: Eliminate or reduce exposure to stress
options.B: Resist capability degradation when stressed
options.C: Replenish lost capability after degradation
options.D: Restructure resources to provide capabilities after stress

correct_answer_text: Resist capability degradation when stressed

question: The resilience fundamental objective "Recover" is defined as:

options.A: Eliminate or reduce exposure to stress
options.B: Resist capability degradation when stressed
options.C: Replenish lost capability after degradation
options.D: Anticipate the nature of potential adversities before they occur

correct_answer_text: Replenish lost capability after degradation

question: In the two-layer resilience objectives taxonomy, what distinguishes Layer 2 "means objectives" from Layer 1 "fundamental objectives"?

options.A: Means objectives are values or ends in themselves; fundamental objectives are not
options.B: Means objectives are organized in a strict one-to-one mapping with fundamental objectives
options.C: Means objectives are not values or ends in themselves — their value resides in helping achieve the three fundamental objectives of resilience
options.D: Means objectives apply only to cyber-physical systems while fundamental objectives apply to all systems

correct_answer_text: Means objectives are not values or ends in themselves — their value resides in helping achieve the three fundamental objectives of resilience

question: In Section 3.1.9, how is "Robustness" characterized as a resilience means objective?

options.A: The ability of a structure to withstand adverse and unforeseen events or consequences of human errors without being damaged (damage insensitivity)
options.B: The ability of a material or structure to resist failure due to the presence of flaws for a specified period of unrepaired usage
options.C: Replenishing lost capability after degradation has occurred
options.D: Restructuring resources to provide capabilities after a stress event

correct_answer_text: The ability of a structure to withstand adverse and unforeseen events or consequences of human errors without being damaged (damage insensitivity)

question: Which best contrasts Robustness with Resilience based on Section 3.1.9?

options.A: Robustness is the overall ability to deliver capability under adversity; resilience is one specific means of achieving it
options.B: Robustness (damage insensitivity) is one means objective by which resilience — providing required capability under adversity — is achieved
options.C: Robustness and resilience are identical terms used in different ISO standards
options.D: Robustness applies only after degradation; resilience applies only before stress

correct_answer_text: Robustness (damage insensitivity) is one means objective by which resilience — providing required capability under adversity — is achieved

question: According to Section 3.1.9, what is the relationship between resilience and survivability?

options.A: Survivability subsumes resilience as a broader concept
options.B: Resilience typically subsumes survivability
options.C: Resilience and survivability are mutually exclusive QCs
options.D: Survivability replaced resilience in the 2010 timeframe

correct_answer_text: Resilience typically subsumes survivability

question: Per the handbook, what is meant by "adversity" in the context of resilience engineering?

options.A: Only malicious actions taken by adversarial parties against the system
options.B: Only environmental hazards that exceed the system's design envelope
options.C: Anything that might degrade the capability provided by a system
options.D: Only single-point random failures of system components

correct_answer_text: Anything that might degrade the capability provided by a system

question: Which statement about the scope of adversity in resilience engineering is MOST accurate per Section 3.1.9?

options.A: Adversities must arise from outside the system to be in scope
options.B: Adversities are limited to malicious acts by adversarial parties
options.C: Adversities may arise from inside or outside the system, may be a single event or a complex causal chain over time, and may be issues, risks, or unknown-unknowns
options.D: Adversities must be expected and quantified in advance to be considered

correct_answer_text: Adversities may arise from inside or outside the system, may be a single event or a complex causal chain over time, and may be issues, risks, or unknown-unknowns

question: Section 3.1.9 distinguishes the engineering view of resilience from non-engineering contexts how?

options.A: Non-engineering resilience emphasizes avoidance; engineering resilience focuses solely on recovery
options.B: Non-engineering resilience is often limited to the ability to recover after degradation; engineering resilience recommends also including "avoiding" and "withstanding" adversity in scope
options.C: Engineering resilience applies only to hardware while non-engineering resilience applies only to organizations
options.D: There is no meaningful difference between the two views

correct_answer_text: Non-engineering resilience is often limited to the ability to recover after degradation; engineering resilience recommends also including "avoiding" and "withstanding" adversity in scope

question: Per Section 3.1.9, which of the following is identified as a means objective for achieving resilience?

options.A: Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)
options.B: Graceful Degradation
options.C: Throughput maximization
options.D: Six Sigma quality control

correct_answer_text: Graceful Degradation

question: In the resilience means-objectives list, "Anticipation" is defined as:

options.A: Reacting appropriately and dynamically to the specific situation to limit consequences
options.B: The ability of a system to adapt to deliver required capability in unpredictably evolving conditions
options.C: Establishing awareness of the nature of potential adversities, their likely consequences, and appropriate responses, prior to the adversity stressing the system
options.D: Limiting the propagation of damage within the system

correct_answer_text: Establishing awareness of the nature of potential adversities, their likely consequences, and appropriate responses, prior to the adversity stressing the system

question: Which means objective in Section 3.1.9 is defined as "dispersing missions, functions, or system elements across multiple systems or system elements"?

options.A: Constrain
options.B: Disaggregation
options.C: Continuity
options.D: Redeploy

correct_answer_text: Disaggregation

question: Per Table 3.3 (Resilience considerations), what should the Business or Mission Analysis Process include for resilience?

options.A: Final selection of the system architecture based on cost only
options.B: Identification of adversities and expectations for performance under those adversities when defining the problem space
options.C: Detailed component-level reliability allocation models
options.D: Validation testing of the production unit against nominal performance envelopes

correct_answer_text: Identification of adversities and expectations for performance under those adversities when defining the problem space

question: Per Table 3.3, what does the System Architecture Definition Process require regarding resilience requirements?

options.A: Resilience requirements should be deferred until after design definition is complete
options.B: Resilience requirements significantly limit and guide the range of acceptable architectures; it is critical that they are mature when used for architecture selection
options.C: Resilience requirements may be omitted if reliability requirements are well-defined
options.D: Resilience requirements are derived solely from component reliability data

correct_answer_text: Resilience requirements significantly limit and guide the range of acceptable architectures; it is critical that they are mature when used for architecture selection

question: Per Table 3.3, what role does the Stakeholder Needs and Requirements Definition Process play for resilience?

options.A: It excludes adversity-related considerations to keep stakeholder requirements focused on nominal capability
options.B: It identifies stakeholder expectations for capability under adverse conditions, including degraded/alternate but useful modes of operation
options.C: It limits stakeholders to those representing operations under nominal conditions only
options.D: It defers all resilience scenarios to the verification process

correct_answer_text: It identifies stakeholder expectations for capability under adverse conditions, including degraded/alternate but useful modes of operation

question: Section 3.1.9 emphasizes WHEN resilience must be considered in the SE life cycle. Which statement is correct?

options.A: Resilience considerations should be deferred until operations and support
options.B: Resilience should be addressed only after design definition is complete
options.C: Resilience must be considered throughout the SE life cycle, but it is critical that it be considered in the early life cycle stages that lead to development of resilience requirements
options.D: Resilience is addressed exclusively during verification and validation

correct_answer_text: Resilience must be considered throughout the SE life cycle, but it is critical that it be considered in the early life cycle stages that lead to development of resilience requirements

question: Per Section 3.1.9, Figure 3.7 "Timewise values of notional resilience scenario parameters" notionally depicts which set of parameters varying as a function of time?

options.A: Reliability, availability, and maintainability over the system life cycle
options.B: The required capability, the stress on the system, and the delivered capability
options.C: Cost, schedule, and performance over the operations phase
options.D: Mean time between failures, mean time to repair, and inherent availability

correct_answer_text: The required capability, the stress on the system, and the delivered capability

question: Per Section 3.1.9, resilience requirements often take what form?

options.A: A single quantitative MTBF allocation
options.B: A resilience scenario — possibly many such scenario threads in the ConOps or OpsCon
options.C: A fault tree linking initiating events to top-level loss
options.D: A binary pass/fail acceptance test at end-of-line

correct_answer_text: A resilience scenario — possibly many such scenario threads in the ConOps or OpsCon

question: Per Section 3.1.9, which item is identified as a component of the information typically included in a resilience scenario?

options.A: Bill of materials and supplier qualification status
options.B: Capability(s) of interest with their metric(s) and units, and target value(s) (required amount) of the capability(s)
options.C: Disposal procedures and end-of-life recycling targets
options.D: Initial public offering schedule and revenue forecasts

correct_answer_text: Capability(s) of interest with their metric(s) and units, and target value(s) (required amount) of the capability(s)

question: In a resilience scenario, what is a "stress" per Section 3.1.9?

options.A: A subjective measure of operator workload during nominal operations
options.B: Any cost overrun or schedule slip in the program
options.C: A type of adversity — a proximate force or influence directly affecting the system that can cause degradation of the system's ability to deliver required capability
options.D: A residual risk score from the program risk register

correct_answer_text: A type of adversity — a proximate force or influence directly affecting the system that can cause degradation of the system's ability to deliver required capability

question: Section 3.1.9 notes that resilience requirements may have multiple resilience targets. Which set of target types is explicitly mentioned?

options.A: Minimum, Maximum, and Nominal
options.B: Threshold, Objective, and As Resilient as Practicable (ARAP)
options.C: Best-case, Worst-case, and Expected
options.D: Required, Desired, and Stretch

correct_answer_text: Threshold, Objective, and As Resilient as Practicable (ARAP)

question: Which characterization BEST captures the difference between a reliability viewpoint and a resilience viewpoint when assessing a system facing disruption?

options.A: Reliability emphasizes recovering required capability after disruption; resilience emphasizes preventing component failure
options.B: Reliability asks "how do we keep components from failing?"; resilience asks "how do we keep delivering required capability under adversity — by avoiding, withstanding, and recovering?"
options.C: Reliability and resilience both target the same metric — mean time between failures
options.D: Resilience replaces reliability requirements once a system is fielded

correct_answer_text: Reliability asks "how do we keep components from failing?"; resilience asks "how do we keep delivering required capability under adversity — by avoiding, withstanding, and recovering?"

question: From Table 3.3, drag each resilience consideration to its category.
categories: Stakeholder Needs and Requirements Definition Process

items.text: Defining the problem space includes identification of adversities and expectations for performance under those adversities.
ConOps, OpsCon, and solution classes consider the ability to avoid, withstand, and recover from the adversities
Evaluation of alterative solution classes consider the ability to deliver required capabilities under adversity
The stakeholder set includes persons who understand potential adversities and stakeholder resilience needs.
items.category: Stakeholder Needs and Requirements Definition Process

items.text: Identifying stakeholder needs identifies expectations for capability under adverse conditions, and degraded/alternate, but useful, modes of operation.
items.category: Stakeholder Needs and Requirements Definition Process

items.text: Operational concept scenarios include resilience scenarios.
items.category: Stakeholder Needs and Requirements Definition Process

items.text: Transforming stakeholder needs to stakeholder requirements includes stakeholder resilience requirements.
items.category: Stakeholder Needs and Requirements Definition Process

items.text: Analysis of stakeholder requirements includes resilience scenarios in the adverse operational environment.
items.category: Stakeholder Needs and Requirements Definition Process

items.text: Resilience is considered in the identification of requirements.
Achieving resilience and other adversity-driven considerations is addressed holistically.
Viewpoints selected support the representation of resilience.
Resilience requirements significantly limit and guide the range of acceptable architectures. It is critical that resilience requirements are mature when used for architecture selection.
Individuals developing candidate architectures are familiar with architectural techniques for achieving resilience.
Individuals developing candidate designs are familiar with design techniques for achieving resilience.
Achieving resilience and the other adversity-driven considerations are addressed holistically.
Risk management is planned to handle risks and opportunities identified by resilience activities.

question: Per SEHB5 Section 3.1.10, Sustainability Engineering is defined as an approach that:

options.A: Maximizes value by providing cost-effective capability over the entire life cycle
options.B: Supports the circular economy over its life
options.C: Reduces the likelihood of harm to people, assets, and the wider environment
options.D: Enables support for the entire life cycle through logistics infrastructure

correct_answer_text: Supports the circular economy over its life

question: According to SEHB5 3.1.10, 'Design for sustainability' is defined as a process that considers which two aspects as key elements in product design to reduce harmful impacts throughout the life cycle?

options.A: Economic and political aspects
options.B: Technical and regulatory aspects
options.C: Environmental and social aspects
options.D: Operational and maintenance aspects

correct_answer_text: Environmental and social aspects

question: Per SEHB5 3.1.10, the circular economy is based on a production and consumption model that involves which of the following sets of activities?

options.A: Extracting, processing, manufacturing, and disposing in a linear flow
options.B: Sharing, reusing, repairing, and recycling existing products and materials
options.C: Outsourcing, offshoring, automating, and standardizing production
options.D: Specifying, validating, certifying, and decommissioning systems

correct_answer_text: Sharing, reusing, repairing, and recycling existing products and materials

question: Per SEHB5 3.1.10, sustainability and disposability goals are noted to be consistent with what international framework adopted by UN Member States in 2015?

options.A: The Kyoto Protocol on greenhouse gas emissions
options.B: The Paris Agreement on climate change
options.C: The 17 Sustainable Development Goals (2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development)
options.D: The ISO 26000 Guidance on Social Responsibility

correct_answer_text: The 17 Sustainable Development Goals (2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development)

question: According to SEHB5 3.1.10, the focus of environmental impact analysis covers the full life cycle of the system. Which stages are explicitly identified as the scope of concern?

options.A: Only the utilization and retirement stages
options.B: Development, production, utilization, support, and retirement stages
options.C: Concept and development stages only, as these drive downstream impacts
options.D: Production and operation stages, since these consume the most energy

correct_answer_text: Development, production, utilization, support, and retirement stages

question: Per SEHB5 3.1.10, during System Architecture Definition and Design Definition, what dual goal is identified for handling residual system elements at end of useful life?

options.A: Minimize disposal cost and maximize regulatory compliance
options.B: Maximize the economic value of residual system elements and minimize the amount of waste materials
options.C: Maximize landfill volume efficiency and minimize toxic emissions
options.D: Maximize remanufacturing throughput and minimize energy use

correct_answer_text: Maximize the economic value of residual system elements and minimize the amount of waste materials

question: SEHB5 3.1.10 identifies 'Design for disassembly' as an important consideration. What is its primary purpose?

options.A: To accelerate factory assembly throughput during production
options.B: To reduce shipping volume by enabling flat-pack transport
options.C: To minimize destructive separation of system elements so material can be reused, remanufactured, or recycled
options.D: To simplify field maintenance by allowing in-place repair of failed components

correct_answer_text: To minimize destructive separation of system elements so material can be reused, remanufactured, or recycled

question: Per SEHB5 3.1.10, which ISO series is cited as 'an excellent resource for methods to analyze and assess industrial operations and their impacts on the environment'?

options.A: ISO 9000 series (Quality Management)
options.B: ISO 14000 series (Environmental Management)
options.C: ISO 15288 series (System Life Cycle Processes)
options.D: ISO 27000 series (Information Security Management)

correct_answer_text: ISO 14000 series (Environmental Management)

question: Per SEHB5 3.1.10, Environmental Product Declarations (EPDs) being developed by ISO are based on what indicator of global environmental impact?

options.A: Water consumption per unit produced
options.B: Hazardous material concentration ratios
options.C: Carbon footprints expressed in carbon emission equivalents
options.D: Total raw material mass throughput

correct_answer_text: Carbon footprints expressed in carbon emission equivalents

question: SEHB5 3.1.10 lists increasingly sophisticated, software-supported methods associated with sustainability analysis. Which of the following is the correct set of these methods?

options.A: FMECA, FTA, RBD, and RCM
options.B: Life cycle assessment (LCA), life cycle impact assessment (LCIA), life cycle optimization (LCO), and life cycle management (LCM)
options.C: COCOMO, COSYSMO, Delphi, and Parametric estimation
options.D: TRIZ, QFD, DFMA, and Six Sigma

correct_answer_text: Life cycle assessment (LCA), life cycle impact assessment (LCIA), life cycle optimization (LCO), and life cycle management (LCM)

question: Per SEHB5 3.1.10, recovery of valuable resources after a product's useful life is described as highly dependent on decisions made when considering which related quality characteristic?

options.A: Reliability and maintainability
options.B: Resilience
options.C: Manufacturability/producibility
options.D: Logistics engineering

correct_answer_text: Manufacturability/producibility

question: Per SEHB5 3.1.10, useful life extensions in a circular closed-loop system are described as relying on which combination of related quality characteristics?

options.A: Affordability and interoperability
options.B: Reliability and maintainability, alongside efficient logistical support, with products designed to be resilient
options.C: Agility and human systems integration
options.D: System safety and system security

correct_answer_text: Reliability and maintainability, alongside efficient logistical support, with products designed to be resilient

question: Per the SE Handbook, which statement BEST captures the definition of System Safety Engineering?

options.A: An approach that guarantees an engineered system is 100% safe from all foreseeable and unforeseeable hazards across its life cycle.
options.B: An approach that reduces the likelihood of harm to people, assets, and the wider environment.
options.C: An approach that protects a system from anomalous and disruptive events, including those in a cyber-contested environment.
options.D: An approach that ensures the system meets functional performance requirements without introducing reliability defects.

correct_answer_text: An approach that reduces the likelihood of harm to people, assets, and the wider environment.

question: According to the handbook, what is the stated GOAL of system safety engineering?

options.A: To eliminate every safety risk from the system before it enters operation.
options.B: To reduce and mitigate hazards of systems to an acceptable level of risk.
options.C: To certify that the system is 100% safe under all operational conditions.
options.D: To shift safety responsibility entirely onto operators and maintainers through procedural controls.

correct_answer_text: To reduce and mitigate hazards of systems to an acceptable level of risk.

question: The handbook characterizes safety as which type of property of the engineered system?

options.A: A static property fixed at design baseline that does not change once requirements are verified.
options.B: The arithmetic sum of system-element-level safety and reliability values.
options.C: An emergent property of the engineered system in its real operational environment.
options.D: A purely regulatory property that depends only on certification authority approval.

correct_answer_text: An emergent property of the engineered system in its real operational environment.

question: The handbook states that regulators typically assess safety risk in terms of which TWO dimensions?

options.A: Cost of mitigation and schedule to implement controls.
options.B: Likelihood of hazards occurring and leading to harm, and severity of the resulting harm.
options.C: Frequency of inspection and number of redundant controls.
options.D: Number of identified hazards and number of mitigations applied.

correct_answer_text: Likelihood of hazards occurring and leading to harm, and severity of the resulting harm.

question: When managing hazards, which option does the handbook identify as the PREFERRED approach?

options.A: Add operational controls and warning labels for operators.
options.B: Provide additional training to operators and maintainers.
options.C: Design changes to eliminate potential hazards.
options.D: Increase the frequency of maintenance inspections.

correct_answer_text: Design changes to eliminate potential hazards.

question: Per the handbook, when a design change to eliminate a hazard is not possible, what is the NEXT most preferred option?

options.A: Issue warning labels and operator training to alert users to the hazard.
options.B: Design mitigations that reduce the likelihood of the hazard occurring.
options.C: Transfer the risk to operators through procedural controls only.
options.D: Accept the hazard as residual risk without further mitigation.

correct_answer_text: Design mitigations that reduce the likelihood of the hazard occurring.

question: When design changes or design mitigations are not possible, the handbook says other means are 'typically employed.' Which set BEST matches those means?

options.A: Marketing disclosures, indemnity clauses, and end-user license agreements.
options.B: Operational controls and limitations, maintenance inspections or activities, warnings via labeling, and training.
options.C: Insurance contracts, regulatory waivers, and post-incident litigation reserves.
options.D: Hardware redundancy, software diversity, and formal verification only.

correct_answer_text: Operational controls and limitations, maintenance inspections or activities, warnings via labeling, and training.

question: The handbook describes 'ALARP' as one perception of acceptably safe. What does ALARP stand for?

options.A: As Low As Reasonably Practicable.
options.B: Acceptable Level of Allowable Risk Profile.
options.C: Assured Limit of Aggregate Risk Probability.
options.D: Audited Loss And Risk Posture.

correct_answer_text: As Low As Reasonably Practicable.

question: Per the handbook, for which class of systems is the 'ALARP, OR the level of hazard of the new system is less than the alternative' framing typically adopted?

options.A: Simple, well-understood, standardized system elements such as electrical cable or bolts.
options.B: Complicated, safety-critical civilian systems such as railway signaling or passenger aircraft.
options.C: Military, medical, or emergency response systems such as artillery or pacemakers.
options.D: Consumer electronics and household appliances.

correct_answer_text: Military, medical, or emergency response systems such as artillery or pacemakers.

question: The handbook says compliance with the necessary product regulations is generally accepted as 'acceptably safe' for which kind of items?

options.A: Passenger aircraft and railway signaling systems.
options.B: Military weapon systems and pacemakers.
options.C: Simple, well-understood, standardized system elements such as electrical cable or bolts.
options.D: Autonomous vehicles and emergency response systems.

correct_answer_text: Simple, well-understood, standardized system elements such as electrical cable or bolts.

question: How does the handbook define a 'near miss'?

options.A: An accident in which the resulting harm was less severe than initially predicted.
options.B: An unmitigated hazardous situation that does occur but does not result in harm.
options.C: A hazard that was identified during analysis but never observed in operation.
options.D: A regulatory finding that resulted in a corrective action plan.

correct_answer_text: An unmitigated hazardous situation that does occur but does not result in harm.

question: Why does the handbook caution against assuming a system is safe simply because no accidents have yet been reported?

options.A: Because reporting is mandatory and any unreported event indicates regulatory non-compliance.
options.B: Because accident statistics are public and always lag actual safety performance by exactly one year.
options.C: Because there may have been near misses, the hazardous functionality may not yet have been used, or the effect/damage has not yet been recognized.
options.D: Because absence of accidents is statistically impossible for any deployed engineered system.

correct_answer_text: Because there may have been near misses, the hazardous functionality may not yet have been used, or the effect/damage has not yet been recognized.

question: The handbook lists four categories of hazard sources. Which set matches those categories?

options.A: Hardware, software, firmware, and human.
options.B: Intrinsic, functional, socio-technical, and management/wider culture.
options.C: Physical, electrical, chemical, and biological.
options.D: Random, systematic, transient, and latent.

correct_answer_text: Intrinsic, functional, socio-technical, and management/wider culture.

question: According to the handbook, which description BEST matches a 'functional hazard'?

options.A: A hazard caused by the material or other design factors of the system elements.
options.B: A hazard resulting from incorrect, unexpected, or undesirable functions or performance of the system.
options.C: A hazard arising from interactions between the physical system and its operators and the wider environment.
options.D: A hazard related to the wider management controls needed to realize and sustain the system.

correct_answer_text: A hazard resulting from incorrect, unexpected, or undesirable functions or performance of the system.

question: Asbestos and flammable substances are used in the handbook to illustrate which category of hazard?

options.A: Functional hazards from undesirable system performance.
options.B: Socio-technical hazards from operator interactions.
options.C: Intrinsic hazards from the material used in system elements.
options.D: Management/cultural hazards from weak governance.

correct_answer_text: Intrinsic hazards from the material used in system elements.

question: The handbook cites the loss of two 737 MAX aircraft as an example illustrating which kind of mistake?

options.A: Failure to specify the MCAS system element as safety critical (a mistake in requirements/design/manufacturing or installation).
options.B: Use of an intrinsically hazardous material such as asbestos.
options.C: Incorrect maintenance of legacy wiring.
options.D: Misuse by inexperienced consumer operators.

correct_answer_text: Failure to specify the MCAS system element as safety critical (a mistake in requirements/design/manufacturing or installation).

question: The Smiler accident is used by the handbook to illustrate which class of hazard?

options.A: An intrinsic material hazard within a system element.
options.B: A misalignment of mental models between operators, maintainers, and designers (operators treated all alarms as false positives).
options.C: A regulatory non-compliance with product safety standards.
options.D: A failure to apply ALARP to a safety-critical civilian system.

correct_answer_text: A misalignment of mental models between operators, maintainers, and designers (operators treated all alarms as false positives).

question: The Piper-Alpha oil platform accident is cited in the handbook as an example of hazards arising from what?

options.A: An intrinsic hazard of the platform's structural material.
options.B: Activities undertaken to design, manufacture, test, and maintain the system (poor change management, maintenance, and contingency planning).
options.C: An inexperienced operator interacting with an icy environment.
options.D: The system creating hazards for the wider SoI through bystander injury.

correct_answer_text: Activities undertaken to design, manufacture, test, and maintain the system (poor change management, maintenance, and contingency planning).

question: The handbook's bull-bars / kangaroo bars example illustrates which kind of hazard?

options.A: An intrinsic material hazard within the vehicle.
options.B: A functional hazard from unintended vehicle behavior.
options.C: The system creating hazards in the wider SoI (reducing driver injury risk but significantly increasing risk to pedestrians).
options.D: A management hazard arising from weak organizational culture.

correct_answer_text: The system creating hazards in the wider SoI (reducing driver injury risk but significantly increasing risk to pedestrians).

question: According to the handbook, the Clapham Junction rail accident was caused by what?

options.A: A failure to properly remove old wiring (incorrect operation or maintenance).
options.B: Misaligned mental models that caused alarms to be overridden.
options.C: Use of an intrinsically hazardous material in a system element.
options.D: A failure to specify a system element as safety critical.

correct_answer_text: A failure to properly remove old wiring (incorrect operation or maintenance).

question: The handbook warns that a hazardous situation can occur WITHOUT a system element failure. Which set of causes does it identify?

options.A: Cyber attack, supply chain compromise, or insider threat.
options.B: A design error, an implementation error, or a misalignment between mental models of designer and operators or maintainers.
options.C: Random hardware failure, software bit-flip, or electromagnetic interference.
options.D: Regulatory change, audit finding, or recertification lapse.

correct_answer_text: A design error, an implementation error, or a misalignment between mental models of designer and operators or maintainers.

question: Regarding the use of statistical methods in safety analysis, the handbook specifically warns that what failure mode can lead to flawed safety decisions?

options.A: Using point estimates instead of confidence intervals.
options.B: Failing to recognize dependencies between hazards when applying statistical methods (accident investigations often reveal such dependencies despite earlier beliefs that causal factors were independent).
options.C: Confusing Bayesian and frequentist interpretations of probability.
options.D: Applying mean-time-between-failures values to non-repairable components.

correct_answer_text: Failing to recognize dependencies between hazards when applying statistical methods (accident investigations often reveal such dependencies despite earlier beliefs that causal factors were independent).

question: The handbook lists activities by which system safety engineering 'seeks to control hazards.' Which activity is NOT listed?

options.A: Understanding the system environment, wider SoI, proposed system, and how it will be used.
options.B: Analyzing the potential safety hazards using a range of techniques.
options.C: Establishing a safety management system to ensure the system remains safe throughout its life cycle.
options.D: Obtaining a binding insurance policy that transfers residual risk to a third-party underwriter.

correct_answer_text: Obtaining a binding insurance policy that transfers residual risk to a third-party underwriter.

question: Per the handbook, the safety management system must ensure that incidents and near misses are reported in what manner?

options.A: Only to the regulator, with internal teams shielded from the data.
options.B: Without fear of retribution, with ongoing monitoring and mitigation creating a learning/improvement cycle.
options.C: Anonymously, with no traceability back to organizational units.
options.D: Quarterly, in aggregate, and only when statistically significant.

correct_answer_text: Without fear of retribution, with ongoing monitoring and mitigation creating a learning/improvement cycle.

question: What does the handbook say about adopting Agile software techniques for safety-critical systems?

options.A: Agile should always be adopted because it accelerates hazard discovery.
options.B: Agile is forbidden by international safety regulations for any safety-critical system.
options.C: Direct adoption of Agile software techniques to safety-critical systems may be problematic; SE practitioners should exercise caution.
options.D: Agile is equivalent to plan-driven approaches for safety-critical systems and the choice is purely organizational preference.

correct_answer_text: Direct adoption of Agile software techniques to safety-critical systems may be problematic; SE practitioners should exercise caution.

question: Regarding the safety case, what does the handbook tell SE practitioners to ensure throughout the system's life?

options.A: That the safety case is locked at design baseline and never revised after certification.
options.B: That the assumptions in the safety case remain valid through life (environment, operators' mental models, use, maintenance, new hazards, expected hazard frequency/severity).
options.C: That the safety case is treated as a confidential commercial document not shared with operators.
options.D: That the safety case is replaced by a security case once the system is fielded.

correct_answer_text: That the assumptions in the safety case remain valid through life (environment, operators' mental models, use, maintenance, new hazards, expected hazard frequency/severity).

question: The handbook says the traditional split between product safety and occupational safety is becoming less clear. Which trend does it cite as a driver?

options.A: Increased outsourcing of regulatory compliance.
options.B: Development becoming more agile, organizations shifting from product to service delivery, and increased use of non-expert operators and maintainers.
options.C: Convergence of safety and security standards into a single global framework.
options.D: Adoption of model-based systems engineering across all industries.

correct_answer_text: Development becoming more agile, organizations shifting from product to service delivery, and increased use of non-expert operators and maintainers.

question: How does the handbook describe the relative importance of people versus processes and tools in safety performance?

options.A: Good processes and tools can fully compensate for poorly qualified people.
options.B: Tools alone determine safety performance because they enforce procedure mechanically.
options.C: Good people may build safe systems despite poor processes and tools, but good processes and tools cannot make up for poorly qualified people.
options.D: Regulator approval is the dominant factor and people are interchangeable once trained.

correct_answer_text: Good people may build safe systems despite poor processes and tools, but good processes and tools cannot make up for poorly qualified people.

question: The handbook cites the UK Air Force acquisition of eight Chinook Mk3 helicopters as an example of what?

options.A: A successful application of ALARP to a military rotorcraft.
options.B: What can happen when safety practitioners are involved too late and organizations are left with systems that cannot be deployed.
options.C: Effective use of operator training as a substitute for design mitigation.
options.D: A near miss that was correctly reported and resolved through the safety management system.

correct_answer_text: What can happen when safety practitioners are involved too late and organizations are left with systems that cannot be deployed.

question: Which two case studies does Section 3.1.11 explicitly point to for illustrations of the importance of system safety?

options.A: Case 2 (Hubble) and Case 3 (FAA NextGen).
options.B: Case 1 (Radiation Therapy - The Therac-25) and Case 5 (Artificial Intelligence in Systems Engineering - Autonomous Vehicles).
options.C: Case 4 (Design for Maintainability - Incubators) and Case 6 (Singapore Water).
options.D: Case 1 (Therac-25) and Case 4 (Incubators).

correct_answer_text: Case 1 (Radiation Therapy - The Therac-25) and Case 5 (Artificial Intelligence in Systems Engineering - Autonomous Vehicles).

question: How does the handbook characterize the relationship between safety as an emergent property and how a system is used, maintained, and managed?

options.A: Use, maintenance, and management have negligible impact compared to inherent design.
options.B: How the system is used, maintained, and managed can have as big an impact on system safety as its inherent design.
options.C: Only inherent design choices made before requirements baseline affect system safety.
options.D: Maintenance dominates safety; operations and management contribute only marginally.

correct_answer_text: How the system is used, maintained, and managed can have as big an impact on system safety as its inherent design.

question: Per the handbook, what is a particularly problematic dynamic for in-service systems with respect to safety monitoring?

options.A: Rapid feedback loops that overwhelm operators with data.
options.B: Slow feedback loops and misaligned mental models, where issues can grow unseen for years before they appear with catastrophic impact.
options.C: Tight integration with business management systems that obscures safety reporting.
options.D: Frequent regulator audits that distract from operational priorities.

correct_answer_text: Slow feedback loops and misaligned mental models, where issues can grow unseen for years before they appear with catastrophic impact.

question: Per the INCOSE SE Handbook 5e formal definition, System Security Engineering is an approach that:

options.A: Eliminates all anomalous and disruptive events from the system before deployment
options.B: Identifies, protects from, detects, responds to, and recovers from anomalous and disruptive events, including those in a cyber contested environment
options.C: Reduces the likelihood of harm to people, assets, and the wider environment from system operation
options.D: Provides the required capability when the system faces adversity by absorbing and adapting to disruption

correct_answer_text: Identifies, protects from, detects, responds to, and recovers from anomalous and disruptive events, including those in a cyber contested environment

question: According to the handbook, what is the PRIMARY focus of System Security Engineering (SSE)?

options.A: Ensuring the system meets its functional performance requirements during normal operation
options.B: Ensuring the system functions under anomalous and disruptive events associated with misuse and malicious behavior
options.C: Ensuring the system minimizes unintentional harm to operators and maintainers
options.D: Ensuring the system can be produced and supported in a cost-effective manner

correct_answer_text: Ensuring the system functions under anomalous and disruptive events associated with misuse and malicious behavior

question: The handbook states that cybersecurity generally refers to which three properties of information assets (the CIA triad)?

options.A: Confidentiality, identification, and authentication
options.B: Compliance, integrity, and accountability
options.C: Confidentiality, integrity, and availability
options.D: Control, isolation, and authorization

correct_answer_text: Confidentiality, integrity, and availability

question: Which of the following BEST distinguishes System Security Engineering from System Safety Engineering as presented in the handbook?

options.A: Security and safety are synonyms used interchangeably across the handbook's QC approaches
options.B: Safety focuses on confidentiality of information, while security focuses on unintentional harm
options.C: Safety reduces the likelihood of harm to people, assets, and the wider environment; security addresses anomalous and disruptive events including those caused by malicious behavior in a cyber contested environment
options.D: Safety addresses only physical hazards while security addresses only software defects

correct_answer_text: Safety reduces the likelihood of harm to people, assets, and the wider environment; security addresses anomalous and disruptive events including those caused by malicious behavior in a cyber contested environment

question: A disruption in the context of SSE may be characterized as either:

options.A: Hardware or software, but never both simultaneously
options.B: Unintentional (misuse) or intentional (malicious) in nature
options.C: Accidental or random, never deliberate
options.D: External only, since internal forces do not constitute SSE threats

correct_answer_text: Unintentional (misuse) or intentional (malicious) in nature

question: According to the handbook, sources of potential anomalous and disruptive events (threats) may emanate from:

options.A: Only external sources such as theft and denial of service attacks
options.B: Only internal forces such as user actions and supporting systems
options.C: External sources (e.g., theft, denial of service attacks, power interruptions) or internal forces (e.g., user actions, supporting systems)
options.D: Only the supply chain prior to system delivery

correct_answer_text: External sources (e.g., theft, denial of service attacks, power interruptions) or internal forces (e.g., user actions, supporting systems)

question: Physical security, as described in section 3.1.12, protects a system from unauthorized access, misuse, or damage caused by physical actions and events. Which of the following BEST captures the handbook's recommended approach?

options.A: A single deterrent system such as a security guard is sufficient for most facilities
options.B: Multiple layers of interdependent systems (e.g., surveillance, intrusion detection, deterrents, guards, barriers, locks, access control)
options.C: Encryption of all data at rest is the primary physical security control
options.D: Physical security is outside the scope of SSE and is handled by facility management only

correct_answer_text: Multiple layers of interdependent systems (e.g., surveillance, intrusion detection, deterrents, guards, barriers, locks, access control)

question: Anti-tampering features in hardware devices are described in the handbook as serving what purpose?

options.A: To increase manufacturing throughput and reduce unit cost
options.B: To detect unauthorized opening or altering of the packaging, either to ensure authenticity or to trigger actions to protect sensitive information
options.C: To improve mean time between failures (MTBF) of the device
options.D: To provide redundant power in the event of a power interruption

correct_answer_text: To detect unauthorized opening or altering of the packaging, either to ensure authenticity or to trigger actions to protect sensitive information

question: Per the handbook, the MOST efficient and effective way to ensure that security is addressed throughout the life cycle is to:

options.A: Address security only at system acceptance through penetration testing
options.B: Treat security as a separate, isolated activity carried out by a dedicated cybersecurity team after design completion
options.C: Integrate and implement systems security using SSE approaches so it becomes part of the overall SE solution, addressed at each stage of the life cycle
options.D: Defer security analysis until operational data reveals exploited vulnerabilities

correct_answer_text: Integrate and implement systems security using SSE approaches so it becomes part of the overall SE solution, addressed at each stage of the life cycle

question: How does the handbook define Trustworthiness in the context of System Security Engineering?

options.A: A measure equivalent to confidentiality of information assets
options.B: A concept that includes privacy, reliability, resilience, safety, and security — therefore worthy of being trusted to fulfill critical requirements
options.C: The probability that a system will operate without failure for a specified period under stated conditions
options.D: A property limited to identity and access management functions

correct_answer_text: A concept that includes privacy, reliability, resilience, safety, and security — therefore worthy of being trusted to fulfill critical requirements

question: The handbook notes that SSE provides complementary engineering capability that extends the notion of trustworthiness to deliver:

options.A: Systems that are guaranteed to be invulnerable to any attack
options.B: Trustworthy secure systems that are less susceptible to the effects of modern adversity such as attacks orchestrated by an intelligent adversary
options.C: Systems that operate without any human user involvement
options.D: Systems certified solely against ISO 9001 quality management standards

correct_answer_text: Trustworthy secure systems that are less susceptible to the effects of modern adversity such as attacks orchestrated by an intelligent adversary

question: Which standards/publications does the handbook reference as the basis for integrating cybersecurity into systems through an SE process?

options.A: ISO 9001 and ISO 14001
options.B: NIST SP 800-160 Vol. 1 (2022) and Vol. 2 (2021), which are based on ISO/IEC/IEEE 15288 (2023) and the SE Handbook
options.C: IEEE 802.11 and IEEE 1471
options.D: MIL-STD-882 and DO-178C

correct_answer_text: NIST SP 800-160 Vol. 1 (2022) and Vol. 2 (2021), which are based on ISO/IEC/IEEE 15288 (2023) and the SE Handbook

question: According to the handbook, what is identified as a MAJOR challenge in managing engineering projects from an SSE perspective?

options.A: The cost of penetration testing tools
options.B: Unclear security roles, responsibilities, and accountability
options.C: The absence of any usable security standards
options.D: The unavailability of cryptographic algorithms

correct_answer_text: Unclear security roles, responsibilities, and accountability

question: Which of the following is listed in the handbook as an example skill/expertise area that SSE practitioners should have?

options.A: Affordability trade-space modeling using CAIV
options.B: Threat assessment, vulnerability assessment, penetration testing, and supply chain security risk assessment
options.C: Failure Mode Effects and Criticality Analysis (FMECA) for hardware reliability
options.D: Reliability-Centered Maintenance (RCM) for preventive maintenance task identification

correct_answer_text: Threat assessment, vulnerability assessment, penetration testing, and supply chain security risk assessment

question: Which case study does the handbook explicitly cite to illustrate the importance of system security in cyber-physical systems?

options.A: Case 1: Radiation Therapy – The Therac-25
options.B: Case 3: Cybersecurity Considerations in Systems Engineering – The Stuxnet Attack on a Cyber Physical System
options.C: Case 4: Design for Maintainability – Incubators
options.D: Case 5: Artificial Intelligence in Systems Engineering – Autonomous Vehicles

correct_answer_text: Case 3: Cybersecurity Considerations in Systems Engineering – The Stuxnet Attack on a Cyber Physical System

question: In the SSE conceptual model, how are 'threat' and 'vulnerability' BEST distinguished as used in the handbook's discussion of security?

options.A: Threats are weaknesses internal to the system; vulnerabilities are external actors that exploit them
options.B: Threats are sources of potential anomalous and disruptive events (internal or external); vulnerabilities are weaknesses in the system that SSE analyzes and mitigates as part of assessing security risks to assets
options.C: Threats and vulnerabilities are synonyms describing the same concept
options.D: Threats represent residual risk; vulnerabilities represent inherent risk

correct_answer_text: Threats are sources of potential anomalous and disruptive events (internal or external); vulnerabilities are weaknesses in the system that SSE analyzes and mitigates as part of assessing security risks to assets

question: The handbook lists Cybersecurity, Information Assurance (IA), Physical Security, and Trustworthiness as representative quality characteristics (QCs) under which QC approach?

options.A: Resilience Engineering
options.B: System Safety Engineering
options.C: System Security Engineering
options.D: Human Systems Integration

correct_answer_text: System Security Engineering

question: Per the handbook, SSE blends which three elements to ensure sufficient protections are available at all times?

options.A: Hardware, software, and firmware
options.B: Technology, management principles and practices, and operational rules
options.C: Encryption, authentication, and authorization
options.D: Detection, deterrence, and prosecution

correct_answer_text: Technology, management principles and practices, and operational rules

question: According to the SE Handbook, how is Loss-Driven Systems Engineering (LDSE) formally defined?

options.A: A safety-engineering technique that quantifies acceptable risk thresholds for hazardous system functions
options.B: The value-adding unification of the Quality Characteristics that address the potential losses associated with developing and using systems
options.C: A reliability-centered methodology that minimizes the probability of system failure during operations
options.D: A management framework for tracking residual risks across the project's risk register

correct_answer_text: The value-adding unification of the Quality Characteristics that address the potential losses associated with developing and using systems

question: Per Section 3.1.13, what gap in conventional SE practice motivates the adoption of Loss-Driven Systems Engineering?

options.A: Conventional SE methodologies overemphasize cost and schedule at the expense of technical performance
options.B: Conventional SE methodologies are largely capability-driven and may not provide integrated attention to the potential losses associated with developing and using systems
options.C: Conventional SE methodologies rely on document-centric artifacts rather than model-based ones
options.D: Conventional SE methodologies fail to address stakeholder needs during the concept stage

correct_answer_text: Conventional SE methodologies are largely capability-driven and may not provide integrated attention to the potential losses associated with developing and using systems

question: Which of the following is identified in Section 3.1.13 as an example of a loss-driven Quality Characteristic?

options.A: Interoperability
options.B: Affordability
options.C: Resilience
options.D: Manufacturability

correct_answer_text: Resilience

question: Section 3.1.13 lists multiple loss-driven Quality Characteristics whose work should be collaborative. Which of the following is the most complete set of examples cited in the handbook?

options.A: Resilience, safety, security, sustainability/disposability, and availability
options.B: Reliability, maintainability, testability, and producibility
options.C: Affordability, agility, flexibility, and interoperability
options.D: Survivability, operability, supportability, and quality assurance

correct_answer_text: Resilience, safety, security, sustainability/disposability, and availability

question: Section 3.1.13 states that work on the loss-driven QCs should be collaborative on several common elements. Which of the following is NOT listed in the handbook as one of those elements?

options.A: The adversities considered
options.B: The weaknesses, defects, flaws, exposures, hazards, and vulnerabilities considered
options.C: The assets and losses considered
options.D: The certification authorities involved

correct_answer_text: The certification authorities involved

question: According to Section 3.1.13, how should SE practitioners handle loss-driven requirements?

options.A: Capture them in a separate loss register maintained by the safety lead
options.B: Defer them until after the system architecture has been baselined
options.C: Elicit, analyze, and capture them as an integrated part of the overall stakeholder and system requirements development
options.D: Delegate them exclusively to the security and safety specialty engineering disciplines

correct_answer_text: Elicit, analyze, and capture them as an integrated part of the overall stakeholder and system requirements development

question: Per Section 3.1.13, how should architectural and design decisions be made with respect to the loss-driven Quality Characteristics?

options.A: Sequentially, one QC at a time, starting with the highest-consequence loss
options.B: Holistically across the loss-driven QC areas
options.C: Independently by each specialty engineering discipline, then reconciled at integration
options.D: Only after operational testing reveals which losses are most likely to occur

correct_answer_text: Holistically across the loss-driven QC areas

question: Section 3.1.13 directs SE practitioners how to handle risks associated with the loss-driven Quality Characteristics. What is the prescribed approach?

options.A: Manage them in QC-specific risk registers owned by each specialty discipline
options.B: Transfer them to the customer through contractual exclusions
options.C: Integrate their management into the project's overall risk management activities
options.D: Track them separately from the project risk register to preserve confidentiality

correct_answer_text: Integrate their management into the project's overall risk management activities

question: Why does Section 3.1.13 emphasize that synergy among the loss-driven QCs (e.g., resilience, safety, security) must be leveraged?

options.A: Because each QC is governed by the same international standard and shares a common verification method
options.B: Because the loss-driven QCs are typically owned by a single specialty engineer
options.C: Because there is significant commonality among them, and they are often considered in isolation - if at all
options.D: Because synergy reduces the total cost of certification more than any other factor

correct_answer_text: Because there is significant commonality among them, and they are often considered in isolation - if at all

question: Per the handbook, which statement is the most precise definition of Modeling within MA&S?

options.A: The process of systematic, reproducible examination to gain insight
options.B: The conception, creation, and refinement of models
options.C: The process of using a model to predict and study system behavior
options.D: Any examination that involves a physical mockup of the system

correct_answer_text: The conception, creation, and refinement of models

question: How does the handbook formally distinguish Analysis from Simulation?

options.A: Analysis is qualitative; Simulation is quantitative
options.B: Analysis is the process of systematic, reproducible examination to gain insight, whereas Simulation is the process of using a model to predict and study behavior; simulation often supports analysis but not all analysis is performed through simulation
options.C: Analysis is performed on digital models; Simulation is performed only on physical mockups
options.D: Analysis is conducted during early life cycle stages; Simulation only during operation

correct_answer_text: Analysis is the process of systematic, reproducible examination to gain insight, whereas Simulation is the process of using a model to predict and study behavior; simulation often supports analysis but not all analysis is performed through simulation

question: According to the handbook, what is the essential artifact of MA&S?

options.A: A signed-off requirements document
options.B: A test report from a hardware prototype
options.C: An explicit model—an idealized representation of one or more aspects of the as-is or to-be SoI
options.D: A configuration management baseline

correct_answer_text: An explicit model—an idealized representation of one or more aspects of the as-is or to-be SoI

question: In Section 3.2.1's terminology, examining a physical model is referred to as which of the following—not a simulation?

options.A: An emulation
options.B: A test
options.C: A digital twin run
options.D: An analysis case

correct_answer_text: A test

question: Per the handbook, what is the term used for grouping selected parameter values to define a particular configuration or situation for analysis or simulation?

options.A: A baseline
options.B: A digital thread
options.C: An experiment (also known as analysis case, load case, or verification case)
options.D: A surrogate

correct_answer_text: An experiment (also known as analysis case, load case, or verification case)

question: Figure 3.8 ('Schematic view of a generic MA&S process') groups the preprocessing activities into which set?

options.A: Build/refine models, build/refine scenario and conditions, and provide system specifications
options.B: Validate models, quantify uncertainties, and organize/visualize results
options.C: Manage MA&S data and quality, perform analysis, and make decisions
options.D: Define purpose and methodology, perform analysis, and postprocess

correct_answer_text: Build/refine models, build/refine scenario and conditions, and provide system specifications

question: In the generic MA&S workflow (Figure 3.8), which activities are part of postprocessing?

options.A: Define purpose, assumptions, and methodology
options.B: Build/refine models and build/refine scenario and conditions
options.C: Organize/visualize results, validate models, and quantify uncertainties
options.D: Provide system specifications and select tools

correct_answer_text: Organize/visualize results, validate models, and quantify uncertainties

question: Which Core Competency in the INCOSE SE Competency Framework does MA&S most directly link to?

options.A: Lifecycle Management
options.B: Concept Definition
options.C: Systems Modeling and Analysis
options.D: Verification and Validation

correct_answer_text: Systems Modeling and Analysis

question: Which life cycle process is highlighted in 3.2.1 as the primary user of descriptive models to ensure the business/mission proposition and problem are correctly understood and shared with stakeholders?

options.A: Business or Mission Analysis (and Stakeholder Needs and Requirements Definition)
options.B: Verification process
options.C: Disposal process
options.D: Maintenance process

correct_answer_text: Business or Mission Analysis (and Stakeholder Needs and Requirements Definition)

question: When verification through testing is impossible or carries prohibitive cost, how does the handbook say verification of the SoI may be done?

options.A: By deferring it to the operational phase
options.B: By analysis or simulation using high fidelity digital models
options.C: By relying solely on stakeholder acceptance
options.D: By substituting a physical mockup test only

correct_answer_text: By analysis or simulation using high fidelity digital models

question: In the Maintenance process, models that reflect the 'as maintained' state of deployed systems are used to develop what?

options.A: Digital twins, possibly for individual deployed systems
options.B: Surrogate models for design exploration
options.C: Black box interface specifications
options.D: Production factory CAD models

correct_answer_text: Digital twins, possibly for individual deployed systems

question: Figure 3.9 ('System development with early, iterative V&V and integration via MA&S') primarily illustrates what concept?

options.A: A document-centric waterfall life cycle
options.B: Model-based iterations between development processes that enable early and iterative V&V and integration
options.C: A hub-and-spokes pattern for tool interoperability
options.D: The taxonomy of physical versus digital models

correct_answer_text: Model-based iterations between development processes that enable early and iterative V&V and integration

question: Which of the following is explicitly listed in 3.2.1 as a cross-cutting use of MA&S (not tied to a specific life cycle process)?

options.A: Manufacturing readiness assessment
options.B: Earned value management
options.C: Automated mapping and transformation of digital models
options.D: Source selection for suppliers

correct_answer_text: Automated mapping and transformation of digital models

question: The handbook states that one of the most important advantages of a model-centric over a document-centric approach is what?

options.A: Lower licensing cost of MA&S tools
options.B: Separation of representation from presentation, maintaining a single 'authoritative source of truth' that generates correct-by-construction views
options.C: Elimination of the need for V&V
options.D: Reduction in stakeholder involvement

correct_answer_text: Separation of representation from presentation, maintaining a single 'authoritative source of truth' that generates correct-by-construction views

question: Per 3.2.1, what is the primary benefit of performing early V&V using MA&S?

options.A: It eliminates the need for stakeholder requirements
options.B: It guarantees zero defects at delivery
options.C: It catches issues early when mitigation is affordable and scope change is still feasible, helping avoid cost and schedule overruns
options.D: It removes the need for any physical testing later in the life cycle

correct_answer_text: It catches issues early when mitigation is affordable and scope change is still feasible, helping avoid cost and schedule overruns

question: In Figure 3.10 (Illustrative model taxonomy), what is the FIRST classification distinction drawn for models?

options.A: Quantitative versus geometric
options.B: Physical versus digital
options.C: Formal versus informal
options.D: Logical versus surrogate

correct_answer_text: Physical versus digital

question: Per the taxonomy in 3.2.1, after distinguishing physical from digital, digital models are next classified along which axis?

options.A: Static versus dynamic
options.B: Open-source versus proprietary
options.C: Informal versus formal
options.D: Discrete versus continuous

correct_answer_text: Informal versus formal

question: How does 3.2.1 define a 'formal model'?

options.A: Any model approved by a configuration control board
options.B: A model expressed in a machine-readable language with explicitly defined semantics (textual and/or graphical), with only one way of interpretation
options.C: A model that has been validated against real-world test data
options.D: A model that uses only mathematical equations

correct_answer_text: A model expressed in a machine-readable language with explicitly defined semantics (textual and/or graphical), with only one way of interpretation

question: Which of the following is identified in 3.2.1 as another name for a logical model?

options.A: Surrogate or reduced model
options.B: Geometric or spatial model
options.C: Descriptive or conceptual model
options.D: Quantitative or mathematical model

correct_answer_text: Descriptive or conceptual model

question: Per the handbook, what is a surrogate model?

options.A: A scaled-down physical mockup substituted for the SoI
options.B: A reduced model derived from a higher-fidelity, more detailed model via a data-driven, typically automated, transformation
options.C: A model owned by a supplier in lieu of the customer's model
options.D: A geometric model used for spatial visualization

correct_answer_text: A reduced model derived from a higher-fidelity, more detailed model via a data-driven, typically automated, transformation

question: Per 3.2.1, what does model 'fidelity' indicate?

options.A: Which aspects of the SoI and its environment(s) are represented and to what extent
options.B: The amount of visible detail captured (depth of decomposition and details of elements)
options.C: How accurately the model represents the real-world system, including computational precision and discretization scheme where applicable
options.D: The tool vendor's commitment to long-term support

correct_answer_text: How accurately the model represents the real-world system, including computational precision and discretization scheme where applicable

question: Which characteristic in 3.2.1 describes the amount of visible detail captured in a model, in terms of depth of system decomposition and level of detail of individual elements?

options.A: Model breadth
options.B: Model granularity
options.C: Model fidelity
options.D: Model interoperability

correct_answer_text: Model granularity

question: Per the handbook, what does model 'breadth' reflect?

options.A: How accurately the model represents the real-world system
options.B: What aspects of the SoI—and possibly its (actual or intended) environment(s)—are represented, and to what extent
options.C: The granularity of system decomposition
options.D: The number of stakeholders that can access the model

correct_answer_text: What aspects of the SoI—and possibly its (actual or intended) environment(s)—are represented, and to what extent

question: In 3.2.1's treatment of model interoperability, which of the following is NOT one of the main categories of standards listed for supporting MA&S?

options.A: Standardized data exchange file
options.B: Modeling language
options.C: Application programming interface (API)
options.D: Funding instrument

correct_answer_text: Funding instrument

question: Per 3.2.1, a general-purpose modeling language typically does not prescribe a methodology. What does the handbook say a methodology adds?

options.A: Guidance on organizing MA&S over a system life cycle, structuring model artifacts, stages, milestones, proven patterns, checklists, and good practices
options.B: A royalty-free open data exchange format
options.C: A guarantee that models can be executed on HPC
options.D: An automated way to generate code from any model

correct_answer_text: Guidance on organizing MA&S over a system life cycle, structuring model artifacts, stages, milestones, proven patterns, checklists, and good practices

question: Per 3.2.1, for further details on methodology in MA&S the reader is referred to which other section of the handbook?

options.A: Section 2.3.5.6 (System Analysis)
options.B: Section 4.2.1 (MBSE) and the OMG MBSE Wiki
options.C: Section 3.2.2 (Prototyping)
options.D: Section 3.1.1 (Quality Characteristics)

correct_answer_text: Section 4.2.1 (MBSE) and the OMG MBSE Wiki

question: The handbook warns that the quality of a model should NOT be confused with what?

options.A: The quality of the modeling tool
options.B: The quality of the design that the model represents
options.C: The skill of the analyst
options.D: The credibility of the simulation infrastructure
