options.B: Apply it verbatim with no tailoring, to preserve neutrality
options.C: Take care to ensure that the defense focus is reasserted upon application
options.D: Replace its processes with those of ISO 9001 quality management

correct_answer_text: Take care to ensure that the defense focus is reasserted upon application

question: Which standard does Section 4.4.4 cite as an example of a specific implementation of ISO/IEC/IEEE 15288 for US Department of Defense projects, providing the basis for selection, negotiation, agreement, and performance of SE activities?

options.A: MIL-STD-882
options.D: MIL-HDBK-61

question: According to Section 4.4.4, with increasing emphasis on networks and capabilities, defense organizations of many countries are recognizing the criticality of which performance dimension?

options.A: Single-platform peak performance benchmarks
options.B: End-to-end System of Systems (SoS) performance and increasing focus on integration to deliver capabilities
options.C: Per-subsystem unit cost reduction independent of integration
options.D: Vendor-proprietary closed-architecture lock-in for security

correct_answer_text: End-to-end System of Systems (SoS) performance and increasing focus on integration to deliver capabilities

question: According to Section 4.4.5, what scope of projects does the infrastructure domain address as discussed in the SE Handbook?

options.A: Mass-produced consumer products manufactured in centralized factories
options.B: Physical capital projects including public works, transport, complex buildings, and industrial facilities
options.C: Software-only enterprise IT systems for government agencies
options.D: Defense weapon platforms and command-and-control systems

correct_answer_text: Physical capital projects including public works, transport, complex buildings, and industrial facilities

question: Per Section 4.4.5, where within infrastructure projects are SE practices typically MOST developed?

options.A: In civil structural design and earthworks construction
options.B: In procurement and contract administration
options.C: In high-technology system elements that involve software development, control systems, system security, or system safety
options.D: In landscape architecture and beautification design

correct_answer_text: In high-technology system elements that involve software development, control systems, system security, or system safety

question: How does Section 4.4.5 characterize the typical infrastructure project approach to requirements, architecture, and interfaces?

options.A: Highly formalized requirements decomposition with rigorous interface control documents
options.B: Projects tend to define the high-level design solution without requirements decomposition, allocation, or interface identification; architectures, traceability, and relationships are often implied rather than specified
options.C: Requirements and architecture are fully model-based and machine-traceable across the life cycle
options.D: Requirements are derived strictly from prototype testing before construction begins

correct_answer_text: Projects tend to define the high-level design solution without requirements decomposition, allocation, or interface identification; architectures, traceability, and relationships are often implied rather than specified

question: Which of the following best distinguishes infrastructure projects from manufacturing and production, per Section 4.4.5?

options.A: Infrastructure projects rely on highly standardized prototypes built in factories
options.B: Infrastructure projects focus on unique, large physical systems where construction takes place on site rather than in a factory
options.C: Infrastructure projects have short, well-bounded life cycles measured in months
options.D: Infrastructure projects use identical, repeatable designs across all geographies

correct_answer_text: Infrastructure projects focus on unique, large physical systems where construction takes place on site rather than in a factory

question: Section 4.4.5 lists several distinguishing characteristics of infrastructure projects. Which set best matches the handbook's description?

options.A: Tightly defined boundaries, stable architectures, single-phase implementation, and short asset life cycles
options.B: Loosely defined boundaries, evolving system architectures, multiphase implementation efforts which can exceed a few decades, and multiple-decade asset life cycles
options.C: Closed system boundaries, fixed requirements, factory-based fabrication, and decadal warranties
options.D: Standardized prototypes, controllable external interfaces, and single-stakeholder governance

correct_answer_text: Loosely defined boundaries, evolving system architectures, multiphase implementation efforts which can exceed a few decades, and multiple-decade asset life cycles

question: In Table 4.7 (Infrastructure and SE definition correlation), which infrastructure terms correspond to the SE term 'Acquirer'?

options.A: Design Consultant or Contractor
options.B: Project need or Business case
options.C: Owner or Agency
options.D: Context diagram or Schematics

correct_answer_text: Owner or Agency

question: Per Table 4.7, the SE term 'System architecture' is correlated with which infrastructure-domain terms?

options.A: Versioning
options.B: Context diagram; Schematics; Process and Instrumentation Diagrams
options.C: Design Criteria; Scope of Work; or Specifications
options.D: Construction Inspection; Quality Control/Quality Assurance

correct_answer_text: Context diagram; Schematics; Process and Instrumentation Diagrams

question: According to Table 4.7, the SE term 'Validation' typically corresponds to which infrastructure practices?

options.A: Design Review; Quality Control (QC)/Quality Assurance (QA)
options.B: Construction Inspection; Quality Control (QC)/Quality Assurance (QA)
options.C: Versioning and change accounting
options.D: Milestone entry and exit criteria definition

correct_answer_text: Construction Inspection; Quality Control (QC)/Quality Assurance (QA)

question: Per Table 4.7, which SE term is correlated with the infrastructure term 'Versioning,' with the recommendation to apply configuration identification, change management, status accounting, and configuration audit per ISO 10007 (2017)?

options.A: Decision gate
options.B: Configuration control
options.C: Performance requirement
options.D: Life cycle

correct_answer_text: Configuration control

question: Per Table 4.7, which SE term corresponds to the infrastructure usage 'Milestone,' for which the recommendation is to clearly define entry and exit criteria?

options.A: Verification
options.B: Decision gate
options.C: Acquisition
options.D: System architecture

correct_answer_text: Decision gate

question: Table 4.7's recommendation for the 'Requirements' row notes infrastructure terms such as Design Criteria, Scope of Work, or Specifications. What does the table recommend integrating into the design criteria?

options.A: Only construction sequencing and crane logistics
options.B: Marketing strategy and brand identity considerations
options.C: Full life cycle considerations, including operations, maintenance, and disposal/replacement planning
options.D: Prototype testing protocols prior to site mobilization

correct_answer_text: Full life cycle considerations, including operations, maintenance, and disposal/replacement planning

question: Per Section 4.4.5, what overall benefit can infrastructure owners gain by applying SE?

options.A: Elimination of all regulatory review steps
options.B: Systematic, formal, verifiable connections between the business needs and the final product
options.C: Replacement of on-site construction with factory prototyping
options.D: Removal of the need for stakeholder engagement

correct_answer_text: Systematic, formal, verifiable connections between the business needs and the final product

question: According to the SE Handbook, how mature is the application of systems engineering within the Oil and Gas (O&G) domain compared to other sectors of similar complexity?

options.A: O&G has been an early adopter of SE, with widespread mature implementation across the supply chain for more than 50 years.
options.B: The emergence of SE within O&G is relatively new compared to other sectors of similar complexity, with most applications occurring within the past decade at varying levels of implementation.
options.C: SE has been fully institutionalized across all upstream, midstream, and downstream segments since the 1980s.
options.D: O&G has explicitly rejected SE practices in favor of pure document-centric stage-gate approaches and shows no signs of adoption.

correct_answer_text: The emergence of SE within O&G is relatively new compared to other sectors of similar complexity, with most applications occurring within the past decade at varying levels of implementation.

question: Per Section 4.4.6, which forces have motivated the traditionally risk-averse Oil and Gas industry to adapt to, and in some cases drive, change?

options.A: Strict ISO 9001 enforcement and antitrust litigation.
options.B: Mandatory adoption of agile software development methods by regulators.
options.C: Fluctuation in oil prices, new systems with increasing complexity, and efforts to reduce greenhouse gas emissions.
options.D: A global shortage of petroleum engineers and a collapse of midstream pipeline infrastructure.

correct_answer_text: Fluctuation in oil prices, new systems with increasing complexity, and efforts to reduce greenhouse gas emissions.

question: According to the SE Handbook, in which SE area has the greatest need been identified within the Oil and Gas domain?

options.A: Disposal and decommissioning process design.
options.B: Human systems integration and operator training.
options.C: Manufacturing automation and production line layout.
options.D: System requirements definition and requirements management.

correct_answer_text: System requirements definition and requirements management.

question: Per Section 4.4.6, with which industry body did INCOSE cooperate on 2017-2018 trials to improve the quality of requirements writing and clearly separate instruction, information, and verification within the O&G domain?

options.A: American Petroleum Institute (API).
options.B: Society of Petroleum Engineers (SPE).
options.C: International Maritime Organization (IMO).
options.D: American Society of Mechanical Engineers (ASME).

correct_answer_text: American Petroleum Institute (API).

question: According to Section 4.4.6, what direction are O&G industry leaders moving with respect to requirements as part of their domain-wide focus on digitalization?

options.A: Outsourcing all requirements work to third-party regulators.
options.B: Abandoning written requirements in favor of verbal tribal knowledge.
options.C: Switching, or planning to switch, to data-centric requirement sets.
options.D: Returning to purely document-centric specifications to ensure auditability.

correct_answer_text: Switching, or planning to switch, to data-centric requirement sets.

question: Beyond requirements, which additional SE practices are noted in Section 4.4.6 as being introduced in the O&G domain?

options.A: Exclusive reliance on Scrum sprints to replace stage-gate reviews.
options.B: Developing system architectures, systematically defining interfaces, and using systems thinking tools (e.g., context diagrams and functional trees) as a foundation for functional modeling.
options.C: Eliminating verification and validation in favor of field-only acceptance testing.
options.D: Replacing engineering reviews with marketing-driven design freezes.

correct_answer_text: Developing system architectures, systematically defining interfaces, and using systems thinking tools (e.g., context diagrams and functional trees) as a foundation for functional modeling.

question: Per Section 4.4.6, what benefit follows from companies incorporating digital design data across disciplines and across the life cycle, combined with digitalization of requirements?

options.A: Elimination of the need for any supplier-acquirer collaboration.
options.B: Auto-creation of specifications and test plans.
options.C: Complete removal of configuration management activities.
options.D: Replacement of system architects with AI-only design teams.

correct_answer_text: Auto-creation of specifications and test plans.

question: According to Section 4.4.6, why is it difficult to evaluate the entire Oil and Gas domain as a single uniform target for SE adoption?

options.A: Because all O&G companies use identical processes, masking adoption gaps.
options.B: Because O&G is a single vertically integrated company globally with no supply chain segmentation.
options.C: Because the long and complicated supply chain includes diverse and segmented companies across the globe, and the domain is not solely crude oil or natural gas (many companies are shifting toward renewable, net-zero solutions).
options.D: Because INCOSE prohibits comparative studies of fossil-based industries.

correct_answer_text: Because the long and complicated supply chain includes diverse and segmented companies across the globe, and the domain is not solely crude oil or natural gas (many companies are shifting toward renewable, net-zero solutions).

question: Per Section 4.4.6, why does SE in O&G tend to be implemented only in pockets that do not always intersect?

options.A: Because most O&G companies follow a well-established and practiced sequential (waterfall) stage-gate process, so SE is generally only implemented where a clear case for change is needed or when all other approaches have been exhausted.
options.B: Because INCOSE limits SE practitioners to one project per company.
options.C: Because regulatory authorities forbid integrated SE in upstream operations.
options.D: Because O&G companies have already achieved full digital twin maturity, making further SE unnecessary.

correct_answer_text: Because most O&G companies follow a well-established and practiced sequential (waterfall) stage-gate process, so SE is generally only implemented where a clear case for change is needed or when all other approaches have been exhausted.

question: According to Section 4.4.6, in what part of the project life cycle is SE in O&G continuing to gain traction and show value most clearly?

options.A: Only during decommissioning and waste storage of legacy assets.
options.B: Only in post-delivery operations and maintenance contracts.
options.C: Only in regulatory compliance audits after first oil.
options.D: Early in new product development projects, where introducing SE principles and methods early lets project teams see the benefits and embrace changes to traditional ways of working.

correct_answer_text: Early in new product development projects, where introducing SE principles and methods early lets project teams see the benefits and embrace changes to traditional ways of working.

question: According to Section 4.4.7, the complex set of requirements driving the early twenty-first-century global energy system stems most directly from which combination of influences?

options.A: ISO 26262 functional safety, automotive electrification mandates, and consumer demand for EVs
options.B: The Paris Agreement, the UN Sustainable Development Goals, and reductions in greenhouse gas emissions
options.C: OPEC production quotas, national security strategies, and shale gas economics
options.D: Moore's Law, semiconductor supply chains, and broadband deployment

correct_answer_text: The Paris Agreement, the UN Sustainable Development Goals, and reductions in greenhouse gas emissions

question: Per Section 4.4.7, how mature is the application of SE in the power and energy sector during the first quarter of the twenty-first century relative to other sectors?

options.A: More mature than defense, space, and transportation due to long asset life cycles
options.B: On par with biomedical and healthcare systems
options.C: Largely immature compared with more established sectors such as defense, space, and transportation
options.D: Fully mature, with SE embedded as the default practice across all incumbents

correct_answer_text: Largely immature compared with more established sectors such as defense, space, and transportation

question: Section 4.4.7 cites distributed energy resources as a real-world illustration of which abstract SE concept?

options.A: A system-of-systems (SoS)
options.B: A product line
options.C: A greenfield clean-sheet system
options.D: A COTS-based system

correct_answer_text: A system-of-systems (SoS)

question: In the architecture and design discussion of Section 4.4.7, which set of assets is identified as belonging to the UPSTREAM portion of the energy supply chain infrastructure?

options.A: Retail outlets, local area networks, and domestic microgeneration
options.B: Large-scale storage, energy vector processing, and transmission and distribution networks
options.C: Solar plant, nuclear, and wind farms
options.D: Private storage, smart meters, and demand-side response devices

correct_answer_text: Solar plant, nuclear, and wind farms

question: Section 4.4.7 lists examples of mid-stream energy supply-chain infrastructure. Which of the following best matches that mid-stream category?

options.A: Solar plants and wind farms
options.B: Large-scale storage, energy vector processing, and transmission and distribution networks
options.C: Retail outlets and domestic microgeneration
options.D: Geo-political negotiations and energy policy formation

correct_answer_text: Large-scale storage, energy vector processing, and transmission and distribution networks

question: Section 4.4.7 highlights that portfolio management in power and energy provides the platform for joined-up roadmaps and communication channels enabling stakeholder acceptance of emerging paradigms. Which set of paradigms is explicitly named?

options.A: Smart grids, district heat networks, renewable technologies, demand-side response, and electric vehicles
options.B: Blockchain trading, hydrogen-only economies, and fusion-only generation
options.C: Coal modernization, oil refining capacity expansion, and pipeline reinforcement
options.D: 5G rollout, AI chip fabrication, and quantum communications

correct_answer_text: Smart grids, district heat networks, renewable technologies, demand-side response, and electric vehicles

question: According to Section 4.4.7, what is the typical life cycle length of power and energy systems (excluding extreme nuclear end-of-life cases)?

options.A: 5-10 years
options.B: 15-25 years
options.C: 30-100 years
options.D: 150-300 years

correct_answer_text: 30-100 years

question: Section 4.4.7 states that power and energy systems must maintain a constant state of equilibrium. How is that equilibrium characterized?

options.A: Generation balanced against export revenues
options.B: Demand balanced by supply, augmented by flexibility solutions such as demand-side response and storage
options.C: Carbon emissions offset entirely by carbon capture
options.D: Reserve margin balanced against regulatory penalties

correct_answer_text: Demand balanced by supply, augmented by flexibility solutions such as demand-side response and storage

question: Section 4.4.7 identifies four overarching goals that energy policy, as an enabling system, should help deliver. Which set names those goals?

options.A: Decarbonization, digitalization, decentralization, and democratization
options.B: Deregulation, diversification, disinflation, and decoupling
options.C: Privatization, globalization, modernization, and standardization
options.D: Electrification, automation, monetization, and securitization

correct_answer_text: Decarbonization, digitalization, decentralization, and democratization

question: Per Section 4.4.7, adapting the mental models and behaviors of system users is crucial to the energy transition. Which approach does the handbook recommend to complement the rigor of SE processes and provide feedback mechanisms influencing micro-behavior while preserving macro-stability?

options.A: Purely market-based incentives with no behavioral consideration
options.B: Mandatory regulation enforced via penalty regimes
options.C: Social sciences, systems science, and systems thinking applied through Human Systems Integration (HSI)
options.D: Outsourcing user engagement to advertising agencies

correct_answer_text: Social sciences, systems science, and systems thinking applied through Human Systems Integration (HSI)

question: Per Section 4.4.8, which characteristic most directly drives the SE risk posture for space systems and the emphasis on first-time success?

options.A: The dominance of agile software development over hardware development
options.B: The relatively high cost of deploying assets into earth orbit or beyond, combined with very limited post-deployment maintenance options
options.C: The use of clean-sheet (greenfield) architectures for every new mission
options.D: The requirement to certify all space systems under ISO/IEC/IEEE 15288 alone

correct_answer_text: The relatively high cost of deploying assets into earth orbit or beyond, combined with very limited post-deployment maintenance options

question: According to Section 4.4.8, the space domain has evolved into three main areas of interest. Which list correctly identifies them?

options.A: Civil, Commercial, and National Security Space
options.B: Civil, Defense, and Scientific Space
options.C: Government, Private, and International Space
options.D: Manned, Unmanned, and Robotic Space

correct_answer_text: Civil, Commercial, and National Security Space

question: Per Section 4.4.8, what does the handbook identify as the key emphases of SE in the space domain?

options.A: Rapid prototyping, MVP delivery, and continuous deployment
options.B: Cost minimization, mass production, and consumer marketing
options.C: Integration, verification (including testing), and validation of highly reliable, well-characterized systems
options.D: Outsourcing, supply chain optimization, and just-in-time manufacturing

correct_answer_text: Integration, verification (including testing), and validation of highly reliable, well-characterized systems

question: According to Section 4.4.8, which life cycle model approach is typically favored for Civil space systems acquired by government agencies?

options.A: Lean startup and continuous delivery
options.B: Sequential approaches such as the SE Vee model or, in some cases, the waterfall model
options.C: Scrum and SAFe scaled agile frameworks
options.D: DevSecOps with biweekly increments

correct_answer_text: Sequential approaches such as the SE Vee model or, in some cases, the waterfall model

question: Per Section 4.4.8, why are Commercial space systems more amenable to incremental and evolutionary life cycle approaches than Civil space systems?

options.A: They are not subject to any standards or regulatory oversight
options.B: They use only COTS components and therefore have no integration risk
options.C: They strive for profitability (cost and schedule), allowing faster deployment and rapid experience gain for later product iterations
options.D: They operate exclusively in low-risk orbital regimes

correct_answer_text: They strive for profitability (cost and schedule), allowing faster deployment and rapid experience gain for later product iterations

question: According to Section 4.4.8, how does National Security Space typically compare with Civil space regarding technology insertion?

options.A: National Security Space strictly forbids any new technology insertion
options.B: National Security Space is typically more tolerant of accepting risk from the injection of new technologies
options.C: National Security Space relies solely on agile methods for technology insertion
options.D: National Security Space avoids the Vee and waterfall models entirely

correct_answer_text: National Security Space is typically more tolerant of accepting risk from the injection of new technologies

question: Per Section 4.4.8, which European initiative was established to develop a coherent, single set of user-friendly standards for use in all European space activities?

options.A: European Space Agency Quality Management Initiative (ESA-QMI)
options.B: European Cooperation for Space Standardization (ECSS)
options.C: Euro-Asian Council for Standardization, Metrology and Certification
options.D: European Aerospace Standards Consortium (EASC)

correct_answer_text: European Cooperation for Space Standardization (ECSS)

question: According to Section 4.4.8, why have Military ("Mil") Standards in the United States been readily adopted by both Civil and Commercial space?

options.A: They are mandated by international treaty for all space activities
options.B: They are the only standards permitted by the FAA for space flight
options.C: Primarily due to the need for high reliability and survivability in a hostile environment
options.D: They are the cheapest standards to implement

correct_answer_text: Primarily due to the need for high reliability and survivability in a hostile environment

question: Per Section 4.4.8, which set of standards is identified as JAXA's library of engineering requirements and guidelines for space systems?

options.A: GOST
options.B: JERG (JAXA Engineering Requirement, Guideline)
options.C: ECSS-J series
options.D: MIL-STD-JP

correct_answer_text: JERG (JAXA Engineering Requirement, Guideline)

question: According to Section 4.4.8, what additional challenge in the space domain arises when humans are integrated into the system?

options.A: Eliminating the need for verification and validation activities
options.B: Removing the requirement for international standards compliance
options.C: Incorporating design features and capabilities that accommodate human interaction to enhance overall safety and mission success, including safe crew recovery from hazardous situations
options.D: Replacing sequential life cycle models with purely agile approaches

correct_answer_text: Incorporating design features and capabilities that accommodate human interaction to enhance overall safety and mission success, including safe crew recovery from hazardous situations

question: Per Section 4.4.8, which scope best describes what space systems may consist of?

options.A: Only spacecraft and their on-board payloads
options.B: Spacecraft (and associated payloads and instruments), mission package(s), ground stations, data links between spacecraft and ground, launch systems, and directly related supporting infrastructure
options.C: Only the launch vehicle and ground stations
options.D: Only crewed spacecraft and life support systems

correct_answer_text: Spacecraft (and associated payloads and instruments), mission package(s), ground stations, data links between spacecraft and ground, launch systems, and directly related supporting infrastructure

question: Per Section 4.4.8, which statement best reflects the role of risk management in space SE?

options.A: Risk management is deferred to the operations stage to avoid disrupting development
options.B: Risk management is key in determining when to incorporate new technologies and how to react to changing requirements through multiyear developments and programmatic challenges
options.C: Risk management is replaced by reliance on COTS products in the commercial sector
options.D: Risk management is limited to schedule risk only because cost is bounded by the contract

correct_answer_text: Risk management is key in determining when to incorporate new technologies and how to react to changing requirements through multiyear developments and programmatic challenges

question: Per the SE Handbook, how are telecommunication systems primarily distinguished from postal systems?

options.A: They have a single owner, whereas postal systems have multiple owners
options.B: They transfer information in the form of electronic media rather than transporting packages or handwritten letters
options.C: They operate exclusively over wireless links rather than physical routes
options.D: They are governed solely by national regulators rather than international standards bodies

correct_answer_text: They transfer information in the form of electronic media rather than transporting packages or handwritten letters

question: According to the SE Handbook, which statement best characterizes the role of telecommunication systems relative to other modern systems?

options.A: They are isolated systems with limited dependence from other domains
options.B: They are enablers for other services, with almost all modern systems either using or containing telecommunication technologies
options.C: They are primarily standalone broadcast systems with few embedded uses
options.D: They are replacing other domains rather than enabling them

correct_answer_text: They are enablers for other services, with almost all modern systems either using or containing telecommunication technologies

question: Per the SE Handbook, why do telecommunication systems such as the internet and the public switched telephone network depend on collaboration in international telecommunication standards bodies?

options.A: Because they are wholly owned by a single multinational carrier
options.B: Because regulators mandate a single proprietary architecture worldwide
options.C: Because they have no single owner, so their design must be coordinated across owners
options.D: Because COTS vendors refuse to integrate without uniform government control

correct_answer_text: Because they have no single owner, so their design must be coordinated across owners

question: Which of the following is listed in the SE Handbook as a typical characteristic of telecommunication systems?

options.A: Single-party ownership with centralized management
options.B: A large, frequently changing set of core functions
options.C: Strong interdependence between nodes, where failures in one node may isolate others unless anticipated in design
options.D: Geographically concentrated deployment at a single site

correct_answer_text: Strong interdependence between nodes, where failures in one node may isolate others unless anticipated in design

question: How does the SE Handbook describe the functional character of telecommunication systems across their constituent system elements?

options.A: A large and rapidly growing set of functions distributed across many disparate elements
options.B: A small stable set of functions allocated across system elements to achieve the common purpose of enabling communication
options.C: A unique set of functions implemented in each constituent system without allocation
options.D: Functions that are tightly coupled to a single vendor's product line

correct_answer_text: A small stable set of functions allocated across system elements to achieve the common purpose of enabling communication

question: Per the SE Handbook, which life cycle characteristic typically applies to the multiple constituent systems that make up telecommunication systems?

options.A: Shared, synchronized life cycles managed by a single owner
options.B: Independent life cycles that continuously evolve over many decades
options.C: Short, fixed life cycles aligned to a single product release
options.D: Identical life cycles dictated by a single international standard

correct_answer_text: Independent life cycles that continuously evolve over many decades

question: According to the SE Handbook, which set of technologies is identified as underpinning telecommunication systems?

options.A: Only cellular networks and Wi-Fi
options.B: Only satellite communication and microwave links
options.C: Satellite communication, cellular networks, land mobile radio, microwave, radio, television, Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, and global positioning systems
options.D: Exclusively wired carrier networks owned by internet service providers

correct_answer_text: Satellite communication, cellular networks, land mobile radio, microwave, radio, television, Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, and global positioning systems

question: Per the SE Handbook, which scope of networks and applications is included under telecommunications?

options.A: Only carrier-owned voice networks
options.B: Only broadcast radio and television networks
options.C: Communication networks owned by carriers, ISPs, government, and other enterprises, plus broadcast networks and over-the-top service provider applications
options.D: Only over-the-top messaging and social media applications

correct_answer_text: Communication networks owned by carriers, ISPs, government, and other enterprises, plus broadcast networks and over-the-top service provider applications

question: Per the SE Handbook, the telecommunication transport network may be dedicated for a specific purpose or shared for multiple services; many of these communication networks are considered to be what?

options.A: Optional commercial conveniences with limited public impact
options.B: National critical infrastructure
options.C: Purely research-oriented testbeds
options.D: Closed proprietary networks exempt from public regulation

correct_answer_text: National critical infrastructure

question: According to the SE Handbook, which trend does the section emphasize regarding the nature of telecommunication systems?

options.A: They are becoming less software-intensive over time
options.B: They are increasingly software-intensive systems
options.C: They are increasingly hardware-only systems with fewer applications
options.D: They are converging on a single proprietary hardware platform

correct_answer_text: They are increasingly software-intensive systems

question: According to Section 4.4.10, what is the typical system life cycle range for ground transportation assets such as highways, buses, mass transit, and rail?

options.A: 5 to 15 years
options.B: 10 to 25 years
options.C: 25 to 100+ years
options.D: 100 to 250 years

correct_answer_text: 25 to 100+ years

question: Section 4.4.10 characterizes ground transportation systems as 'invariably within a SoS on an operational level.' Which combination best reflects the constituent enterprises identified as making up this emerging SE practice area?

options.A: Aerospace primes, defense contractors, and certification bodies
options.B: Transit authorities, railroad operators, rolling stock manufacturers, and civil engineering construction firms
options.C: Software vendors, cloud providers, and standards organizations
options.D: Automotive OEMs, tier-1 suppliers, and ride-share platforms only

correct_answer_text: Transit authorities, railroad operators, rolling stock manufacturers, and civil engineering construction firms

question: Per Section 4.4.10, what societal/cultural trends are cited as being well aligned with the more advanced regional SoS approaches to ground transportation?

options.A: Smart cities, embedded safety culture, and a through-life approach to service delivery
options.B: Privatization, deregulation, and competitive bidding
options.C: Just-in-time manufacturing, lean inventory, and outsourcing
options.D: Open-source software, agile sprints, and continuous deployment

correct_answer_text: Smart cities, embedded safety culture, and a through-life approach to service delivery

question: Which UK-based organization is cited in Section 4.4.10 as having made solid progress in moving toward an SE approach within the civil engineering domain?

options.A: Network Rail
options.B: The Institution of Civil Engineers
options.C: ProRail
options.D: The Royal Academy of Engineering

correct_answer_text: The Institution of Civil Engineers

question: Section 4.4.10 cites Intelligent Transportation System (ITS) concepts as evidence of what driver for additional SE mandates in transportation?

options.A: Declining ridership in legacy networks
options.B: Reduced regulatory oversight
options.C: Rapidly increasing system complexities in modern transportation systems
options.D: Falling fuel costs and vehicle electrification subsidies

correct_answer_text: Rapidly increasing system complexities in modern transportation systems

question: Which of the following is NOT listed in Section 4.4.10 as an example of the emerging complexities within modern ground transportation capability?

options.A: Train control automation and bus scheduling
options.B: Autonomous vehicles and micro-grid hybrid traction power
options.C: Passenger fare collection and trip planner smartphone apps
options.D: Deep-sea cargo container routing and customs clearance

correct_answer_text: Deep-sea cargo container routing and customs clearance

question: According to Section 4.4.10, ground transportation services are typically what type of system that must migrate to an updated version while still maintaining service?

options.A: Greenfield, clean-sheet systems
options.B: 'In-service' brownfield systems
options.C: Pure COTS-based systems
options.D: Single-use disposable systems

correct_answer_text: 'In-service' brownfield systems

question: Why does Section 4.4.10 emphasize that careful planning is required for some transit assets in large cities?

options.A: They are owned exclusively by private investors who demand quarterly returns
options.B: They run 24/7 service as a matter of public safety and demand
options.C: They are normally idle and seldom used outside peak hours
options.D: They use proprietary, single-vendor technology with no upgrade path

correct_answer_text: They run 24/7 service as a matter of public safety and demand

question: What organizational stance does Section 4.4.10 conclude is needed for success in the ground transportation domain?

options.A: A 'whole' organization approach
options.B: A single-discipline engineering approach
options.C: An exclusively outsourced vendor approach
options.D: A fully autonomous, unattended approach

correct_answer_text: A 'whole' organization approach

question: Per Section 4.4.10, the transportation domain uses the same unique terms and concepts as which other domain (referencing Table 4.7)?

options.A: Automotive
options.B: Defense
options.C: Infrastructure
options.D: Telecommunication

correct_answer_text: Infrastructure

question: Section 4.4.10 cites the Governance for Railway Investment Projects (GRIP) as an SE-related initiative established by which organization?

options.A: ProRail in the Netherlands
options.B: Network Rail in the UK
options.C: Deutsche Bahn in Germany
options.D: SNCF in France

correct_answer_text: Network Rail in the UK

question: According to Section 4.4.10, what common, open architectures are leading EU train operating companies and rolling stock manufacturers developing to support trackside and rolling stock acquisitions and upgrades?

options.A: GRIP and ProRail SE Handbook
options.B: EULYNX and the Reference Command and Control System Architecture
options.C: ARINC 429 and MIL-STD-1553
options.D: DO-178C and ARP4754A

correct_answer_text: EULYNX and the Reference Command and Control System Architecture

question: Section 4.4.10 identifies which Dutch network management institution as advocating SE as part of their project management approach for delivering rail infrastructure projects?

options.A: Rijkswaterstaat
options.B: ProRail
options.C: Nederlandse Spoorwegen
options.D: TNO

correct_answer_text: ProRail

question: Section 4.4.10 notes that the public funding of long-lived ground transportation assets carries what associated obligation that shapes SE practice in this domain?

options.A: A short-term profitability mandate
options.B: A related fiducial public trust
options.C: An obligation to avoid all change once in service
options.D: A requirement to use only domestic suppliers

correct_answer_text: A related fiducial public trust

section_title: Difference between Hard and Soft Skills
question: According to Section 5.1, how does the Handbook distinguish between the terms 'competence' and 'competency' as applied to SE practitioners?

options.A: Competence is the set of skills required to perform a job, while competency is the ability to perform an activity or task.
options.B: Competence is the ability to perform an activity or task, while competency is the set of skills required in the performance of a job.
options.C: Competence and competency are synonyms used interchangeably in the INCOSE SECF.
options.D: Competence applies only to soft skills, while competency applies only to hard skills.

correct_answer_text: Competence is the ability to perform an activity or task, while competency is the set of skills required in the performance of a job.

section_title: Difference between Hard and Soft Skills
question: Per Section 5.1, what is the relationship between an individual's competencies and their competence?

options.A: Competence is one specific subset within a competency.
options.B: Competencies and competence are measured on independent scales with no defined relationship.
options.C: The sum of an individual's competencies makes up their competence.
options.D: Competence and competency must be assessed only at the team level, not the individual level.

correct_answer_text: The sum of an individual's competencies makes up their competence.

section_title: Difference between Hard and Soft Skills
question: The INCOSE Systems Engineering Competency Framework (SECF) organizes competencies into how many themes, and what are they?

options.A: Three themes: technical, management, and professional.
options.B: Four themes: core, technical, management, and integrating.
options.C: Five themes: core, professional, technical, management, and integrating.
options.D: Six themes: core, professional, technical, management, integrating, and ethical.

correct_answer_text: Five themes: core, professional, technical, management, and integrating.

section_title: Difference between Hard and Soft Skills
question: Per the INCOSE SECF themes described in Section 5.1, which theme recognizes SE as an integrating discipline that considers activities from other specialists in project management, logistics, quality, and finance to create a coherent whole?

options.A: Core competencies
options.B: Professional competencies
options.C: Management competencies
options.D: Integrating competencies

correct_answer_text: Integrating competencies

section_title: Difference between Hard and Soft Skills
question: Per Section 5.1, what is the primary purpose of the INCOSE Systems Engineering Competency Assessment Guide (SECAG)?

options.A: To define new SE competencies that supersede the SECF.
options.B: To provide guidance on evaluating individuals for proficiency in competencies and differentiating between proficiency levels defined in the SECF.
options.C: To certify organizations as conforming to ISO/IEC/IEEE 15288.
options.D: To replace the INCOSE Handbook chapters on technical processes.

correct_answer_text: To provide guidance on evaluating individuals for proficiency in competencies and differentiating between proficiency levels defined in the SECF.

section_title: Difference between Hard and Soft Skills
question: Per Section 5.1, the INCOSE SECF and SECAG are described as intended for use in which set of activities?

options.A: Procurement, contracting, accreditation, and litigation support.
options.B: Software configuration management and version control.
options.C: Hiring, assessing, training, and advancing SE practitioners.
options.D: Marketing, sales, and customer relationship management.

correct_answer_text: Hiring, assessing, training, and advancing SE practitioners.

section_title: Difference between Hard and Soft Skills
question: Figure 5.1 in the Handbook portrays SE practitioners as 'T-shaped.' What does this metaphor describe?

options.A: A practitioner who only specializes vertically in one engineering discipline, without breadth.
options.B: A practitioner with depth of knowledge in a fundamental engineering discipline plus breadth of knowledge about systems and the multiple disciplines involved.
options.C: A practitioner whose career is divided equally between two unrelated industries.
options.D: A practitioner who reports to two managers in a matrix organization.

correct_answer_text: A practitioner with depth of knowledge in a fundamental engineering discipline plus breadth of knowledge about systems and the multiple disciplines involved.

section_title: Difference between Hard and Soft Skills
question: Why does the Handbook state that SE practitioners are often described as 'T-shaped' rather than expert in everything?

options.A: Because SE practitioners are restricted by certification rules to a single discipline.
options.B: Because, given the complexity and multidisciplinary nature of today's systems and systems of systems, it is not possible for a single person to know everything about a system of interest.
options.C: Because the SECF prohibits SE practitioners from acquiring breadth of knowledge outside their home discipline.
options.D: Because most organizations only hire SE practitioners as generalists with no technical depth.

correct_answer_text: Because, given the complexity and multidisciplinary nature of today's systems and systems of systems, it is not possible for a single person to know everything about a system of interest.

section_title: Difference between Hard and Soft Skills
question: Per Section 5.1.1, which of the following is the best example of a 'hard skill' for an SE practitioner?

options.A: Influencing a peer.
options.B: Motivating a team.
options.C: Requirements analysis, architectural evaluation, and risk management.
options.D: Resolving a conflict between two stakeholders.

correct_answer_text: Requirements analysis, architectural evaluation, and risk management.

section_title: Difference between Hard and Soft Skills
question: Per Section 5.1.1, which of the following is the best example of a 'soft skill' for an SE practitioner?

options.A: Performing a structural or hydrodynamic analysis.
options.B: Influencing a peer, motivating a team, and resolving a conflict.
options.C: Conducting a quantitative reliability analysis.
options.D: Executing requirements analysis using a model-based tool.

correct_answer_text: Influencing a peer, motivating a team, and resolving a conflict.

section_title: Difference between Hard and Soft Skills
question: Per Table 5.1, which set of characteristics is associated with hard skills (as contrasted with soft skills)?

options.A: Subjective definitions, difficult to measure, social application, high self-awareness required.
options.B: Concrete definitions, measurable, testable, individual application, low self-awareness required, personal affect has low impact.
options.C: Aspirational definitions, qualitative only, team-only application, high personal affect impact.
options.D: Ethical definitions, regulated, contractual application, no self-awareness required.

correct_answer_text: Concrete definitions, measurable, testable, individual application, low self-awareness required, personal affect has low impact.

section_title: Difference between Hard and Soft Skills
question: Per Table 5.1, which set of characteristics is associated with soft skills (as contrasted with hard skills)?

options.A: Concrete definitions, measurable, testable, individual application.
options.B: Subjective definitions, difficult to measure, difficult to test, social application, high self-awareness required, personal affect has high impact.
options.C: Mathematical definitions, quantitative modeling, individual application, low personal affect impact.
options.D: Engineering definitions, deterministic, testable, technology-level application.

correct_answer_text: Subjective definitions, difficult to measure, difficult to test, social application, high self-awareness required, personal affect has high impact.

section_title: Difference between Hard and Soft Skills
question: Per Section 5.1.1, what kinds of problems do hard skills versus soft skills typically address for SE practitioners?

options.A: Hard skills address people-related problems; soft skills address engineering-related problems.
options.B: Hard skills require science, mathematics, and quantitative modeling to solve engineering-related problems; soft skills require emotional intelligence and appropriate behaviors to solve people-related problems.
options.C: Both hard and soft skills address only engineering-related problems.
options.D: Both hard and soft skills address only stakeholder-related problems.

correct_answer_text: Hard skills require science, mathematics, and quantitative modeling to solve engineering-related problems; soft skills require emotional intelligence and appropriate behaviors to solve people-related problems.

section_title: Difference between Hard and Soft Skills
question: Per Section 5.1.1, which statement best characterizes the role of soft skills (also known as interpersonal, intrapersonal, people, or professional skills) in SE practice?

options.A: Soft skills are optional add-ons that rarely affect engineering outcomes.
options.B: Soft skills are relevant only at executive levels and have no application to working SE practitioners.
options.C: Soft skills are related to aspects such as teamwork, collaboration, and facilitation, and they often make the difference between a smooth application of SE and one riddled with challenges.
options.D: Soft skills are quantitative analyses similar to hard skills but applied to organizational metrics.

correct_answer_text: Soft skills are related to aspects such as teamwork, collaboration, and facilitation, and they often make the difference between a smooth application of SE and one riddled with challenges.

section_title: Difference between Hard and Soft Skills
question: From Table 5.1, drag each attribute to the skill type it characterizes.
Per Section 5.1.2, how is SE professionalism best summarized?

options.A: Mastery of modeling tools and quantitative analysis methods used during system design
options.B: A personal commitment to professional standards of behavior, ethics, obligations to society, the profession, and the environment
options.C: Holding a recognized engineering license and adhering to organizational quality procedures
options.D: The ability to manage cost, schedule, and scope across the entire system life cycle

correct_answer_text: A personal commitment to professional standards of behavior, ethics, obligations to society, the profession, and the environment

question: According to Section 5.1.2, why are SE practitioners trusted to reach unbiased, informed, and potentially significant decisions?

options.A: Because they hold positional authority over project teams and stakeholders
options.B: Because they apply standardized organizational procedures uniformly across all projects
options.C: Because of their specialized knowledge, skills, abilities, and behaviors used to apply reasoning, judgment, and problem solving
options.D: Because they exclusively rely on quantitative engineering analyses to remove subjectivity

correct_answer_text: Because of their specialized knowledge, skills, abilities, and behaviors used to apply reasoning, judgment, and problem solving

question: Section 5.1.2 states that SE professionalism includes consideration of personal behaviors beyond which of the following?

options.A: Using methods and tools
options.B: Communicating with stakeholders
options.C: Mentoring junior engineers
options.D: Documenting design decisions

correct_answer_text: Using methods and tools

question: Which of the following is NOT listed in Section 5.1.2 as an example of a behavior or outcome related to Professional Competencies that SE practitioners recognize the benefits of?

options.A: Communications and negotiation
options.B: Team dynamics and facilitation
options.C: Coaching and mentoring
options.D: Requirements analysis and architectural evaluation

correct_answer_text: Requirements analysis and architectural evaluation

question: Per Section 5.1.2, in which INCOSE resource are the Professional Competencies documented?

options.A: The INCOSE Systems Engineering Vision 2035
options.B: The INCOSE Systems Engineering Competency Framework (SECF)
options.C: The INCOSE Code of Ethics
options.D: The INCOSE Systems Engineering Body of Knowledge (SEBoK)

correct_answer_text: The INCOSE Systems Engineering Competency Framework (SECF)

question: According to Section 5.1.2, evaluation of an individual's Professional Competencies can be accomplished using which INCOSE resource?

options.A: The INCOSE Systems Engineering Competency Framework (SECF)
options.B: The INCOSE Systems Engineering Handbook (SEH)
options.C: The INCOSE Systems Engineering Competency Assessment Guide (SECAG)
options.D: The INCOSE Code of Ethics

correct_answer_text: The INCOSE Systems Engineering Competency Assessment Guide (SECAG)

question: Per Section 5.1.2, to whom or to what does SE professionalism include obligations?

options.A: The employer, the customer, and the supply chain
options.B: Society, the profession, and the environment
options.C: The project team, the program, and the enterprise
options.D: Regulators, certifying bodies, and standards organizations

correct_answer_text: Society, the profession, and the environment

question: Section 5.1.2 lists "ethics, professionalism, and technical leadership" alongside which of the following groupings as benefits of Professional Competencies?

options.A: Requirements engineering, verification, and validation
options.B: Risk management, configuration management, and quality assurance
options.C: Communications, negotiation, team dynamics, facilitation, emotional intelligence, coaching, and mentoring
options.D: Cost estimation, scheduling, and earned value management

correct_answer_text: Communications, negotiation, team dynamics, facilitation, emotional intelligence, coaching, and mentoring

question: According to Section 5.1.3, how is leadership generally defined?

options.A: The exercise of positional authority to direct subordinates toward organizational objectives
options.B: The act (or art!) of enabling people to produce results or achieve outcomes they would not have on their own
options.C: The application of technical expertise to solve complex engineering problems
options.D: The management of resources, schedules, and budgets to deliver projects on time

correct_answer_text: The act (or art!) of enabling people to produce results or achieve outcomes they would not have on their own

question: As illustrated in Figure 5.2, Technical Leadership exists at the intersection of which two elements?

options.A: Technical Expertise and Leadership Skills
options.B: Hard Skills and Soft Skills
options.C: Management Competencies and Core Competencies
options.D: Professional Competencies and Integrating Competencies

correct_answer_text: Technical Expertise and Leadership Skills

question: Why must SE practitioners lead through influence rather than positional authority?

options.A: Because SE practitioners are forbidden from holding management positions
options.B: Because INCOSE ethics prohibit the use of formal authority in technical decisions
options.C: Because they seldom have the positional authority to ensure outcomes and must leverage technical knowledge and personal qualities
options.D: Because positional authority is reserved for project managers under all circumstances

correct_answer_text: Because they seldom have the positional authority to ensure outcomes and must leverage technical knowledge and personal qualities

question: According to Section 5.1.3, what must a good technical leader possess in addition to expertise in one or more technical areas?

options.A: Certification in every engineering discipline relevant to the system
options.B: Some level of understanding across a wide range of disciplines, plus awareness of the limits of their own knowledge
options.C: Direct managerial control over all technical contributors on the project
options.D: Exclusive focus on a single deep technical specialty to maintain credibility

correct_answer_text: Some level of understanding across a wide range of disciplines, plus awareness of the limits of their own knowledge

question: Which organization developed the technical leadership model presented in Table 5.2 that identifies six interrelated behaviors?

options.A: INCOSE Professional and Soft Skills Working Group
options.B: INCOSE Systems Engineering Competency Framework Working Group
options.C: INCOSE Technical Leadership Institute (TLI)
options.D: INCOSE Code of Ethics Committee

correct_answer_text: INCOSE Technical Leadership Institute (TLI)

question: In the Table 5.2 technical leadership model, which behavior is described as ensuring an aspirational statement that defines who we are and where we want to go, providing an impelling purpose?

options.A: Holding the Vision
options.B: Thinking Strategically
options.C: Fostering Collaboration
options.D: Communicating Effectively

correct_answer_text: Holding the Vision

question: In the Table 5.2 technical leadership model, which behavior involves formulating a hypothesis before acting, treating the action as an experiment, and continuing the path or formulating a new hypothesis based on observed results?

options.A: Demonstrating Emotional Intelligence
options.B: Thinking Strategically
options.C: Enabling Others to Succeed
options.D: Holding the Vision

correct_answer_text: Thinking Strategically

question: In Table 5.2, which technical leadership behavior is described as allowing new ideas to emerge through creative conflict and experimentation, recognizing that complex problems cannot be solved by individuals working alone?

options.A: Holding the Vision
options.B: Communicating Effectively
options.C: Fostering Collaboration
options.D: Enabling Others to Succeed

correct_answer_text: Fostering Collaboration

question: According to Table 5.2, what does "Communicating Effectively" emphasize beyond just speaking?

options.A: Producing comprehensive written technical documentation for all stakeholders
options.B: Actively listening with attention to both the content being spoken and the emotion with which it is communicated
options.C: Delivering presentations with polished slides and engaging visuals
options.D: Translating technical jargon into language understandable by non-engineers

correct_answer_text: Actively listening with attention to both the content being spoken and the emotion with which it is communicated

question: According to Table 5.2, what is the role of the technical leader under the "Enabling Others to Succeed" behavior?

options.A: To personally perform the most critical tasks to guarantee success
options.B: To assign tasks rigorously and monitor compliance with the schedule
options.C: To influence, guide, encourage, and support those who can produce desired outcomes
options.D: To replace underperforming team members with proven specialists

correct_answer_text: To influence, guide, encourage, and support those who can produce desired outcomes

question: In Table 5.2, the "Demonstrating Emotional Intelligence" behavior states that to lead others, technical leaders must continually do which of the following?

options.A: Seek feedback to decrease their blind spots and be willing to reveal things that help others know them better
options.B: Maintain emotional distance from team members to preserve objectivity
options.C: Suppress personal feelings to remain wholly objective in decision-making
options.D: Rely on personality assessments to identify team strengths and weaknesses

correct_answer_text: Seek feedback to decrease their blind spots and be willing to reveal things that help others know them better

question: According to Table 5.2, which reflective question is associated with the "Fostering Collaboration" behavior?

options.A: What outcome are we striving to achieve and how am I advancing that vision?
options.B: What patterns are emerging from my actions, and what are the implications for what I should do next?
options.C: What relationships am I building today for myself and for others?
options.D: What obstacles are preventing others from acting, and how can I help remove those obstacles?

correct_answer_text: What relationships am I building today for myself and for others?

question: According to the INCOSE Code of Ethics (2023) as cited in Section 5.1.4, the practice of SE can result in significant social and environmental benefits, but only if which of the following occurs?

options.A: The project is delivered on schedule and within the approved budget baseline
options.B: Unintended and undesired effects are considered and mitigated
options.C: All requirements are formally verified by an independent test agency
options.D: The SE practitioner holds a recognized professional certification

correct_answer_text: Unintended and undesired effects are considered and mitigated

question: Section 5.1.4 identifies a pervasive pressure on SE practitioners that makes ethical practice especially important. Which pressure is named?

options.A: Pressure to adopt model-based SE before the workforce is ready
options.B: Pressure to cut corners to deliver projects faster or at lower costs
options.C: Pressure to over-document low-risk design decisions
options.D: Pressure to standardize on a single life cycle process model

correct_answer_text: Pressure to cut corners to deliver projects faster or at lower costs

question: Per Section 5.1.4, part of the role of the SE practitioner as a leader and professional includes knowing when unacceptable risks or trade-offs are being made, knowing how to influence key stakeholders, and which additional attribute?

options.A: Deferring final judgment to the project manager's risk acceptance authority
options.B: Maintaining strict confidentiality of internal trade-off decisions from outside parties
options.C: Having the courage to stand up for stakeholders, the community, and the profession when necessary
options.D: Escalating only those issues that have a quantified safety-of-life impact

correct_answer_text: Having the courage to stand up for stakeholders, the community, and the profession when necessary

question: Section 5.1.4 states that the INCOSE Code of Ethics contains which three sections to help the SE practitioner apply ethics practically?

options.A: "Personal Conduct," "Employer Loyalty," and "Continuing Education"
options.B: "Fundamental Principles," "Fundamental Duties to Society and Public Infrastructure," and "Rules of Practice"
options.C: "Safety," "Sustainability," and "Stakeholder Satisfaction"
options.D: "Core Values," "Professional Obligations," and "Disciplinary Procedures"

correct_answer_text: "Fundamental Principles," "Fundamental Duties to Society and Public Infrastructure," and "Rules of Practice"

question: A senior systems engineer on a medical radiation therapy program (analogous to the Therac-25 case) discovers that internally reported overdose incidents have not been disclosed to the regulator or to clinical users, while management pressures the team to ship the next release on schedule. Based on the ethics guidance in Section 5.1.4, what is the most defensible course of action?

options.A: Defer to management's schedule decision, since formal regulatory reporting is outside the SE practitioner's lane
options.B: Document personal disagreement in a private engineering notebook and continue with the release
options.C: Recognize that unacceptable risks are being made and exercise the courage to stand up for stakeholders, the community, and the profession by escalating and ensuring the safety issue is reported
options.D: Wait until an independent verification activity in a later life cycle stage detects the issue

correct_answer_text: Recognize that unacceptable risks are being made and exercise the courage to stand up for stakeholders, the community, and the profession by escalating and ensuring the safety issue is reported

question: Section 5.1.4 frames ethics as especially important for SE because of which characteristic of the profession?

options.A: SE is performed primarily by independent contractors with limited employer oversight
options.B: SE practitioners normally hold positional authority to enforce technical decisions
options.C: There will always be pressure to cut corners to deliver projects faster or at lower costs, especially for a profession such as SE
options.D: SE work products are not subject to external audit or independent review

correct_answer_text: There will always be pressure to cut corners to deliver projects faster or at lower costs, especially for a profession such as SE

question: Per Section 5.1.4, which capability is specifically called out as part of the SE practitioner's role as a leader and professional?

options.A: Knowing how to influence key stakeholders when unacceptable risks or trade-offs are being made
options.B: Independently approving safety waivers without management review
options.C: Selecting the verification methods used by the test organization
options.D: Negotiating the contractual terms of supplier agreements

correct_answer_text: Knowing how to influence key stakeholders when unacceptable risks or trade-offs are being made

question: A systems engineer is offered a paid advisory role by a supplier whose product is one of three candidates in a current trade study they are leading. Applying the ethics framing of Section 5.1.4 and the INCOSE Code of Ethics, which response best reflects the practitioner's duties to society, the profession, and stakeholders?

options.A: Accept the role privately, since their personal judgment will remain objective
options.B: Accept the role only if the compensation is below a self-determined threshold
options.C: Disclose the conflict of interest and recuse from or otherwise mitigate involvement in the trade study to protect stakeholders and the profession
options.D: Decline only if the supplier wins the trade study, to avoid the appearance of bias

correct_answer_text: Disclose the conflict of interest and recuse from or otherwise mitigate involvement in the trade study to protect stakeholders and the profession

question: Section 5.1.4 of the SE Handbook explicitly cites which document as the basis for SE ethical practice?

options.A: The ABET Code of Professional Conduct
options.B: The ISO/IEC/IEEE 15288 Ethics Annex
options.C: The PMI Code of Ethics and Professional Conduct
options.D: The INCOSE Code of Ethics (2023)

correct_answer_text: The INCOSE Code of Ethics (2023)

question: Per the ABET definitions cited in Section 5.2, which term is defined as 'the range of human differences, encompassing the characteristics that make one individual or group different from another'?

options.A: Inclusion
options.B: Equity
options.C: Diversity
options.D: Equality

correct_answer_text: Diversity

question: According to the ABET definitions referenced in Section 5.2, equity is best described as:

options.A: Providing every user exactly the same resources regardless of circumstance
options.B: Fair treatment, access, opportunity, and advancement for all people, achieved by intentional focus on their disparate needs, conditions, and abilities
options.C: The intentional, proactive, and continuing efforts in which all members respect, support, and value others
options.D: The range of human differences that make individuals or groups different from one another

correct_answer_text: Fair treatment, access, opportunity, and advancement for all people, achieved by intentional focus on their disparate needs, conditions, and abilities

question: How does Section 5.2 define inclusion?

options.A: Recruiting team members from underrepresented demographic categories until quotas are met
options.B: Ensuring every stakeholder receives identical resources and identical treatment
options.C: The intentional, proactive, and continuing efforts and practices in which all members respect, support, and value others
options.D: The accommodation of differences within the stakeholder community by adapting the realized system

correct_answer_text: The intentional, proactive, and continuing efforts and practices in which all members respect, support, and value others

question: In Figure 5.3 'Categorized dimensions of diversity,' the representative characteristics of diversity are grouped into which five areas?

options.A: Intrinsic, employment, environment, interaction, and family
options.B: Race, gender, age, religion, and nationality
options.C: Cognitive, social, emotional, cultural, and physical
options.D: Personal, professional, organizational, societal, and global

correct_answer_text: Intrinsic, employment, environment, interaction, and family

question: Section 5.2 states that DEI is vital to successful Systems Engineering primarily because of:

options.A: Regulatory requirements imposed by ABET and ISO standards on engineering teams
options.B: The need to satisfy human resources reporting obligations for engineering organizations
options.C: The wide range of contexts in which SE is applied and the consideration of multiple stakeholder viewpoints at the heart of the systems approach
options.D: The fact that SE practitioners must demonstrate DEI competencies to obtain INCOSE certification

correct_answer_text: The wide range of contexts in which SE is applied and the consideration of multiple stakeholder viewpoints at the heart of the systems approach

question: According to Section 5.2, SE practitioners play a pivotal role in integrating DEI concepts through which two activities?

options.A: Conducting bias training for executives and publishing annual diversity statistics
options.B: Ensuring the SE team is inclusive and welcomes diverse talent, and ensuring the systems realized are as accommodating as possible of differences within the stakeholder community
options.C: Recruiting from underrepresented groups and providing mentorship programs to junior staff
options.D: Enforcing equal pay policies and standardizing identical work assignments across team members

correct_answer_text: Ensuring the SE team is inclusive and welcomes diverse talent, and ensuring the systems realized are as accommodating as possible of differences within the stakeholder community

question: Section 5.2 indicates that failure to address DEI within the SE team or the realized system can result in which of the following?

options.A: Immediate disqualification of the project from INCOSE certification
options.B: Increased project costs due solely to additional compliance reporting
options.C: Suboptimal outcomes such as missed solutions, lower productivity, or delivery of a system that does not equitably meet the needs of the full stakeholder community
options.D: Loss of intellectual property rights for the developing organization

correct_answer_text: Suboptimal outcomes such as missed solutions, lower productivity, or delivery of a system that does not equitably meet the needs of the full stakeholder community

question: Section 5.2 distinguishes equality from equity. Which statement best captures the distinction the handbook draws?

options.A: Equality and equity are interchangeable terms for fair distribution of resources among system users
options.B: Equality means providing each user exactly the same resources but may not result in similar outcomes, while equity proactively addresses disparities between users so each can experience a similar outcome
options.C: Equality requires changing the system for each user, whereas equity requires identical treatment of all users
options.D: Equality applies to the SE team composition while equity applies only to the delivered system

correct_answer_text: Equality means providing each user exactly the same resources but may not result in similar outcomes, while equity proactively addresses disparities between users so each can experience a similar outcome

question: Per Section 5.2, what specific deliberate actions may an SE team need to take to ensure its leadership promotes cognitive diversity and diversity of ideas?

options.A: Mandating identical workloads and compensation across all team members
options.B: Restricting team membership to practitioners with similar educational backgrounds
options.C: Providing psychological safety and communication equity
options.D: Eliminating all hierarchy from the SE team structure

correct_answer_text: Providing psychological safety and communication equity

question: From Figure 5.3 (Categorized dimensions of diversity), drag each diversity attribute to its dimension category.
Per the introduction to Section 5.3, how do SE practitioners typically operate with respect to other engineering disciplines?

options.A: They work in isolation from other disciplines and integrate only at the end of the life cycle
options.B: They routinely work within broad multidisciplinary teams, interacting with key related disciplines
options.C: They supervise other disciplines but do not engage with their practitioners directly
options.D: They replace specialist disciplines with a single generalist SE role

correct_answer_text: They routinely work within broad multidisciplinary teams, interacting with key related disciplines

question: According to ISO/IEC/IEEE 42010 (2022), as cited in Section 5.3.1, a software-intensive system is best defined as:

options.A: Any system whose components are implemented exclusively as software
options.B: Any system where software contributes essential influences to the design, construction, deployment, and evolution of the system as a whole
options.C: Any system that includes embedded firmware on a microcontroller
options.D: Any system that supports software-based user interfaces

correct_answer_text: Any system where software contributes essential influences to the design, construction, deployment, and evolution of the system as a whole

question: Section 5.3.1 notes that software-intensive systems are also known by which other terms?

options.A: Hardware-centric and electromechanical systems
options.B: Software-enabled and cyber-physical systems
options.C: Information-only and data-centric systems
options.D: Closed-loop and analog systems

correct_answer_text: Software-enabled and cyber-physical systems

question: Per ISO/IEC TR 19759 (2015), as cited in Section 5.3.1, software engineering (SWE) is defined as:

options.A: The art of writing computer programs using high-level languages
options.B: The management of software projects from requirements through retirement
options.C: The application of a systematic, disciplined, quantifiable approach to the development, operation, and maintenance of software; that is, the application of engineering to software
options.D: The discipline of selecting commercial off-the-shelf (COTS) software for system integration

correct_answer_text: The application of a systematic, disciplined, quantifiable approach to the development, operation, and maintenance of software; that is, the application of engineering to software

question: Section 5.3.1 explains why software elements can typically be tailored, adapted, and modified more readily than physical elements, especially in the support stage. The primary reason given is that software elements are:

options.A: Less expensive than physical elements
options.B: Malleable
options.C: Tested exhaustively before deployment
options.D: Developed by larger teams than hardware

correct_answer_text: Malleable

question: According to Section 5.3.1, which system attributes does software often provide or facilitate?

options.A: Mass, inertia, and thermal dissipation only
options.B: Material strength, durability, and corrosion resistance
options.C: Functionality, behavior, quality characteristics (QCs), and system interfaces (both internal and external)
options.D: Logistics support and disposal planning only

correct_answer_text: Functionality, behavior, quality characteristics (QCs), and system interfaces (both internal and external)

question: Section 5.3.1 describes software elements as logical entities composed of textual and iconic symbols. Which statement best captures the assurance challenge this creates for SE practitioners?

options.A: Software elements are immune to single-symbol errors because compilers automatically detect them
options.B: A single mistyped symbol or logical mistake can cause failure of the entire system, and exhaustive testing of all logical branches and iteration loops is generally infeasible in reasonable time
options.C: Software quality can always be guaranteed by code reviews alone, with no need for testing
options.D: Software defects are easy to detect during development because software has few branches compared to hardware

correct_answer_text: A single mistyped symbol or logical mistake can cause failure of the entire system, and exhaustive testing of all logical branches and iteration loops is generally infeasible in reasonable time

question: Section 5.3.1 cautions that detailed concentration on the precise development of software elements and software interfaces may cause SWE practitioners to:

options.A: Improve overall system-level traceability automatically
options.B: Eliminate the need for SE involvement in software-intensive systems
options.C: Lose sight of the impacts of their software on larger system issues
options.D: Become more aligned with hardware engineering practices

correct_answer_text: Lose sight of the impacts of their software on larger system issues

question: Per Section 5.3.1, which Handbook section is cross-referenced for additional discussion of software-intensive systems?

options.A: Section 2.3.4 (Technical Management Processes)
options.B: Section 4.3.4
options.C: Section 5.1 (SE Competencies)
options.D: Section 1.3.5 (Make or Buy)

correct_answer_text: Section 4.3.4

question: Section 5.3.1 states that software embedded in software-intensive systems can also play which additional role?

options.A: Replace the need for system verification and validation
options.B: Monitor system performance and provide observable performance indicators
options.C: Eliminate the need for hardware interfaces
options.D: Set hardware budgets such as size, weight, and power

correct_answer_text: Monitor system performance and provide observable performance indicators

question: Per Section 5.3.1, what is stated about replacing software elements within a system?

options.A: Software elements cannot be replaced once delivered
options.B: Software elements, like physical elements, can be improved and replaced if the interfaces are preserved
options.C: Software elements must always be replaced together with their hardware
options.D: Software elements are replaced only during disposal and retirement

correct_answer_text: Software elements, like physical elements, can be improved and replaced if the interfaces are preserved

question: Section 5.3.1 emphasizes that software has become ubiquitous in modern systems. Why is this relevant to SE practitioners working on older systems?

options.A: Older systems do not contain software and can be ignored
options.B: Software is important in updates to many older systems
options.C: Older systems must be entirely replaced rather than updated
options.D: Software in older systems is exempt from SE practices

correct_answer_text: Software is important in updates to many older systems

question: According to the SE Handbook, what disciplines does Hardware Engineering (HWE) explicitly include?

options.A: Software engineering (SWE) and firmware engineering
options.B: Mechanical engineering (ME) for mechanical elements and electrical engineering (EE) for electrical and electronic elements
options.C: Industrial engineering (IE) and manufacturing engineering
